sections.title: Why You Need Game Theory
lines: Introduction, part one. Why you need game theory.
There is a stubborn myth floating around poker circles that game theory is an advanced topic reserved only for elite players grinding the toughest games in the world.

Andrew Brokos wants to dispel that myth right from the start, because the truth is almost the exact opposite: game theory is for anyone who has ever sat at a poker table and felt genuinely unsure about what the right play was.
If you have never experienced that uncertainty, congratulations — you are apparently the one player in the world who has figured everything out, and the rest of us would love to read your book.
For everyone else, game theory is exactly what you need.
So what is game theory, technically speaking?

The Oxford Dictionaries definition describes it as the branch of mathematics concerned with analyzing strategies in competitive situations where the outcome of what one participant chooses to do depends critically on what other participants choose to do.
Put plainly, game theory gives us a way to build strategies that will perform reasonably well no matter what our opponents decide to do.
For a long time, only a small number of mathematically sophisticated poker players engaged deeply with game theory.

It helped those players grasp certain broad strategic principles, but the sheer complexity of poker meant there was still a great deal of guesswork when it came down to the specifics of any given hand.
What changed everything was the arrival of dedicated solver software and increasingly affordable computing power.
Today, any player willing to do the work can apply game theoretic principles to poker with a degree of rigor and precision that simply was not possible a generation ago.

And even if you have never personally opened a solver, if you have read a poker book or watched an instructional video produced in recent years, you have already been touched by the insights that this software has unlocked.
Game theory is not a cure-all, and Brokos is careful to say so.
It is not the only way to think about poker, but it has become an increasingly indispensable lens.
If you do not have at least a foundational understanding of the game theory that underlies poker, then in an important sense you do not really understand the game.
What you may have instead are strategies that work against certain types of opponents in certain situations you encounter frequently — strategies that were built through trial and error.
That kind of experience-based knowledge can take you a long way, but it has real limits.
You probably feel comfortable in familiar spots and start to struggle when something less common comes up.
And as more of your opponents develop their own game theory knowledge, they will increasingly be able to put you in precisely those difficult, unfamiliar spots.
Here is something important to understand about game theory solver software: it generates strategies of extreme complexity, often mixing many different actions with carefully calibrated frequencies.
But you do not need to memorize those details or reproduce them exactly at the table in order to benefit from studying them.
Unless you are competing at the absolute highest levels of the game, the right goal when studying game theory is not to memorize the precise solution to every specific situation.
Instead, your goal should be to extract broad, transferable principles that can guide you across a wide variety of situations whenever you are unsure what the best play is.
That is exactly what this book is designed to help you do.
Starting from simple, carefully constructed scenarios, it builds toward the most important theoretical concepts that underlie poker strategy.
At each step, you gain new tools you can use immediately and new analytical skills that will help you investigate more complex situations on your own.
You will learn how to ask the right questions, draw the right conclusions from what you observe, and apply these ideas in real time while you are actually playing.
The focus throughout is on trends and principles rather than frequencies and memorized lines.
Along the way, the book may challenge some things you think you already know about poker strategy.

In other cases, it will simply illuminate the theory behind things you already do well, helping you understand not just that something works but why it works and under what conditions it might stop working.
Ultimately, developing a solid grounding in game theory should make you more comfortable and more confident at the poker table.
You will be better equipped to recognize and exploit the mistakes of less-skilled opponents, and you will be able to hold your own against those who are more skilled.
Most importantly, you will feel prepared to handle whatever situation the game throws at you, even situations you have never seen before.

sections.title: The Game Theory Approach
lines: Introduction, part two. The game theory approach.
Most of what poker players know about strategy was learned through an exploitative framework — and most players who use this framework do not even realize that is what they are doing.
Exploitative play means that your strategy depends on assumptions about how your opponents will behave.
Those assumptions might be conscious or unconscious, but they are always there, quietly driving your decisions.
Take one of the most widely known concepts in poker: the continuation bet.
The conventional wisdom is that if you raised before the flop, you should generally make a bet on the flop even if that card did nothing for your hand.
But this is not some iron law of poker handed down from on high.

The profitability of that continuation bet depends entirely on specific assumptions about your opponents — that they tend to call too loosely before the flop, that they give up too easily after the flop, and that they rarely raise as a bluff.
In many games, especially at lower stakes, those are perfectly safe assumptions, and the continuation bet works beautifully.
The danger of exploitative play is the other side of that coin.

Any time you adjust your own strategy to take advantage of a mistake you expect your opponent to make, you are simultaneously opening yourself up to exploitation if that assumption turns out to be wrong.
Your opponents may be actively studying you, trying to figure out your tendencies, and then deliberately doing the opposite of what you expect.
A simple example makes this concrete.
Suppose you decide to bluff at a very high frequency because you believe your opponent folds too often.
If you are right, that is an excellent strategy — you profit handsomely every time he incorrectly releases a hand.
But if you are wrong, and your opponent actually calls too much, then you are the one making an expensive mistake, not him.
Game theory offers a fundamentally different framework for approaching these decisions.
Rather than assuming your opponent will make a particular mistake, the game theory framework assumes that whenever there is a mathematically correct play available to your opponent, he will make it.
Your goal, then, is not to bet on a specific mistake your opponent will make but to create situations where no clearly correct play exists for him.
You want to put him in a position where calling is not obviously right and folding is not obviously right either.
If he calls when you bet and you have a strong hand, he loses.
If he calls when you bet and you are bluffing, you lose.
But if you have constructed your betting range properly — with the right mixture of strong hands and bluffs — then neither calling nor folding will be consistently profitable for him.
He will be stuck, unable to take advantage of you no matter what he chooses.
At this point in the explanation, Brokos anticipates a natural objection.

You are probably thinking about a specific opponent right now — maybe someone who always calls, or someone who always folds — and wondering why you would ever need game theory when you can just exploit that person directly.
And that is a completely valid point.
Game theory is a tool, not a mandate.
If you genuinely believe you know how a specific opponent will behave in a specific situation, by all means build your strategy around that belief.
You do not need game theory in every hand.
But the reality is that you probably do not know how every opponent will play in every situation.
When you run out of reliable reads, or when you are up against a strong opponent whose strategy is hard to pin down, that is when game theory becomes invaluable.
One important clarification about what the game theory framework actually assumes: it does not assume your opponent knows what cards you hold.
What it does assume is that your opponent knows your strategy — meaning he knows the full range of hands you might have in any given situation.
Consider a river all-in bet.
Your opponent does not know that you specifically have a flush, but if you only ever go all-in on the river when you have a flush, he knows that too.
And the counter-strategy is trivially simple: fold everything that cannot beat a flush, call with everything that can.
To prevent your opponent from exploiting you this easily, you need to balance that all-in bet by including some bluffs in your range.
When you sometimes go all-in as a bluff on that river, suddenly your opponent faces a genuine dilemma — calling might be wrong, and folding might be wrong.
He cannot be sure.
This principle of balance applies far beyond river bets.

When you raise before the flop, ideally your range should include some hands that are happy to face a re-raise, some hands that will reluctantly call a re-raise, and some hands that will fold to a re-raise.
If you get that mixture right, your opponents can never reliably predict how you will respond to pressure, which makes the decision of whether to re-raise you a genuinely difficult one for them.
A common misunderstanding is that embracing game theory means throwing all your reads out the window and pretending your opponents always play perfectly.
That is not what game theory asks of you.
Game theory does not require anything of you — it is simply a toolkit, and you get to decide when to reach for it.
When you have a confident read on an opponent and a clear exploitative line to take, take it.

When you are uncomfortable making that kind of assumption — either because your opponent is skilled and unpredictable, or simply because you are in unfamiliar territory — that is when you open the game theory toolbox.
The game theory approach can also open your eyes to exploitation opportunities you would never have spotted otherwise.
There are many situations in poker where it is not at all obvious which hands would make good bluffs, and as a result, most players simply do not bluff in those spots.
But here is the beautiful irony: precisely because bluffing is so rare in those situations, many opponents fold far too often when they do encounter a bet there.

If you can identify these non-intuitive bluffing spots and figure out which hands are the best candidates, you can extract a lot of value from opponents who are over-folding without even realizing it.
Game theory is what helps you find those spots.

The approach in this book is to begin with equilibrium solutions — that is, the strategies where neither player can improve their results by changing what they are doing, assuming the other player plays optimally.

From those equilibrium solutions, you will then learn how to derive exploitative strategies, identifying the specific kinds of mistakes an opponent might make and the best ways to profit from each one.

By the end, you should feel genuinely comfortable thinking in both modes — equilibrium thinking and exploitative thinking — and confident in your ability to decide which mode fits any given situation you face at the table.

sections.title: How to Use This Book
lines: Introduction, part three. How to use this book.
Play Optimal Poker is built around a series of scenarios, each one designed to illuminate specific concepts in a focused and digestible way.
The book begins not with full poker hands but with what are called toy games — simplified games that share some structural features with poker but strip away most of the complexity.
Toy games are easy enough to solve completely, which means you can study them without getting lost in the competing priorities that arise in real poker situations.
Each scenario in the book adds a new layer of complexity on top of what came before, so that you can see how all the individual pieces fit together as you progress.
Eventually, the book works its way up from these abstract toy games to applying the lessons directly to real poker situations you will actually encounter.
The way each scenario is structured is important.
Before presenting the solution, the book prompts you to make your own predictions about what the optimal strategies should be.
Some of these prediction questions will be straightforward, and others will be genuinely challenging.

Brokos recommends not banging your head against the wall trying to arrive at a perfectly precise answer before reading on, but he does strongly encourage you to spend some real time thinking independently before you look at the explanation.

The reason is simple: you learn and retain information much more effectively when you first engage with a problem yourself, even if you get it wrong, than when you simply read a solution handed to you.
The scenarios build on each other in a cumulative way, which means it matters that you genuinely understand the central concepts from each chapter before moving on to the next.
To help with this, each scenario includes overview sections and challenge questions that highlight the main takeaways and test whether you have actually absorbed them.
A good rule of thumb is to review those questions before you move on and make sure you can answer each one comfortably.
If you cannot, that is a signal to revisit the material rather than push forward.
Brokos also acknowledges that some readers may already have a solid background in game theory.
If that describes you, the early scenarios might feel very familiar.

The prediction questions at the start of each section are an opportunity to test how well you really know the material — and Brokos notes that even people with meaningful game theory knowledge sometimes get these details wrong.
He admits that in writing the book, he discovered gaps in his own understanding of concepts he thought he had fully mastered.
So if the prediction questions are easy and you get them right, feel free to skim or skip the rest of that section and move ahead.
But do check yourself honestly first.
After working through the equilibrium solution for each scenario, the book shifts to the exploitative angle.
This means thinking about the kinds of mistakes a player might realistically make in that situation and then figuring out how to best take advantage of each type of error.

Understanding the equilibrium is a valuable first step even when your actual goal is to play exploitatively, because knowing what the optimal baseline looks like makes it much easier to spot when an opponent is deviating from it.
The scenarios throughout the book feature two recurring players named Ivan and Opal.
Ivan is always the player who is in position — meaning he acts after his opponent on the betting rounds after the flop.
Opal is always out of position — she acts first.
In much of the academic and technical game theory literature, these roles are simply labeled I P and O P.
Brokos chose human names starting with those same letters because he believes they make the scenarios easier to visualize and follow.
Most of the scenarios involve just these two players, and that is intentional.
The lessons are most directly applicable to heads-up pots — just two players contesting the same hand.
In practice, even at a table with nine or ten players, many hands come down to exactly two players by the time the river is dealt, so the material is highly relevant to real games.

The general principles that emerge from two-player game theory analysis also mostly carry over to multi-way pots, even though the precise mathematics may be somewhat different when three or more players are involved.
On the subject of mathematics: yes, this book does involve some math.
But Brokos urges you not to get intimidated or bogged down by the numbers.
The exact numerical details are rarely the point.

What matters is understanding the concepts at a deeper level — grasping the relationship between how large a bet is and how often you need to bluff, for instance, or understanding why certain hands make better bluffing candidates than others.
Focus on the underlying logic, and let the specific numbers serve as illustrations rather than things to memorize.
Finally, Brokos is clear that these scenarios are meant to be engaging and useful, not purely theoretical exercises.
He has taken care to connect each abstract scenario back to the kinds of concrete decisions you actually face at a poker table.
At the end of the day, the instruction is the same as it is for game theory itself: take what is useful to you and leave behind what is not.
This book is another tool in your toolbox — a powerful one, built to make you a sharper, more confident, and more adaptable poker player.

sections.title: Overview & Objectives
lines: Chapter one, section one. Overview and Objectives.
Welcome to the first chapter of our companion to Play Optimal Poker by Andrew Brokos.
Before we ever look at a poker hand, we need to build our understanding of one of the most important ideas in all of game theory: the concept of equilibrium.

Brokos dedicates this entire opening chapter to it, and for good reason — if you don't have a firm grip on what equilibrium means and how to reason about it, everything that follows will be much harder to absorb.

So this chapter takes a deliberate detour away from poker itself and instead examines a handful of extremely simple, everyday situations — situations you have almost certainly been in before, but probably never thought of as exercises in game theory.

The approach here is intentional: by starting with scenarios that are simple enough to reason through completely, you can focus entirely on the logic of equilibrium without getting distracted by the complexity of cards, bet sizes, and hand ranges.
That groundwork then transfers directly to poker once we're ready to go there.

Along the way, the chapter also introduces two other foundational game-theory ideas that will appear constantly throughout the rest of the book: the precise meaning of the word 'strategy' as game theorists use it, and the role that computers play in solving equilibria for games that are too complicated for human reasoning alone.
By the time you finish this chapter, you should be able to clearly define both 'strategy' and 'equilibrium' the way game theorists define them — not loosely, but with real precision.
You should be able to look at a pair of strategies and determine whether they are in equilibrium or not, and to explain exactly why you reached that conclusion.

You should understand the difference between a pure strategy — always doing the same thing — and a mixed strategy, where a player randomizes among options, and you should understand when each type is necessary.
And perhaps most practically, you should understand what it actually takes to randomize your play in a way that is genuinely unpredictable and therefore unexploitable.
That last point might sound simple, but as we will see later in the chapter, it turns out to be surprisingly difficult for human beings to do correctly.
Let's start by getting the vocabulary right.

sections.title: What is a Strategy?
lines: Chapter one, section two. What is a Strategy?
In everyday conversation, the word 'strategy' gets used loosely — someone might say their strategy is to play aggressively, or to be patient, or to trap their opponents.
In game theory, though, the word has a much more precise and demanding definition.
Wikipedia captures it well: a strategy is a complete algorithm for playing the game, meaning it specifies exactly what a player will do in every possible situation that could arise during the game.
Not a general approach, not a rough style of play, but a specific, exhaustive prescription covering every possible situation without exception.
To appreciate how demanding this definition is, think about what a complete poker strategy would actually require.
It's not enough to say you play tight and aggressive before the flop.

A complete strategy needs to say exactly which hands you raise with, which you call with, and which you fold — and in what proportions — from every position, at every table size, against every possible betting action you might face.
And then it needs to describe all of that for every possible flop, every possible turn card, every possible river card, every possible action your opponents might have taken on every street.
Brokos gives a concrete illustration: imagine his pre-flop strategy when sitting first to act at a nine-handed table.
He might raise three times the big blind with any pair of sevens or better, with ace-queen offsuit or better, and with any suited broadway hand.

For suited connectors ranging from ten-nine suited down to seven-six suited, he might raise only twenty percent of the time — meaning four times out of five he folds that hand in that spot, and one time out of five he raises.
He would fold everything else.

But even that level of detail is only the beginning, because a complete strategy would also need to describe how he responds to a three-bet of various sizes from each of his opponents — a different answer potentially for every opponent, every three-bet size, and every hand in his range.
The sheer volume of information required for a genuinely complete strategy is staggering, far beyond what any human being can hold in their head at once.

This is one of the central reasons why computers are so valuable for studying game theory in a rigorous way: the machines can store and process the kind of exhaustive strategic detail that would completely overwhelm a human mind.
Now, when we're actually sitting at a poker table, we're not going to be operating from anything close to a complete strategy in this formal sense.
We make decisions situation by situation, drawing on pattern recognition, intuition, and whatever principles we've internalized from study.
But here's the key insight from the book: even if we can't hold a complete strategy in our heads, we still need to think beyond the current moment.
To make a good decision right now, we often need to consider what situations we might face later in the hand, and how we intend to respond to those situations.

For example, if you're deciding whether to call a bet on the turn, part of that decision involves thinking about what you'll do on the river — what kinds of bets you'll be able to make, what kinds of bets you'll be able to call, what hands in your range can serve what purpose.

Similarly, having some model of your opponent's likely strategy — what range of hands they might have, what they tend to do with strong hands versus weak ones — is essential for making good decisions yourself.
Knowing that a particular river card would leave you vulnerable to bluffs, for instance, might influence which hands you call with on the turn.
So even without a fully explicit strategy, you're always implicitly working with strategic frameworks, both for yourself and for your opponents.
The game-theoretic definition just makes all of that explicit and rigorous.

sections.title: Nash Equilibrium
lines: Chapter one, section three. Nash Equilibrium.
With the definition of strategy in hand, we can now define what game theorists mean by an equilibrium — specifically, what's called a Nash Equilibrium, named after the mathematician John Nash.
Here is the formal definition: an equilibrium is a set of strategies for all players in a game such that no player has any incentive to deviate unilaterally from their strategy.
Let's unpack what that means, because every word in that definition matters.
First, an equilibrium is not a single player's strategy — it's a set of strategies, one for each player in the game.
An equilibrium describes what every player is doing, all at the same time.
Second, the defining condition is that no player has any incentive to deviate unilaterally.
Unilaterally means one player acting on their own, without any change in what the other players are doing.
So the test is: if I told you exactly how I was going to play, and you told me exactly how you were going to play, would either of us want to change anything?
If neither of us wants to change even after we have complete information about the other's plan, then we're at an equilibrium.
That might sound abstract, so let's look at the example the book uses to make it concrete.
Imagine two cars driving toward each other on a road, moving in opposite directions.
Each driver has three choices: stay to the right side of the road from their own perspective, stay to the left side, or drive straight down the middle.
Neither driver has any inherent preference about which side of the road they use — they just want to avoid a collision.
Successfully passing each other safely is worth one point to each driver.
A collision is worth negative ten points to each — a very bad outcome for everyone involved.
We can lay out the entire game in a simple grid where the rows represent what Driver One does and the columns represent what Driver Two does, and each cell shows the payoffs for both players.
If Driver One stays right and Driver Two stays right, both get one point — safe passage.
If Driver One stays right and Driver Two stays left, they collide — both get negative ten.
If Driver One stays right and Driver Two goes down the middle, again, collision — negative ten for both.
If both drivers go down the middle, they collide — negative ten each.
The only cells where both players get the payoff of one are the one where both go right and the one where both go left.
Now let's apply the equilibrium definition to the case where both drivers stay to their right.
Driver One is getting one point — the maximum possible payoff in this game.
If Driver One unilaterally switches to driving left or down the middle while Driver Two keeps going right, Driver One gets negative ten instead of one.
That's clearly worse.
The same logic applies to Driver Two: any unilateral change from 'stay right' makes things worse, not better.
So 'both drive right' is an equilibrium.
By exactly the same logic, 'both drive left' is also an equilibrium.
Neither player can do better by changing alone.
Notice that there are two equilibria here, and neither is inherently better than the other — they produce identical payoffs.
The real world reflects this: in the United States, the convention is to drive on the right, while in the United Kingdom and some former British colonies, the convention is to drive on the left.
Both are stable equilibria; what matters is simply that everyone within a given system agrees on which one to use.
It's also worth noting what never happens in this game: no rational driver would drive straight down the middle.
No matter what the other driver does, going down the middle produces either a collision — negative ten — or another collision.
There is literally no scenario in which it helps.
In game theory vocabulary, this is called a dominated strategy — one that is always worse than at least one of the available alternatives, regardless of what the other players do.
A rational player never plays a dominated strategy.
This example also makes clear something important about what equilibrium does and doesn't mean.
It doesn't mean the strategies produce the best possible outcome in some absolute sense.
It means that given what the other players are doing, no single player can improve their own situation by changing their own approach.
That self-stability is the defining feature.

sections.title: Proving an Equilibrium
lines: Chapter one, section four. Proving an Equilibrium.
The driving example was intuitive enough that the equilibria were pretty obvious — of course both going right or both going left are stable solutions.
But Brokos points out that in more complicated scenarios, it won't always be so clear, and we need a systematic method for proving or disproving whether a given pair of strategies is an equilibrium.
That method follows directly from the definition.
Recall that a set of strategies is an equilibrium if and only if no single player can unilaterally change their strategy and get a better payoff.

So to prove that a particular set of strategies is an equilibrium, you take each player one at a time, hold everyone else's strategies fixed, and ask: is there any change that this one player could make to improve their own payoff?
If the answer is no for every player, you've proven it's an equilibrium.
If the answer is yes for even one player, you've proven it's not.
Let's walk through this with the driving game.
Start with the proposed equilibrium where both drivers go to the right.
Hold Driver Two's strategy fixed — they're going right.
Now look at Driver One's options.
Driver One can go right, getting a payoff of one.
Driver One can go left, getting a payoff of negative ten.
Driver One can go down the middle, getting a payoff of negative ten.
The payoff of one from going right is the highest available.
There is no deviation that improves Driver One's outcome.
Now hold Driver One's strategy fixed — they're going right — and examine Driver Two's options.
By the same reasoning, Driver Two gets one for going right, negative ten for any other choice.
No deviation helps Driver Two either.
Therefore, both-go-right is proven to be an equilibrium.
The book also illustrates how to use this method to prove that something is not an equilibrium.
Consider what happens if Driver One goes right while Driver Two goes left.
Both players get negative ten — they've collided.
Now, is this an equilibrium?
To find out, hold Driver Two's strategy fixed — they're going left — and ask whether Driver One can do better by changing.
If Driver One switches from right to left, they go from negative ten to positive one.
That is a strictly better outcome achieved by a unilateral change.
That single finding is enough — we don't need to check Driver Two.
The pair 'Driver One right, Driver Two left' is not an equilibrium.
And of course the same logic applies from Driver Two's side: if Driver Two switches from left to right while Driver One stays right, Driver Two also improves from negative ten to positive one.
Both players have incentive to deviate, confirming this is not an equilibrium.
One crucial point the book emphasizes: the test is always about unilateral deviation.
It doesn't matter whether both players changing their strategies simultaneously could lead to a better outcome — that's a different question entirely.
The equilibrium test asks what a single player can do on their own, holding everything else constant.
This might seem like a fine distinction right now, but it becomes very important when we move to more complex scenarios.

Sometimes a pair of strategies is not ideal in some global sense — both players might do better if they both changed — but it's still an equilibrium because neither player can improve their own situation acting alone.
In the Going to the Movies scenario coming up next, we'll see exactly that situation play out.
The discipline of checking each player's options one at a time, always keeping the other players' strategies fixed, is the core technique for equilibrium analysis throughout this book.

sections.title: Scenario: Going to the Movies
lines: Chapter one, section five. Scenario: Going to the Movies.
Now we move into the first of the book's interactive scenarios — a situation drawn from everyday social life that turns out to have a surprisingly rich game-theoretic structure.
The setup: two friends, Andrew and Blanca, have decided to go to a movie together.
Andrew would prefer to see a drama; Blanca would prefer to see a comedy.
But here's the key: for both of them, the most important thing is being together.
Andrew would rather see a comedy with Blanca than watch a drama by himself.
Blanca would rather see a drama with Andrew than watch a comedy by herself.
Company trumps genre for both of them.
This has all the ingredients of a game in the game-theory sense: multiple players, each choosing from multiple options, and payoffs for each player that depend on what both of them decide.
Andrew can choose comedy or drama.
Blanca can choose comedy or drama.
What each person gets out of the evening depends on both of their choices, not just their own.
One important detail the book asks you to accept: assume both players make their choices independently, without being able to consult each other first.
You can imagine Andrew lost his phone after they made plans, so they can't coordinate.
Andrew might know Blanca's strategy — he might know she tends to flip a coin, for example — but he won't know the actual result of that coin flip.
The choices happen in isolation.
Now, to do any math, we need to put numbers on the preferences.
For Andrew: his best outcome is seeing a drama with Blanca, which is worth five points.
His second-best outcome is seeing a comedy with Blanca — still together, just not his preferred genre — worth four points.
Going to a drama alone is worth one point.
The worst outcome for Andrew is going to a comedy alone — he misses out on both his preferred genre and his preferred company — worth zero points.
For Blanca, the preference ordering is mirrored.
Her best outcome is seeing a comedy with Andrew, worth five points.
Going to a drama with Andrew is second-best, worth four points.
Going to a comedy alone is worth one point.
Going to a drama alone is the worst for her — worth zero points.
We can organize all four possible outcomes into a two-by-two grid.
The rows represent Andrew's choices and the columns represent Blanca's choices.
In each cell, the first number is Andrew's payoff and the second is Blanca's.
If both go to the comedy: Andrew gets four, Blanca gets five — they're together at Blanca's preferred film.
If Andrew goes to comedy but Blanca goes to drama: Andrew gets zero, Blanca gets zero — they're both alone at a film they chose.
If Andrew goes to drama but Blanca goes to comedy: Andrew gets one, Blanca gets one — again, both alone.
If both go to the drama: Andrew gets five, Blanca gets four — they're together at Andrew's preferred film.
The grid makes the whole structure of the game visible at a glance.
Now for the questions this scenario asks you to work through.
First question: suppose both players always go to the comedy — Andrew goes to comedy, Blanca goes to comedy, every time, as a pure strategy.
Is this an equilibrium?
How can you tell?
Think about it using the definition: can either player unilaterally deviate and get a better payoff?
Second question: what if Andrew's strategy is always to go to the drama and Blanca's strategy is always to go to the comedy?
Is that an equilibrium?
Again, apply the definition — can either player do better on their own?
Try to work through both questions before moving to the answers section.
The reasoning process is at least as important as the conclusions, so think carefully about the structure of the argument, not just the final answer.
Keep in mind that the equilibrium test requires checking whether a unilateral change helps — not whether some combination of changes by both players might produce a better overall outcome.
That distinction is going to matter for at least one of these questions.

sections.title: Equity and Expected Value
lines: Chapter one, section six. Equity and Expected Value.
Before we get to the answers for the Going to the Movies questions, the book introduces two foundational concepts you will use constantly throughout your study of poker: equity and expected value.
These two tools are how we put a number on situations where we're operating under uncertainty — where we don't know exactly what cards will come or exactly what our opponents will do.
Let's start with the broader point about why these tools are necessary.
Poker is inherently uncertain, and many players resist that fact.
You see it all the time: a player won't bluff unless they're confident their opponent will fold.
They won't value bet the river unless they're virtually certain their hand is best.
Some players even feel uncomfortable getting all-in before the flop with king-king because they can't be sure an ace won't appear.
But this resistance to uncertainty is a strategic mistake.
Poker is not a game of certainty.
It is a game of making good decisions under uncertainty, and the best players don't just tolerate that uncertainty — they embrace it.
They accept that the outcome of any individual decision is unknowable in advance, and they focus instead on whether the decision has positive expectation over time.
Uncertainty in poker comes in several layers.
Before the river, you don't know which cards will fall.
You don't know which specific cards your opponents hold.

Even if you could perfectly narrow down an opponent to a range of hands — the set of all hands consistent with their actions so far — you still wouldn't know which particular hand in that range they actually have.
And even if you somehow knew their exact hand, you still might not know how they'd react to your actions — whether they'd call a big bet, whether they'd bluff if you checked.
Equity and expected value are the two main tools for converting that uncertainty into a number you can reason with and act on.
Let's take equity first.

Equity, as the term is commonly used in the poker world, is a measure of how much of the pot a particular hand or range of hands would win on average if there were no further betting and the hand ran out to the river.
It removes uncertainty about future board cards by computing an average across all possible runouts.
The way this works in practice is through equity calculator software.
You enter two or more hands or ranges, and the calculator simulates a hundred thousand or more possible boards, tracking how often each hand wins, loses, or ties.
It then gives you the average fraction of the pot that each hand would win across all those simulations.
Here are some concrete examples from the book.
Before the flop, ace-king offsuit has approximately thirty percent equity against pocket kings.
That means if you got all the money in before the flop with ace-king against king-king, you would win about thirty percent of the total pot on average.
Against a range that includes ace-king, king-king, and ace-ace, ace-king offsuit has about thirty-seven percent equity.
Equity is useful because it collapses all the uncertainty about board cards into a single number.

You might not win this particular hand with your ace-king — in fact, against pocket kings, you'll lose about seventy percent of the time — but you can still say precisely what your holding is worth over the long run.
One important limitation: equity is only a clean number when you're all-in, because at that point there's no further betting to complicate things.

If you're not all-in, knowing your equity is still useful as a rough baseline, but the actual value of your hand will be shaped by many other factors: whether you'll get bluffed off your hand, whether you'll be able to successfully bluff, whether you'll pay off an opponent's value bet, or whether you'll get paid when you have the best hand.
All of those future actions create a gap between your raw equity and what your hand is actually worth in a given spot.
Now let's talk about expected value, which the book abbreviates as EV.

Expected value is a broader tool that tries to account for all possible outcomes of a decision — including the effects of future actions by both you and your opponents — weighted by how likely each outcome is.
The book gives a clear example.
Suppose you're on the river and you make a bluff of fifty dollars into a pot of one hundred dollars.
You don't know whether your opponent will call or fold.
But you can express the value of that bluff as a formula.
When your opponent folds, you win the one-hundred-dollar pot.
When your opponent calls or raises, you lose your fifty-dollar bet.
So your expected value equals the probability they fold times one hundred dollars, minus the probability they call times fifty dollars, minus the probability they raise times fifty dollars.
We can simplify that last part: calling and raising both result in you losing fifty dollars, so we can combine them into a single non-fold frequency.
The formula becomes: EV equals one hundred dollars times the fold frequency, minus fifty dollars times the non-fold frequency.
Now plug in some numbers.
If your opponent folds forty percent of the time, your EV is: one hundred times zero-point-four minus fifty times zero-point-six.
That's forty dollars minus thirty dollars, which equals ten dollars.
So that fifty-dollar bluff into a hundred-dollar pot, against an opponent who folds forty percent of the time, makes you an average of ten dollars every time you make it.
This formula is also useful for a different question: how often does your opponent need to fold for your bluff to break even?
That would be the fold frequency that makes your EV equal to exactly zero.
We'll work through that kind of algebra in detail in the next chapter.
For now, the key point is that expected value is the tool that lets you put a price on a decision under uncertainty, by averaging all the possible outcomes weighted by their probabilities.
The book also includes four practice problems to test your understanding of equity and expected value, and let's work through all of them.
Problem one: how much equity does ten-nine of hearts have against ace-king — ace of spades, king of clubs — before the flop?
The answer is approximately forty-one percent.
This might surprise you — ten-nine suited is not that far behind ace-king offsuit before the flop.
It has two big card outs plus its flush and straight potential.
Problem two: how much equity does ten-nine of hearts have against a range of all pocket pairs fifty-fives or better, ace-jack or better, and king-queen?
Against that wider, stronger range, ten-nine suited has about thirty-seven percent equity.
The suited connector loses a little ground against the full range compared to just ace-king.
Problem three: how much equity does ten-nine of hearts have against ace-ace — ace of spades, ace of clubs — on a flop of eight of hearts, seven of hearts, three of spades?
On this flop, ten-nine of hearts has flopped an open-ended straight draw and a flush draw simultaneously — a massive draw.
Against the pocket aces, ten-nine of hearts has approximately fifty-six percent equity, meaning it is actually a slight favorite on this particular board.
Problem four: suppose you make a bluff of one hundred dollars into a one-hundred-dollar pot on the river.
Your opponent folds forty percent of the time.
Whenever they don't fold, you lose your bet.
What is the expected value of this bluff?
This time you're risking one hundred dollars to win one hundred dollars.
The formula: EV equals one hundred dollars times forty percent, minus one hundred dollars times sixty percent.
That's forty dollars minus sixty dollars, which equals negative twenty dollars.
So this bluff loses you an average of twenty dollars each time you attempt it, even though forty percent of the time you win the pot.
The risk-to-reward ratio doesn't justify the bet at that fold frequency.

Notice the contrast with the earlier example: when you were bluffing fifty dollars into a hundred-dollar pot, you only needed your opponent to fold one-third of the time to break even — a relatively low bar.
When you bluff a full pot-sized bet, you need your opponent to fold fifty percent of the time just to break even.
At forty percent folds, a pot-sized bluff is a money-loser.

sections.title: Scenario: Dodging a Debt
lines: Chapter one, section seven. Scenario: Dodging a Debt.
Now we come to what is arguably the most important scenario in the chapter — a game that, unlike the previous examples, has no equilibrium involving pure strategies at all.
The scenario: Christina owes David one hundred dollars.
It's Saturday night, and both of them independently plan to go to one of two poker rooms — call them Room A and Room B.
If they end up at the same room, Christina will have to pay David the hundred dollars she owes him.
David's goal is to find Christina and collect.
Christina's goal is to avoid David and not pay.
These interests are completely opposed — whatever is good for David is bad for Christina, and vice versa.
The payoff grid for this game is clean and symmetric.
If both go to Room A, David collects his hundred dollars: Christina's payoff is negative one hundred, David's is positive one hundred.
If Christina goes to Room A but David goes to Room B, they miss each other: Christina's payoff is zero, David's is zero.
If Christina goes to Room B and David goes to Room A, they again miss each other: Christina zero, David zero.
If both go to Room B, David again collects: Christina is negative one hundred, David is positive one hundred.
The grid has four cells and only two distinct outcomes: they meet, in which case Christina pays and David receives, or they don't meet, in which case both get zero.
Now for the four questions this scenario poses.
The first question asks you to explain or demonstrate why no single cell in this grid represents an equilibrium.
In other words, why is there no pure-strategy equilibrium for this game?
The second question asks: given that no pure-strategy equilibrium exists, what is the equilibrium for this game?
This is a more challenging question that requires you to think beyond always-do-one-thing strategies.
The third question asks: at that equilibrium, what is each player's expected value?
And the fourth asks you to prove that neither player has any incentive to deviate from the equilibrium strategy.
Let's set up the intuition before you try to work through the answers.
Think about what would happen if Christina simply decided to always go to Room A.
If David knows that's her strategy, he should always go to Room A too — then he always collects.
But if David always goes to Room A, Christina should switch to Room B — then she never pays.
But if Christina always goes to Room B, David should follow her there.
And if David always goes to Room B, Christina should go to Room A.
No matter which room either player commits to, their opponent has a response that beats them.
This is a fundamentally different structure from the driving game or the movie scenario, where we could find stable resting points.
Here, every pure-strategy combination creates a situation where one player is unhappy and wants to change.
The only way out of this cycle is to make your choice unpredictable — to randomize.
If Christina can't be predicted, David can't reliably follow her.
If David can't be predicted, Christina can't reliably avoid him.
The question then becomes: what specific kind of randomization produces an equilibrium?
This is where the concept of mixed strategies becomes essential.

A mixed strategy is one in which a player doesn't always do the same thing, but instead assigns probabilities to their available actions and randomly selects among them according to those probabilities.
The question is what probabilities create a stable equilibrium — where neither player has any reason to change their randomization.
Think about whether it matters which probabilities they use, or whether there's a specific fifty-fifty split that is special.
Also think about question three from a symmetry standpoint: if the game is perfectly symmetric and both players randomize equally, what does that imply about the long-run payoffs?
And for question four, try to construct the mathematical argument showing that no change in strategy by either player can improve their expected value above what the equilibrium gives them.
These are challenging questions, and the next section will walk through all of them in full detail.
The tools you'll use are exactly the expected-value framework we just covered in the previous section.

sections.title: Answers & Explanation
lines: Chapter one, section eight. Answers and Explanation.
Let's now work through both the Going to the Movies answers and the Dodging a Debt answers in full detail.
Starting with Going to the Movies.
Question one: suppose both Andrew and Blanca play a pure strategy of always going to the comedy.
Is this an equilibrium?
The answer is yes — this is an equilibrium.
Here's how to prove it.
We check each player separately, holding the other's strategy fixed.
Start with Blanca: she goes to the comedy, and so does Andrew, putting them in the upper-left cell with payoffs of four for Andrew and five for Blanca.
Blanca's payoff of five is the highest possible payoff she can get in this entire game.
There is simply no cell where she gets more than five.
She has no incentive to change whatsoever.
Now check Andrew: he goes to the comedy while Blanca also goes to the comedy, giving him four points.
Andrew would prefer the drama in terms of the movie itself, so let's ask what happens if he unilaterally switches to going to the drama while Blanca stays at the comedy.
Now we're in the lower-left cell: Andrew goes to drama, Blanca goes to comedy, they're apart.
Andrew's payoff drops to one — lower than the four he was getting by going to the comedy together.
So Andrew's unilateral switch from comedy to drama makes him worse off.
Even though Andrew has a preference for dramas, what he cares about most is being with Blanca, and he loses that by defecting.
Neither player can unilaterally improve their situation.
Therefore, both-go-comedy is an equilibrium.
Now here is the crucial point the book emphasizes about this answer: this equilibrium does not give Andrew his highest possible payoff.
Andrew's personal best outcome is both-go-drama, which gives him five points.
He could get five instead of four — but only if Blanca also changed to the drama.
Since that requires both players to change simultaneously, it doesn't violate the equilibrium condition.
Andrew cannot get five points by acting alone; he can only get five points if Blanca cooperates.
The 'unilaterally' qualifier in the definition is doing real work here.
An equilibrium doesn't have to be the best possible world for everyone — it just has to be a stable point where no individual player is motivated to act differently on their own.

By the same logic, both-go-drama is also an equilibrium, by symmetric reasoning: Blanca can't do better by switching to comedy while Andrew stays at drama, and Andrew is already at his personal maximum.
So this game actually has two pure-strategy equilibria, just like the driving game had two.
Question two: what if Andrew's strategy is always to go to the drama and Blanca's strategy is always to go to the comedy?
Is this an equilibrium?
The answer is no — this is not an equilibrium.
In this scenario, we're in the lower-left cell: Andrew at drama, Blanca at comedy, both alone.
Andrew gets one point; Blanca gets one point.
Now test Andrew: if he switches from drama to comedy while Blanca stays at comedy, Andrew's payoff jumps from one to four.
That is a strictly better outcome achieved by a unilateral change.
Andrew has every incentive to deviate.
That alone is enough to prove this is not an equilibrium.
But let's also check Blanca: if she switches from comedy to drama while Andrew stays at drama, Blanca's payoff goes from one to four as well.
Both players would benefit from unilaterally changing their strategy, so this is definitively not an equilibrium.
The core issue is that in this cell, both players are alone at their movie — both are getting their worst company outcome — and both can fix that by simply switching to what the other person chose.
Now let's move to the Dodging a Debt answers, which involve the more sophisticated reasoning about mixed strategies.
Question one: why does no cell in the Dodging a Debt grid represent an equilibrium?
Let's go through all four cells systematically.
Cell one: both go to Room A.
David gets one hundred dollars; Christina pays one hundred dollars.
David is happy, but Christina can unilaterally improve her situation by switching to Room B — she'd go from negative one hundred to zero.
That's strictly better, so this is not an equilibrium.
Cell two: Christina goes to Room A, David goes to Room B.
Both get zero.
Now David can unilaterally improve by switching to Room A — he'd collect one hundred dollars instead of zero.
Not an equilibrium.
Cell three: Christina goes to Room B, David goes to Room A.
Both get zero again.
This time it's David who can unilaterally improve by switching to Room B — one hundred dollars instead of zero.
Not an equilibrium.
Cell four: both go to Room B.
David collects; Christina pays.
Christina can unilaterally improve by switching to Room A — zero instead of negative one hundred.
Not an equilibrium.
The pattern is clear: whenever they meet, Christina wants to leave.
Whenever they miss each other, David wants to follow her.
There is always exactly one player who would prefer to be doing something else.
Pure strategies are inherently predictable, and in a zero-sum game like this one — where what's good for David is bad for Christina — predictability is exploitable.
The only escape from this cycle is randomization: mixed strategies.
Question two: what is the equilibrium?
The equilibrium is for each player to randomize their choice of poker room, going to Room A with probability one-half and Room B with probability one-half.
The book explains why this specific fifty-fifty split is the equilibrium, not some other random mix.
Christina's situation is symmetric: she doesn't inherently prefer one room over the other, and she has no information advantage that would tell her which room David is more likely to be in.
Her only goal is to be unpredictable so that David has no way to reliably find her.
If Christina were to go to Room A even slightly more than fifty percent of the time — say fifty-one percent — then there would be a clear best response for David: always go to Room A.
By doing so, David would find Christina more than half the time and collect his money more than half the time on average.
Christina's strategy would be slightly exploitable.
At exactly fifty percent, however, it doesn't matter which room David goes to — or even what mix he uses — because he has a fifty percent chance of finding Christina no matter what.
By being perfectly unpredictable, Christina eliminates any advantage David could gain from choosing one room over another.

David's reasoning is perfectly symmetric: if he went to Room A more than fifty percent of the time, Christina could exploit that by going to Room B more than fifty percent of the time, and she'd dodge him more often than not.
At exactly fifty percent, David makes Christina completely indifferent between the two rooms — we'll come back to that word 'indifferent' in the next section.
Also important to note: the earlier scenarios we looked at were cooperative in nature — both drivers want to avoid a collision, Andrew and Blanca both want to be together.
In those games, predictability was an asset, because there are win-win outcomes both players are trying to coordinate toward.
But the Dodging a Debt game is a zero-sum game: every dollar David gains is a dollar Christina loses, and every dollar Christina saves is a dollar David fails to collect.
There are no win-win outcomes here.
In zero-sum games, predictability is a liability, not an asset, because your opponent can always exploit a predictable pattern.
Most two-player poker situations share this zero-sum structure, which is exactly why mixed strategies — and the unpredictability they generate — are so central to poker game theory.
Question three: what is each player's expected value at the equilibrium?
With both players randomizing at fifty-fifty, we can calculate EV by listing all four possible outcomes, computing the probability of each, and weighting the payoff accordingly.
Outcome one: both go to Room A.
Probability: Christina's fifty percent chance of Room A, times David's fifty percent chance of Room A, equals twenty-five percent.
David's payoff in this outcome: one hundred dollars.
Outcome two: Christina goes to Room A, David goes to Room B.
Probability: fifty percent times fifty percent equals twenty-five percent.
David's payoff: zero.
Outcome three: Christina goes to Room B, David goes to Room A.
Probability: twenty-five percent.
David's payoff: zero.
Outcome four: both go to Room B.
Probability: twenty-five percent.
David's payoff: one hundred dollars.
David's expected value equals zero-point-two-five times one hundred, plus zero-point-two-five times zero, plus zero-point-two-five times zero, plus zero-point-two-five times one hundred.
That equals twenty-five plus zero plus zero plus twenty-five, which equals fifty dollars.
So David's expected value is fifty dollars.
Intuitively, this makes sense: David has a fifty percent chance of ending up in the same room as Christina — whichever room that turns out to be — and when he does, he collects one hundred dollars.
Half of one hundred is fifty.
Because this is a zero-sum game, Christina's expected value must be the mirror image of David's: negative fifty dollars.
There is a fifty percent chance she ends up in the same room as David and pays her debt, and a fifty percent chance she avoids him and pays nothing.
Her average outcome is losing fifty dollars.
Question four: prove that neither player has incentive to deviate from the equilibrium.
The method here is the same one we've used throughout: hold one player's strategy fixed and experiment with changes to the other player's strategy.
Let's hold Christina's strategy fixed — she's still flipping a coin and going to each room with fifty percent probability.
Now let's try different strategies for David and compute his EV each time.
First, suppose David plays a pure strategy of always going to Room A.
He goes to Room A with one hundred percent probability and Room B with zero percent probability.
Outcome one: both at Room A, probability fifty percent times one hundred percent equals fifty percent, payoff one hundred dollars.
Outcome two: Christina at Room A, David at Room B, probability fifty percent times zero percent equals zero percent.
Outcome three: Christina at Room B, David at Room A, probability fifty percent times one hundred percent equals fifty percent, payoff zero dollars.
Outcome four: both at Room B, probability zero percent.
David's EV: zero-point-five times one hundred plus zero-point-five times zero equals fifty dollars.
His EV is still fifty dollars — identical to what he got at the equilibrium.
Now try David going to Room A eighty percent of the time and Room B twenty percent of the time.
Outcome one: both at Room A, probability fifty percent times eighty percent equals forty percent, payoff one hundred dollars.
Outcome two: Christina at Room A, David at Room B, probability fifty percent times twenty percent equals ten percent, payoff zero.
Outcome three: Christina at Room B, David at Room A, probability fifty percent times eighty percent equals forty percent, payoff zero.
Outcome four: both at Room B, probability fifty percent times twenty percent equals ten percent, payoff one hundred dollars.

David's EV: zero-point-four times one hundred, plus zero-point-one times zero, plus zero-point-four times zero, plus zero-point-one times one hundred, equals forty plus zero plus zero plus ten, equals fifty dollars.
Still fifty.
The pattern will hold for any strategy David might try, because Christina's fifty-fifty randomization means she's equally likely to be at either room regardless of what David does.
He always has a fifty percent chance of finding her, no matter how he distributes his own probability.
The same analysis works in reverse for Christina: if David plays his fifty-fifty equilibrium strategy, Christina's EV is always negative fifty dollars, regardless of what she does.
She cannot improve beyond negative fifty by changing her strategy.
This is an important nuance the book highlights: the fact that David's EV stays the same when he deviates from the equilibrium doesn't mean he should deviate.

If David plays a pure strategy of always going to Room A while Christina keeps flipping her coin, David's expected value remains fifty dollars, but the strategy pair is no longer an equilibrium — because now Christina could do better by changing her strategy.
She could start always going to Room B, and then she'd dodge David every single time for an EV of zero, which beats her equilibrium EV of negative fifty.
So if David breaks from the equilibrium, he creates an opportunity for Christina to exploit him.
There are infinitely many strategy pairs that produce EVs of fifty for David and negative fifty for Christina in this game, but only the fifty-fifty, fifty-fifty pair is an equilibrium.
The distinguishing feature of the equilibrium is mutual stability: neither player can do better by acting alone, even though a unilateral deviation might not do worse either.

sections.title: Indifference
lines: Chapter one, section nine. Indifference.
The Dodging a Debt analysis leads us directly to one of the most important and counterintuitive concepts in poker game theory: indifference.
Let's start with exactly what happened when we proved the equilibrium in the previous section.

When Christina plays her equilibrium strategy of going to each room with fifty percent probability, we showed that no matter what David does — go to Room A always, go to Room B always, flip a coin, go to Room A seventy percent of the time, any percentage at all — his expected value is always fifty dollars.
In game theory vocabulary, we say that Christina's equilibrium strategy makes David indifferent between all of his available strategies.
He has no preference among them — they're all equally good, which means they're all equally bad, which means none of them can be called the 'right' play.
Similarly, David's equilibrium strategy of going to each room with fifty percent probability makes Christina indifferent between all of her strategies.
No matter what she does, her EV is negative fifty dollars.
Now why does this matter for poker?
Because indifference is actually what you're trying to create when you play correctly.
Think about a river situation where you might bet or check with a particular hand.
If you always bet for value and never bluff, your opponent can simply fold every time you bet and call every time you check.
Your play is transparent — they always know what to do.
But if you always bluff in certain spots, your opponent can call you every time.
Again, they always know what to do.
The ideal scenario — the game-theoretically sound scenario — is to play in such a way that your opponent has no clearly correct response.
If your betting range is constructed so that calling and folding have exactly the same EV for your opponent, then your opponent is indifferent — there is no right play.
You can't force your opponent to make a mistake in the sense of taking an action that is objectively clearly wrong.
What you can do is make the decision so close that it's hard to get right, and human beings under pressure will inevitably err.
Here's a telling example from the book about how sensitive this is.
If Christina were to go to Room A fifty-one percent of the time instead of exactly fifty percent, then David would have a clear best response: always go to Room A.
He'd find Christina fifty-one percent of the time instead of fifty percent, which over the long run translates to more money collected.
That one-percent shift away from fifty is enough to make one room strictly better than the other for David.
Only at exactly fifty percent does Christina make David completely indifferent, giving him absolutely no advantage from choosing one room over the other.
This illustrates something important about equilibrium strategies: they are precisely calibrated.
Getting the frequencies even slightly wrong creates exploitability.
The book also addresses an important point about real-world poker players and their preferences.
In practice, people play poker with all sorts of motivations that aren't purely about maximizing expected value.
Some players are risk-averse — they hate losing big pots even if the expected value math says taking the risk is correct.
Some are risk-seeking — they love gambling, they enjoy the thrill of big swings, and they'll accept worse expected value in exchange for more variance.
Some care about how they're perceived at the table and won't make unconventional plays even when those plays are profitable.
Brokos acknowledges all of that and explicitly sets it aside.
The framework in this book assumes that all players are trying to maximize their expected value and nothing else.
That is a simplifying assumption, not a statement about how real humans actually behave.
But here's the practical implication: even if you have other motivations yourself, understanding your opponents' preferences can be a major edge.
A risk-averse player can be bluffed more easily because they hate losing big pots.
A risk-loving player can be value-bet more aggressively because they'll call with weaker hands for the thrill of having a shot at the pot.
A player obsessed with protecting their hand will fold the turn to a raise even when calling is clearly the better EV play.
Understanding those deviations from pure EV-maximization is how you craft exploitative strategies.
In game-theoretic analysis, indifference has a precise definition: two options are indifferent when they produce the same expected value.
A player who is indifferent between two options has no preference between them — neither is better or worse by the only criterion we're using, which is EV.

In poker terms, the goal is to construct your ranges and frequencies so that your opponents face exactly these kinds of indifference situations — spots where calling and folding produce the same result for them, or where betting and checking produce the same result for them.
That's the standard that equilibrium strategies are designed to achieve.
And finally, the book offers a reframe that might change how you think about the goal of poker.
Most players think about poker in terms of inducing mistakes: they want their opponent to call when they should fold, or fold when they should call.
The game-theoretic reframe is to think about creating indifference: make the decisions so close and so difficult that there is no obviously correct answer.

If you can put your opponent in a spot where calling and folding are genuinely equal, then whatever they do is neither right nor wrong — but in practice, humans faced with genuinely difficult decisions will make errors, and those errors are where your edge comes from.
That shift in perspective — from inducing specific mistakes to creating difficult situations — is one of the most fundamental things this book tries to accomplish.

sections.title: Your Brain Is Not Capable of Randomization
lines: Chapter one, section ten. Your Brain Is Not Capable of Randomization.

We've established that mixed strategies — randomizing your choices with specific probabilities — are sometimes essential for reaching an equilibrium, particularly in zero-sum situations like most poker scenarios.
But there's a serious practical challenge: human brains are genuinely terrible at generating true randomness.
This section of the book addresses that challenge directly, and the advice it gives is more important than it might initially seem.
Let's say you've determined that when facing a bet on the river with a certain bluff-catcher, you should call fifty percent of the time.

Maybe the math tells you that your opponent's bluffing frequency makes calling and folding exactly equal in EV — you're indifferent — and so you've decided to call half the time and fold the other half.
How do you actually implement that?
One approach that seems intuitive but is completely wrong: you tell yourself that you folded the last time you were in this spot, so you'll call this time.
That way, over two situations, you've called once and folded once, achieving a fifty-fifty split.
The book explains why this is flawed in multiple ways.
First, the previous situation was almost certainly not the same situation.
The board was different, the action leading up to it was different, the opponent might have been different, your hand was different.
Even if it looked similar on the surface, the underlying variables were different, which means the correct strategy might have been different too.
You can't treat two distinct hands as instances of the same situation just because they superficially resembled each other.
Second, and more fundamentally, this approach makes your behavior predictable in exactly the way you're trying to avoid.
Your opponent knows that you folded the last time they made this kind of bet.
If you have a policy of alternating — fold one time, call the next — then your opponent can predict that you'll call this time.
And if they can predict your action, they can exploit it.
A person who knew you alternated could time their bluffs to coincide with your 'fold' turns and their value bets to coincide with your 'call' turns.
You would have completely undermined the point of randomizing.

Third, the book points out that even in the hypothetical where you found yourself in precisely identical situations back to back — same board, same action, same opponent, same hand — the correct approach is still to treat each instance as fully independent.
Each time you're in the situation, there must be a fifty percent chance you call, regardless of what happened last time.
The past is not relevant.
If what you did last time influences what you do this time, that's a pattern, and patterns are predictable.
The book uses a beautiful analogy to make this point: think about flipping a fair coin.
The probability of heads on any given flip is exactly fifty percent.
That never changes based on previous flips.
If you flipped heads three times in a row, the probability of heads on the fourth flip is still exactly fifty percent.
Each flip is an independent event.
You cannot and should not try to predict the next flip based on the history of previous flips.
Your poker decisions need to work the same way.
When you're in a spot where you should call fifty percent of the time, each occurrence of that spot needs its own independent randomization, completely unaffected by what you did last time.
So if alternating and remembering previous hands are wrong approaches, what's the right one?
Use an external random number generator.
The book offers several practical options.
If you're playing live and have a digital watch or clock with a seconds display, you can glance at the last digit before acting.
If it's zero, one, two, three, or four — that is, less than five — you call.
If it's five, six, seven, eight, or nine — five or above — you fold.
That gives you exactly a fifty percent chance of calling, determined by a number that neither you nor your opponent can predict or control.
If you're playing online, a coin flip is perfectly workable.
For different probability splits — say you need to call thirty percent of the time — you could roll a ten-sided die and call on a result of one, two, or three.
There are also websites and apps that generate true random numbers on demand, like Random.org.
The key principle is that the randomization device needs to be genuinely unpredictable and outside your control.
Your own thought process does not qualify.

Human intuitions about randomness are notoriously unreliable — we systematically overestimate how often patterns should 'break,' we're influenced by recency, and we have unconscious biases toward certain numbers or choices.
Multiple experiments in psychology have demonstrated that when people try to generate random sequences, the sequences they produce have statistical patterns that a simple algorithm could detect.
We're not wired for true randomness.

The implication for poker is direct and practical: if your game-theoretically correct strategy requires you to call with a hand some fraction of the time and fold the rest of the time, find a mechanical randomization method before you sit down.
Decide in advance what your randomization device will be and how you'll interpret its output.
Do not rely on your own sense of what 'feels random' in the moment, and do not rely on what you did in previous hands.
The goal of randomization is to become unpredictable to your opponent.
Anything that introduces a pattern — however subtle — defeats that goal.

sections.title: Computer Assistance
lines: Chapter one, section eleven. Computer Assistance.

Human brains struggle to generate true randomness, as we've just seen, but that's actually a relatively minor limitation compared to the deeper challenge that game theory presents: finding equilibrium strategies for real poker games in the first place.

Solving the kinds of toy games we've examined in this chapter — the driving game, the movie scenario, the debt-dodging game — is something human beings can do with nothing more than a grid and some arithmetic.
But no-limit Texas hold 'em is not a two-by-two grid.
It is an almost incomprehensibly large game.
The number of possible situations — the number of distinct combinations of hole cards, community cards, position, stack sizes, and action histories — runs into the billions.
Finding true equilibrium strategies across that entire game space is completely beyond human unaided reasoning.
This is why specialized software called game theory solvers, or equilibrium solvers, or just 'solvers,' have become essential tools for serious poker study.

These programs use computational methods to approximate equilibrium solutions for poker situations — not toy games, but real poker spots with specific ranges, specific bet sizes, and specific stack depths.
The word 'approximate' matters here, and the book is careful about it.
Even with modern computing power, solvers don't solve no-limit hold 'em in its full generality.
The fundamental problem is the size of the game's strategy space.
Consider a simple example: you're in a one-dollar two-dollar cash game with two hundred dollars effective stacks.
When it's your turn to bet, you can bet any whole-dollar amount from two dollars up to two hundred dollars.
That's nearly two hundred distinct betting options just for your first action.
After you bet some amount, your opponent can raise to any of potentially hundreds of different sizes.
You could then re-raise.
The number of possible game paths explodes combinatorially with each additional action.
No computer can handle that full complexity in a reasonable amount of time.
Instead, when working with a solver, you restrict the set of available bet sizes to a manageable number.
For example, you might allow flop bets of fifty percent of the pot, turn bets of fifty percent or one hundred percent of the pot, and river bets of fifty percent, one hundred percent, or all-in.
With those constraints in place — just a handful of betting options at each decision point — the solver can compute an equilibrium within a much smaller game.

The book acknowledges that restricting players to three bet sizes produces a result that's probably not too far from the true no-limit equilibrium, but strictly speaking, what the solver finds is an equilibrium for a related but different game — a game where certain bet sizes simply don't exist.
It's an approximation, and the quality of the approximation depends on how well the allowed bet sizes capture the important choices in a given situation.
Solvers also have a second important limitation: they need starting inputs.
If you want to solve a flop situation, you have to tell the solver which range of hands each player is bringing to the flop.
Those ranges are typically not precisely known — they depend on what happened before the flop, how each player tends to play, and many other factors.
Getting the input ranges right is itself a skill, separate from the technical operation of running the solver.
Brokos uses a vivid analogy: think of a scientist designing a laboratory experiment.

If she designs the experiment poorly — uses the wrong controls, asks the wrong question, introduces confounding variables — then the results might be technically valid within the experiment but completely fail to illuminate what she actually wanted to know.
The same is true of solvers: garbage in, garbage out.

A solver will faithfully find the equilibrium for the game you defined, but if you defined the wrong game — if your input ranges are wrong or your bet size choices are inappropriate for the situation — then the output won't tell you what you think it tells you.

There is now a category of solver capable of tackling pre-flop situations, computing equilibrium strategies for the entire pre-flop game including opening ranges, calling ranges, three-betting ranges, and responses.
These are extremely computationally intensive, often requiring hundreds or even thousands of dollars worth of processing time to complete a single analysis.
They are not practical tools for most players studying poker at home.
Despite all these limitations, solvers have been transformative for poker study.

They've allowed researchers and dedicated players to explore equilibrium strategies for real poker spots in ways that were completely impossible before, and they've generated enormous amounts of insight about how to construct balanced ranges, when to use certain bet sizes, and how various strategic choices play out in equilibrium.
The book makes clear that you do not need access to a solver to work through the material here.
Brokos has already done the solver work; when solver outputs appear in the book, they've been set up and interpreted for you.
Your job as a reader is to analyze and learn from those outputs, not to generate them yourself.

But the book also promises that by the time you've finished studying its material, you will have developed the conceptual vocabulary and analytical framework to use a solver independently and productively if you choose to do so.

That's the longer-term payoff: not just understanding what equilibrium means in abstract terms, but building the intuition and skill to work with the tools that reveal equilibrium strategies in the games you actually play.
And that's where we land at the end of chapter one.

We've covered the definition of strategy, the definition of Nash Equilibrium, how to prove or disprove an equilibrium by testing unilateral deviations, the distinction between pure and mixed strategies, why mixed strategies are necessary in zero-sum games, how to calculate expected value under uncertainty, the concept of indifference and why it's the real goal of balanced play, the limitations of human randomization and how to work around them, and the role that computers and solvers play in extending these ideas to real poker.
Every one of these concepts will come up again throughout the rest of the book.
The work we've done in this chapter is the foundation everything else rests on.

sections.title: Overview & Objectives
lines: Chapter two, section one. Overview and objectives.
