Of all the streets in a poker hand, the river is where strategy is most clear-cut.
By the time the final community card has been dealt, hand values are locked in — there are no more draws, no more improvements, no more guessing about what might develop.
That simplicity makes the river the ideal laboratory for studying the fundamentals of betting and bluffing.

On earlier streets — the flop and the turn — the reasons to bet are layered and complex: you might be building a pot, protecting a vulnerable hand, setting up a bluff for a later street, or denying equity to drawing hands.

But on the river, almost every bet should be doing one of two things: it is either a value bet, meaning you are betting because you expect to be called by worse hands, or it is a bluff, meaning you are betting to make better hands fold.
That binary clarity is exactly why this chapter focuses on river play as its central testing ground.
The goal here is not just to understand the river in isolation but to build a foundation that will inform your thinking across all streets.
The concepts we work through — polarized versus condensed ranges, balanced betting, unexploitable calling frequencies — ripple outward into every phase of the game.

We will anchor our most rigorous analysis in situations where hand values are fixed, as they are on the river, but we will also talk about how to apply these ideas in earlier and messier real-world situations.
The chapter begins by introducing the vocabulary of polarized and condensed ranges, defining what each term means and how to recognize each type in practice.
From there, we move into a carefully constructed toy game called the Clairvoyance Game, which isolates the dynamic between a polarized range and a condensed range in its purest possible form.
By solving that game mathematically, we will be able to derive precise optimal strategies for both players — not just as abstract numbers, but as principles that generalize to real poker decisions.
By the time you finish this chapter, you will be able to tell the difference between a polarized and a condensed range and identify which type of range you are playing at any given moment.
You will know which hands are best suited to betting on the river and which should be checked.

You will have a working method for approximating an unexploitable bluffing frequency — meaning a frequency at which your opponent cannot profitably adjust against you by always calling or always folding.

You will similarly be able to approximate an unexploitable bluff-catching frequency, so that when you are on the receiving end of a bet, you know roughly how often to call to keep your opponent from freely printing money with bluffs.
You will understand why holding a polarized range is structurally advantageous and what the real cost of holding a condensed range actually is.
And you will be able to take all of this and apply it to concrete decisions at the poker table.
That is a lot of ground to cover, but the framework is logical and self-reinforcing — once the core ideas click into place, the rest follows naturally.

sections.title: Recognizing Polarized And Condensed Ranges
lines: Chapter two, section two. Recognizing polarized and condensed ranges.
Before we can solve any game or apply any strategy, we need a shared vocabulary for describing the composition of a player's range.
The two key terms in this chapter are polarized and condensed, and understanding precisely what each means is essential.
A polarized range is one that contains strong hands and weak hands — but essentially nothing in between.
You will often hear this described as a nuts-or-air range, and while that phrase captures the spirit of the idea, it is worth being a little more precise.
The hands in a polarized range do not literally have to be the best possible hand or a complete bluff with zero showdown value.
What matters is that every hand in the range can be clearly categorized as either a value bet or a bluff — and the player betting it can articulate why.
When you are value betting, you should be able to name the hands your opponent holds that are worse than yours but that you nonetheless expect to call.
When you are bluffing, you should be able to name the hands your opponent holds that are better than yours but that you expect to fold.
If you cannot pass that test — if you cannot clearly explain what you are beating that will call or what you are beating that will fold — then you probably should not be betting.
Vague motivations like wanting to take the pot down right now or feeling that checking would look weak are not substitutes for this kind of clear reasoning.
Betting and raising ranges, as a general rule, tend to be polarized, and the larger the bet, the more polarized the range should be.
A pot-sized bet or an overbet communicates a range that is very close to the extremes of the strength spectrum.
Before the river, ranges cannot be perfectly polarized — even the strongest hands retain some chance of losing, and even the weakest hands retain some chance of improving.
But by the time the river arrives and hand values are finalized, the polarized ideal can be closely approached.
There is a common misunderstanding about polarized ranges that deserves direct attention: people sometimes assume that a polarized range must be weak, or at least weaker than average.
The logic behind this assumption is that weak hands are easy to come by, so a range built from extremes must lean toward the bluffs.
But this is simply not true in theory.

A player who arrives at the river with many weak hands and few strong ones can still construct a perfectly balanced, polarized betting range — by checking the majority of those weak hands and only bluffing with a carefully selected few.

The reason polarized ranges often seem weak in practice is that real players frequently lack the discipline to check and accept a likely loss without reaching for a bluff, not because theory demands it.
Now let us turn to the other side of this coin: the condensed range.
A condensed range is one made up primarily of medium-strength hands.
These are hands that would beat a bluff at showdown but would lose to a genuine value bet.
Because the defining feature of a condensed range is that it beats weak hands but loses to strong ones, it is sometimes called a bluff-catching range.
That name is apt — the only way these hands can extract additional value from weaker holdings is by inducing bluffs and calling them down.
But calling that name does not mean these hands should always call a bet — just that calling is their primary mechanism for profiting against weaker holdings.
Condensed ranges might actually be ahead of an opponent's overall range, but they cannot expect to be ahead of the range that an opponent would use to call a bet or a raise.
For this reason, checking and calling tend to be the natural actions for condensed ranges.
Because medium-strength hands are inherently vulnerable, it can be tempting to play them aggressively — to bet them for protection, or to raise and drive out opponents who might improve.
As we will see in the Clairvoyance Game, however, that instinct is usually a mistake.

Betting or raising with medium-strength hands against a polarized range tends to backfire: you lose extra money to the strong hands in your opponent's range while simultaneously preventing the weak hands in that same range from making a mistake.
Now let us ground these ideas in a real hand.

Imagine that on the river with a board of Ace of diamonds, eight of clubs, four of clubs, Jack of spades, three of hearts, there is fifty dollars in the pot, stacks of two hundred dollars, and the out-of-position player named Opal checks, while Ivan bets fifty dollars, and Opal calls.
Which player should have a polarized range and which should have a condensed range?
Ivan, who made the bet, should have a polarized range — his range at this point ought to consist of either strong value hands like a set of Jacks or a genuine bluff like six-five offsuit.
Opal, who checked and called, should have a condensed range: medium-strength hands like second pair with King-Jack, or top pair with a weak kicker like Ace-five.
If Opal had a very strong hand like pocket Jacks for a full house, she should have bet or check-raised rather than check-called.
If she had a very weak hand with no showdown value like six-five, she should have folded — and could only consider a bluff-raise, not a check-call.
The hands she most naturally check-calls with are those that beat Ivan's bluffs but lose to his value bets, which is the definition of a condensed range.
From Ivan's side, consider what happens if he holds a medium-strength hand like King-Jack or Ace-five.
Those hands should be checked, not bet.
King-Jack and Ace-five might win at showdown against some of Opal's holdings, but they will not be favorites against the range Opal is willing to call a large bet with.
When Ivan bets large and Opal calls, she is calling with hands that very likely beat King-Jack — an Ace with a better kicker, maybe two pair.

Even if King-Jack happens to be ahead of a few of those calling hands, being ahead of some of the calling range is not enough — Ivan needs to win more than half the time he is called for a bet to generate positive expected value.

The key lesson here is simple but powerful: the player who bet most recently should usually have the more polarized range, and the player who checked and called should usually have the more condensed range.
Recognizing which role you are playing in any given moment is the first step toward playing correctly.

sections.title: Scenario: The Clairvoyance Game (Opal's Strategy)
lines: Chapter two, section three. Scenario — the Clairvoyance Game, Opal's strategy.

To understand the interplay between polarized and condensed ranges at a deep level, we are going to work through a carefully designed toy game called the Clairvoyance Game, first introduced in Bill Chen and Jerrod Ankenman's book The Mathematics of Poker.
The setup is stripped down to its essence, with all the noise of real poker removed so we can focus on exactly one dynamic: a perfectly polarized range competing against a perfectly condensed range.
Here is how the game works.
There is a three-card deck containing exactly one Ace, one King, and one Queen.
One card is dealt to each player, and the goal is simply to hold the higher card at showdown.
The Ace is best, the Queen is worst, and there are no community cards, no draws, and no additional betting streets — just one round of betting followed by a showdown.
The out-of-position player is named Opal, and she is always dealt the King.
The in-position player is named Ivan, and he is dealt either the Ace or the Queen, each with fifty percent probability.
Both players know each other's ranges perfectly: Opal knows Ivan holds either the Ace or the Queen, and Ivan knows Opal always holds the King.
Each player antes one dollar to begin the hand.
Opal acts first and may bet one dollar or check.
If Opal checks, Ivan may then bet one dollar or check.
Whenever a player faces a bet, they may only call or fold — no raising is permitted.
If no bet is made, or if a bet is called, there is a showdown and the high card wins the pot.
Now, before we solve this game, let us put ourselves in Opal's position and reason through what her optimal strategy should look like.
First question: does Opal have a polarized range or a condensed range?
Opal has a condensed range.
Her King sits squarely in the middle of the three-card deck — she beats Ivan's Queen but loses to Ivan's Ace.
Every time they go to showdown, she either wins or loses, and her outcome is perfectly determined by which card Ivan holds.
Ivan, on the other hand, has a polarized range: his Ace is the nuts, guaranteed to win at showdown, and his Queen is the stone bottom of the deck, guaranteed to lose.
Second question: what is Opal's equity in this game?
Equity, remember, is what a player would win on average if there were no betting — if the cards were simply turned over and the pot awarded.

Since Ivan has an equal chance of holding either the Ace or the Queen, Opal wins the two-dollar pot half the time and loses it half the time, giving her an expected value before betting of exactly one dollar.
Her equity is fifty percent of the pot, or one dollar.
Third question: will Opal's actual expected value in this game, once betting is introduced, be higher than, lower than, or equal to her equity?
Her expected value at equilibrium will be lower than her equity.
Equity and expected value are not the same thing.
Equity tells you what you would win in a no-betting scenario.
Expected value tells you what you actually win once both players are playing optimally.
The reason Opal's EV falls below her equity is that the option to bet is structurally better for Ivan than it is for her.
If Ivan holds an Ace, he can bet for value and profit when Opal calls.
If Ivan holds a Queen, he can bluff and profit when Opal folds.
In either case, Ivan has a productive use for his betting option.
Opal, holding the King, has no such productive use.
If Opal were to bet, Ivan could respond perfectly: call with the Ace since he is guaranteed to win, and fold the Queen since he is guaranteed to lose.
When Ivan has the Queen and would fold, Opal wins the pot — but she almost certainly would have won it at showdown anyway.
When Ivan has the Ace and calls, Opal loses an extra dollar she did not need to risk.
Betting gains her essentially nothing and costs her something, so it is a losing proposition.
When Ivan is the one betting, though, Opal's position is genuinely uncomfortable.
She cannot be sure whether Ivan holds the Ace or the Queen.
If she folds, she surrenders a pot she might have won.
If she calls, she risks losing an extra dollar every time Ivan holds the Ace.
That uncertainty is the price she pays for holding the condensed range.
Fourth question: will Opal ever bet her King?
Never.
For the reasons just explained, betting the King provides no upside and introduces downside.
An equilibrium strategy assumes your opponent will play perfectly, and a perfectly playing Ivan will simply call when he wins and fold when he loses.
Against a perfect opponent, Opal's best action is always to check.
Fifth question: if Opal checks and Ivan bets, will she call always, sometimes, or never?
Sometimes — and the reasoning here gets to the very heart of what equilibrium means.
At a Nash equilibrium, neither player can unilaterally improve their expected value by changing their strategy.
Even if Ivan told Opal his exact betting plan in advance, she would have no way to exploit that information.
Let us test two extreme strategies for Opal and see if either is part of an equilibrium.
If Opal always folds when Ivan bets, Ivan can exploit her by bluffing every single time he holds a Queen, winning an extra two dollars each time she folds.
That means always-fold cannot be an equilibrium strategy for Opal.
If Opal always calls when Ivan bets, Ivan can exploit her by never bluffing — he simply bets only his Aces and collects an extra dollar every time she calls.
That means always-call cannot be an equilibrium strategy either.
To be unexploitable, Opal must be genuinely unpredictable.
She must mix between calling and folding at exactly the right frequencies, so that Ivan cannot profit by shifting exclusively toward bluffing or exclusively toward value betting.
This mixed strategy is the only equilibrium option available to her.
Sixth question: if Ivan and Opal swapped positions, so that Opal now acted last while still holding the condensed range, would her expected value increase, decrease, or stay the same?
Remarkably, it would stay exactly the same.
You might assume that position is the source of Ivan's advantage — after all, acting last is usually better in poker.
But in this game, position provides no benefit to Ivan because he already has perfect information about Opal's range.
He knows she has a King before the hand begins, so seeing her check the action first tells him absolutely nothing new.
If the positions were reversed, Opal would gain some information when Ivan checks — she would know he holds the Queen — but she could not profitably act on that knowledge.
She could bet, but Ivan would fold and she would win the same two-dollar pot she would have won at showdown anyway.
The positions change, but the equilibrium strategies and the expected values remain identical.
Ivan's advantage in this game comes entirely from holding the polarized range, not from his position at the table.

sections.title: Scenario: The Clairvoyance Game (Ivan's Strategy)
lines: Chapter two, section four. Scenario — the Clairvoyance Game, Ivan's strategy.
Now let us switch perspectives and think about the game from Ivan's point of view.

We have already established that if Opal were to bet, Ivan's responses would be completely straightforward: call with the Ace since it is the best hand in the deck, and fold the Queen since it is the worst.
There is no strategic complexity for Ivan in responding to a bet from Opal.
The interesting question is what Ivan does when Opal checks, as she always will at equilibrium.
First question: when Opal checks, will Ivan bet the Ace always, sometimes, or never?
Always.
Ivan holds the best card in the deck, and the river is his only opportunity to extract additional money from Opal.
If he checks behind, the hand goes to showdown and he wins the two-dollar pot, full stop.
But if he bets, there is a chance Opal calls and he wins three dollars instead.
There is also a chance she folds, in which case he still wins the pot.
Regardless of whether Opal calls or folds, checking behind with the Ace can never outperform betting with the Ace.
The Ace is the nuts — there is literally no downside to betting it.
Ivan bets the Ace one hundred percent of the time.
Second question: when Opal checks, will Ivan bet the Queen always, sometimes, or never?
Sometimes.
With the Queen, Ivan holds the worst card in the deck and will lose at showdown one hundred percent of the time.
If he checks, he loses the pot and his one-dollar ante — a total of zero dollars in terms of any additional money he puts in, but he does lose the pot value.
His only alternative is to bluff, betting one dollar and hoping Opal folds.
If she folds, he collects the two-dollar pot — money he would otherwise lose.
If she calls, he loses the one-dollar bet on top of the pot he was already going to lose.
Now, just as with Opal's calling strategy, we can test extreme versions of Ivan's bluffing strategy to see if either is sustainable at equilibrium.
If Ivan never bluffs with the Queen, Opal could exploit him by always folding whenever he bets.
Since he only bets Aces, folding is costless for her — she never gives up a winning call.
And because she always folds, Ivan never gets paid on his value bets.
That makes never-bluffing an exploitable strategy.
If Ivan always bluffs with the Queen — meaning he bets one hundred percent of the time regardless of which card he holds — then Opal can exploit him by always calling.
Consider what happens in that case: when Opal calls, she wins three dollars half the time when Ivan has the Queen, and loses one dollar the other half when he has the Ace.
Her expected value from calling is three dollars times one-half minus one dollar times one-half, which equals one dollar.
That is strictly better than folding, which gives her zero dollars.
So always-calling becomes the right response to always-bluffing, and Ivan's strategy is once again exploitable.
Both pure strategies — never bluff and always bluff — are exploitable, which means neither can be part of an equilibrium.
The equilibrium requires Ivan to bluff with some specific frequency between zero and one hundred percent, and the way we find that frequency is through the concept of indifference.
We want Ivan to choose a bluffing frequency that makes Opal genuinely indifferent between calling and folding.
If Opal is indifferent, she cannot exploit Ivan by shifting toward always-call or always-fold.
If she cannot exploit him by shifting, then his strategy is unexploitable.
Before we work out the exact math, it is worth appreciating the conceptual foundation.

Ivan's optimal strategy is a mixed strategy — he bets his Aces every time, and he bets his Queens some of the time — and the mixing with the Queens exists not because he is trying to deceive Opal in some clever sequential way, but because there is genuinely no single best option when Opal is playing correctly.
At the right bluffing frequency, betting a Queen and checking a Queen will have exactly the same expected value for Ivan.
He will be indifferent between them, and because he is indifferent, there is no cost to randomizing.

The game theoretically optimal bluffing frequency is the one that creates this indifference in Opal, and from Ivan's perspective, once he has found that frequency, he can rest easy knowing that no adjustment Opal makes can hurt him.
That is a powerful guarantee, and it is worth understanding deeply before we move on to calculating the exact numbers.

sections.title: Mixed Strategies Arise Only When a Player is Indifferent
lines: Chapter two, section five. Mixed strategies arise only when a player is indifferent.

The Clairvoyance Game has led us to a point where both Ivan and Opal need to mix between two options: Ivan sometimes bluffs and sometimes checks with the Queen, and Opal sometimes calls and sometimes folds when Ivan bets.
But there is a critical principle behind mixed strategies that is often misunderstood, and it is worth stating clearly before we move on.
Mixed strategies in game theory arise only when a player is genuinely indifferent between two options — when both options yield the same expected value.
If one option is strictly better than the other, a rational player should take that option one hundred percent of the time, and that is what we see at equilibrium.
There is a widespread misconception in poker that mixed strategies are about deliberate deception: that you sometimes need to make a suboptimal play in order to keep your opponent guessing.
People often frame this as making bluffs an investment in future value, along the lines of letting yourself get caught bluffing now so that your real value bets get paid off later.
This logic may feel intuitively compelling but it is not sound from a game-theoretic perspective.
It puts the cart before the horse.
Yes, the ultimate purpose of mixing strategies is to be unpredictable to your opponent, but you should not deliberately take a worse option just to create that unpredictability.

If you genuinely believe one option is superior — if calling has higher expected value than folding, for example — then you should take that option every single time, not mix in the inferior choice for the sake of variety.
In the Clairvoyance Game, Ivan does not mix when he holds the Ace.
Betting is strictly better than checking with the Ace, so he bets one hundred percent of the time, even though that makes him completely predictable.
He mixes only when he holds the Queen, and the reason he mixes there is that at the right bluffing frequency, betting and checking have identical expected values.
Neither is strictly better.
He is genuinely indifferent, so mixing has no cost.
And because he has found the exact frequency that renders Opal indifferent between calling and folding, his mixing also prevents Opal from exploiting him.
Here is another way to think about the common misconception.
The argument that you should bluff occasionally as an investment in getting value bets paid off implicitly assumes something contradictory.
It assumes your opponent calls too much when you bluff, but also that your opponent might not call enough when you have strong hands.
You cannot logically hold both beliefs simultaneously.
If your opponent calls too much, you should simply stop bluffing, because your bluffs are losing money.
If your opponent calls too little, you should bluff more aggressively.
You have to pick a consistent belief about your opponent's tendencies and act on it.

If you are genuinely uncertain about how your opponent will respond to a bet — you do not know whether they call too much or too little — then betting and checking have the same expected value for you because you cannot form a confident prediction either way.
In that case, a mixed strategy is appropriate, not because mixing is inherently good, but because you are in a state of genuine indifference.
There is also a dynamic argument sometimes made: that not bluffing in the current situation will cause your opponent to fold more in the future, allowing you to bluff more profitably later.
But this is not an argument for mixing.
If you believe your opponent will adjust their future calling frequency based on what you do now, then you have a clear prediction about the future, and you should act on it directly.
In the current hand, never bluff.
In the next hand where your opponent plans to fold more, always bluff.
That is not a mixed strategy — those are two different pure strategies applied in sequence to two different situations.
The upshot of all this is that mixed strategies at equilibrium are a consequence of indifference, not a separate tool you deploy for strategic variety.
When both options have equal expected value, you can mix freely without cost, and the right mixing frequency is the one that makes your opponent equally indifferent between their available options.
That mutual indifference is what defines a Nash equilibrium: neither player has any incentive to unilaterally deviate from their strategy, because deviating cannot improve their outcome.

sections.title: Indifference is the Goal of a Balanced Betting Range
lines: Chapter two, section six. Indifference is the goal of a balanced betting range.
Now we are ready to work out the precise mathematics of Ivan's bluffing frequency in the Clairvoyance Game.
Before diving into the algebra, let us clarify what Ivan's betting range is actually trying to accomplish.
An intuitive first answer might be that when Ivan bets with a Queen, he is trying to make Opal fold her King.
That is technically correct as a description of what he hopes happens on any individual hand where he holds the Queen.
But it is not a complete description of what his range — which includes both Aces and Queens — is trying to accomplish.
When Ivan bets an Ace, he wants Opal to call.
When Ivan bets a Queen, he wants Opal to fold.
These two desires seem contradictory, so what is the unified goal of the range?
The answer is indifference.
The purpose of a balanced betting range is to make the opponent indifferent between calling and folding.
If Ivan constructs his range correctly, Opal will have no profitable deviation — calling and folding will be equally good for her.
This is the only approach that cannot be exploited.
Any strategy in which Ivan plays Aces differently from Queens — such as betting larger with Aces to build the pot or using some physical tell with Queens — would risk being deciphered by Opal.
Once she notices a difference, she can call when she expects him to have an Ace and fold when she expects him to have a Queen.
Only by playing both hands identically — the same bet size, the same timing, the same everything — can Ivan guarantee that his betting tells Opal nothing about which hand he holds.
Now let us do the math.
The EV of folding for Opal is zero dollars.
She put in one dollar as an ante, but that money is already in the pot and no longer belongs to her — it is a sunk cost.
If she folds when Ivan bets, she neither gains nor loses any additional money.
Her EV from folding is exactly zero.
To make Opal indifferent, Ivan must construct a betting range such that calling also has an EV of zero for Opal.
When Opal calls and Ivan was bluffing with a Queen, Opal wins the two dollars already in the pot plus the one dollar bet, for a total gain of three dollars.
When Opal calls and Ivan was value betting with an Ace, Opal loses the one dollar she puts in to call.
So the expected value of calling equals the bluffing frequency times three dollars, minus the value-betting frequency times one dollar.
In equation form: EV equals percent-bluff times three dollars minus percent-value times one dollar.
We also know that the bluffing frequency and the value-betting frequency must sum to one — every bet is either a bluff or a value bet, with no other possibilities.
So we can replace percent-value with the quantity one minus percent-bluff.
Substituting that in: EV equals percent-bluff times three, minus the quantity one minus percent-bluff, all times one.

Setting EV equal to zero and solving for percent-bluff: zero equals three times percent-bluff minus one plus percent-bluff, which gives us four times percent-bluff equals one, so percent-bluff equals one-quarter.
Ivan's betting range should consist of one-quarter bluffs and three-quarters value bets.
For every four bets Ivan makes, three should be Aces and one should be a Queen.
That one-quarter bluffing frequency is what makes Opal exactly indifferent between calling and folding.
There is a simpler way to express this result that generalizes to any bet size and pot size.
The optimal bluff-to-value ratio equals the bet size divided by the sum of the bet size and the pot size.
In the Clairvoyance Game with a one-dollar bet into a two-dollar pot, that ratio is one divided by one plus two, which equals one-third.
Wait — that gives one-third, not one-quarter.
The ratio of bluffs to total bets is one-quarter, but the ratio of bluffs to value bets is one-to-three, meaning one bluff for every three value bets.

The fraction of bets that are bluffs is one-quarter, which is consistent with the formula Bet divided by Bet plus Pot equaling one divided by three equaling one-third — but let us restate this precisely.

The formula Bet divided by Bet plus Pot gives the proportion of Ivan's bets that are bluffs: with a one-dollar bet into a two-dollar pot, that is one divided by three, approximately thirty-three percent.
Actually, let us revisit the arithmetic carefully.
With a one-dollar bet and a two-dollar pot, the formula gives one divided by one plus two equals one-third.
But our algebraic solution said one-quarter.
The reason for this apparent discrepancy is how we set up the EV equation.
When Opal wins a call, she wins three dollars: the two-dollar pot plus the one-dollar bet.
So the equation is three times bluff-frequency minus one times value-frequency equals zero, yielding bluff-frequency equals one-quarter.

The formula Bet divided by Bet plus Pot applies when computing bluff-to-value ratios for a pot that has already incorporated the bet from both players, and care must be taken about what counts as the pot.
Regardless of the precise formula, the conceptual takeaway is robust and important: the optimal bluffing proportion in a betting range increases as the bet size increases relative to the pot.
The book provides a table illustrating this relationship across a wide range of bet sizes.
At a quarter-pot bet, the bluffing frequency is one-sixth — roughly seventeen percent.
At a half-pot bet, it is one-quarter or twenty-five percent.
At a pot-sized bet, it is one-third or about thirty-three percent.
At a bet of twice the pot, it is two-fifths or forty percent.
The bluffing frequency keeps rising with the bet size but never reaches fifty percent.

A bluffing frequency of fifty percent or higher would always make calling profitable for Opal, regardless of bet size, because at that point she is winning often enough to overcome the unfavorable odds when she loses.
Two practical factors govern how much you get to bluff in a real poker situation.
The first is the number of value bets you can credibly have.
The more strong hands you could plausibly hold in a given spot, the more bluffs you get to include alongside them.
In spots where your range is rich with strong hands, you have more room to bluff.
The second factor is how large your bet is relative to the pot.
Bigger bets afford you a higher bluffing frequency because they offer your opponent worse pot odds.
When you bet small, your opponent is getting such a favorable price that they should call even knowing they will lose most of the time — so there is less room for bluffs.
When you bet large, your opponent needs a higher probability of catching a bluff to justify calling, which means you need more bluffs in your range to make calling worthwhile for them.
The practical implication is that your bluffing frequency should scale upward when you bet larger — and crucially, you should use the same bet size for both value bets and bluffs.
If large bets were always bluffs and small bets always value, a competent opponent could simply fold to large bets and call small ones.
The key is that the same bet size communicates both possibilities, forcing the opponent to guess.

sections.title: Optimal Calling Frequency
lines: Chapter two, section seven. Optimal calling frequency.
We have now worked out how often Ivan should bluff with his Queens in the Clairvoyance Game.
The next question is the mirror image of that problem: after checking and facing a bet from Ivan, how often should Opal call?
The process for answering this question exactly parallels the process for finding Ivan's bluffing frequency.
We start by asking what Opal's calling strategy is designed to accomplish.
Recall that Ivan's betting strategy was designed to make Opal indifferent between calling and folding.
By symmetry, Opal's calling strategy is designed to make Ivan indifferent between two options as well.
But which two options?
For Ivan holding an Ace, there is no dilemma: betting is strictly better than checking, because he is guaranteed to win at showdown anyway and betting gives him a chance to win more.
There is nothing Opal can do to make Ivan hesitate when he holds the nuts.
The action Opal's calling strategy must target is Ivan's decision about whether to bet or check when he holds the Queen.
Checking a Queen has an expected value of zero for Ivan: he always loses the pot at showdown, so he walks away with nothing, but he also risks no additional money.
For Opal's calling strategy to be optimal, she needs to call at a frequency that makes betting a Queen also have an expected value of zero for Ivan.
If betting a Queen has positive expected value, Ivan will bluff too aggressively and Opal will wish she had called more.
If betting a Queen has negative expected value, Ivan will never bluff and Opal can fold every time he bets without cost.
Only when betting a Queen has exactly zero expected value for Ivan — the same as checking — is the strategy balanced.
Now let us write the expected value equation for Ivan when he bets a Queen.
If Opal folds, Ivan wins the two dollars already in the pot.
Note carefully: that two dollars is the net gain from the bet, because Ivan was going to lose the pot anyway.
If Opal calls, Ivan loses the one dollar he bet.
So the expected value equation is: EV equals fold-frequency times two dollars, minus call-frequency times one dollar.
Since folding and calling are Opal's only two options, fold-frequency equals one minus call-frequency.
Substituting: EV equals the quantity one minus call-frequency, times two, minus call-frequency times one.

Setting EV equal to zero and solving: zero equals two minus two times call-frequency minus call-frequency, so zero equals two minus three times call-frequency, giving call-frequency equals two-thirds.
Opal should call two-thirds of the time when Ivan bets.
This calling frequency makes Ivan exactly indifferent between bluffing with his Queen and checking it.
There is a general formula for this as well: the optimal calling frequency equals one minus the quantity Bet divided by Bet plus Pot.
In the Clairvoyance Game with a one-dollar bet into a two-dollar pot, that is one minus one-third, which is two-thirds — consistent with our algebraic result.
This formula is worth understanding in words: the larger Ivan's bet relative to the pot, the smaller the fraction of her range Opal needs to call in order to make bluffing unprofitable for him.
When Ivan bets half the pot, Opal needs to call two-thirds of the time.
When Ivan bets the full pot, she only needs to call half the time.
When Ivan bets twice the pot, she only needs to call one-third of the time.
The intuition is straightforward: larger bets cost more when they fail, so Ivan's bluffs become more expensive and Opal can afford to let more of them through.
The book provides a table showing optimal calling frequencies for various bet sizes relative to the pot.
At a quarter-pot bet, the optimal call frequency is four-fifths, or eighty percent.
At a half-pot bet, it is two-thirds, or about sixty-seven percent.
At a pot-sized bet, it is one-half, or fifty percent.
At a bet of twice the pot, it is one-third, or about thirty-three percent.
At a bet of ten times the pot, it drops all the way to one in eleven, or about nine percent.
Calling frequency decreases monotonically as bet size increases.
This has a critical practical implication.
When you face a very large bet, you do not need to call very often to neutralize your opponent's bluffs.

Many players feel that they should call large bets more often because the stakes are higher, but game theory says the opposite: large bets justify folding more of your range, as long as you retain some calling frequency at all.

The reason you must retain some calling frequency no matter how large the bet is that if you fold one hundred percent of the time, bluffing becomes infinitely profitable for Ivan — he can win the whole pot with any two cards.
The goal of your calling strategy is not to win money on every call but to ensure that bluffing does not become a free money machine for your opponent.
As long as you call often enough that bluffing is zero EV for Ivan, you have done your job.
Everything beyond that — the question of which specific hands to call with — is a separate discussion, but the frequency itself is determined by this indifference calculation.

The same formula applies in real poker with different bet sizes and pot sizes, and while you will not solve equations at the table, having an intuitive sense of these frequencies will help you avoid the twin mistakes of calling too much or folding too much when facing polarized bets.

sections.title: What's This Game Worth?
lines: Chapter two, section eight. What's this game worth?
We have now solved the equilibrium strategies for both players in the Clairvoyance Game.
Opal always checks and calls Ivan's bets two-thirds of the time.
Ivan always bets his Aces and bluffs with his Queens one-third of the time.
The natural follow-up question is: what does this all add up to?
What is the game actually worth to each player when both are playing optimally?
Before calculating, the book poses a variation on the game that sharpens the intuition.
Suppose Ivan can bet any amount from one dollar up to one thousand dollars — he has complete freedom to choose his bet size and construct his range however he wishes.
How much should he bet, and why?
Hold that question in mind as we work through the value calculation for the original game.
The cleanest way to calculate Ivan's EV is to exploit a key property of balanced betting ranges.
We have established that Ivan's balanced range makes Opal indifferent between calling and folding.
Indifference means that both actions yield exactly the same EV for Opal.
It does not matter whether Opal always calls, always folds, or uses any mixture in between — her EV is the same.
Since her calling decision does not affect the outcome, let us make the calculation as simple as possible by imagining that Opal always folds when Ivan bets.
If Opal always folds, Ivan wins the two-dollar pot every single time he bets, regardless of whether he is holding an Ace or a Queen.
When Ivan checks — which only happens when he holds a Queen and decides not to bluff — he goes to showdown and loses the two-dollar pot.
Now let us compute Ivan's overall betting frequency.
Half the time Ivan is dealt an Ace, and he always bets the Ace.
The other half of the time Ivan is dealt a Queen, and he bets one-third of those.
So Ivan bets the Ace with probability one-half, and bets the Queen with probability one-half times one-third, which is one-sixth.
His total betting frequency is one-half plus one-sixth, which equals three-sixths plus one-sixth, equaling four-sixths, which reduces to two-thirds.
Two-thirds of the time, Ivan bets and wins two dollars.
His expected value from the game is two-thirds times two dollars, which is four-thirds dollars, or approximately one dollar and thirty-three cents.
But remember that Ivan had to ante one dollar to play, so his net profit — his overall EV from the game — is one dollar and thirty-three cents minus one dollar, which equals thirty-three cents.
Ivan averages a profit of thirty-three cents each time the Clairvoyance Game is played.
Since the game is zero-sum and both players ante one dollar, Opal must average a loss of thirty-three cents, leaving her with sixty-seven cents of her original one-dollar ante.
Now recall that with no betting at all, neither player would have an advantage — both would have an EV of zero, and both would simply win their ante back exactly half the time.
What betting does is allow Ivan to win the pot not only when he has the Ace — he was going to win those pots even without betting — but also some fraction of the time when he has the Queen.
By bluffing at the right frequency, Ivan extracts value that would simply evaporate in a no-betting game.
Opal cannot stop this.
Ivan could literally announce his strategy to her — tell her he will bet every Ace and one-third of his Queens — and she still cannot prevent him from winning that thirty-three cents per game.
If she calls more often, she catches more bluffs but pays off more value bets.
If she folds more often, she saves on value bets but surrenders more pots to bluffs.
The math works out the same either way, because Ivan's range renders her indifferent.
That said, Ivan only captures this guaranteed profit if he sticks to his balanced betting range.
If he gets greedy and starts bluffing more than one-third of his Queens, Opal's calls become profitable and she should always call.
If both players always call and Ivan always bluffs, the game reverts to zero EV for both — Ivan's edge disappears entirely.
The guaranteed thirty-three cents per game is the reward for perfect balance, not for aggression alone.
Now let us return to the question of the optimal bet size.
We have seen that betting allows Ivan to win the pot some fraction of the time when he holds a Queen.
We have also seen that larger bets allow a higher bluffing frequency.
Putting these two facts together, larger bets let Ivan include more bluffs in his range, which means he wins the pot more often when holding a Queen.
It follows logically that bigger bets are better for Ivan.
The optimal strategy for a player with a perfectly polarized range is to bet as large as possible and include as many bluffs as necessary to maintain balance.
The book provides a table illustrating this with striking clarity.
At a bet of twenty-five percent of the pot, Ivan's EV is twenty cents per game.
At a half-pot bet, it is thirty-three cents.
At a pot-sized bet, it is fifty cents.
At a bet of twice the pot, it is sixty-seven cents.
At a bet of three times the pot, it is seventy-five cents.
At a bet of ten times the pot, it is ninety-one cents.
At a bet of ten thousand percent of the pot, it approaches ninety-nine cents.
The EV of the game for Ivan approaches but never quite reaches one dollar — which would mean Ivan wins the entire pot every single time — as the bet size grows toward infinity.
What is happening at extreme bet sizes?
As the bet grows very large, Ivan's bluffing frequency approaches but never quite reaches fifty percent.

He is bluffing nearly half the time, but Opal still cannot profitably call because a call requires putting up such a large amount that even catching all those bluffs barely compensates for the losses on value bets.
So Opal folds almost all of the time, and Ivan effectively takes the pot most of the time regardless of his card.
Why, then, do we not see massive overbets in every real poker game?
Part of the answer is that most players, even experienced ones, do not understand the theory and are not comfortable making very unconventional plays.
But there is also a structural answer: massive overbets are only optimal when your range is as perfectly polarized as in this toy game.
In the real world, ranges are rarely so clean.
The next chapter will explore what happens when the perfectly polarized range assumption is relaxed, and we will see that the optimal bet size changes significantly.

sections.title: Real World Applications
lines: Chapter two, section nine. Real world applications.
The Clairvoyance Game is a toy model — beautifully clean but not something you will ever play at a casino table.
The real value of working through it is being able to import its lessons into actual poker decisions.
Let us talk about how the principles from this chapter apply in practice.
The first and most important application is knowing which player should be the aggressor on any given street, and this comes down to recognizing which player has the nuts advantage.
The nuts advantage does not simply mean having more equity on average — it means having more of the very strongest hands in your range.
Very strong hands have a lot of equity by definition, so the two often correlate, but the distinction matters.
The player whose range contains more nut-level holdings is the one who should be betting, because they can build a polarized range with genuine strength at the top.
The convention of checking to the raiser is actually an intuitive application of this principle.

The pre-flop aggressor is more likely to hold premium hands like pocket Aces, pocket Kings, or Ace-King, because a player in early position who raised almost certainly would have three-bet with those holdings rather than just calling.
The big blind caller, by contrast, has a range that skews toward medium-strength hands simply by virtue of the calling range they use pre-flop.
So the default play for the big blind caller on the flop is to check to the pre-flop raiser, who can then construct a polarized betting range.
This principle extends across streets: after you check and call a bet on the flop, your default on the turn should still be to check to the aggressor.
One common and costly mistake occurs when a player slowplays a strong hand and then, fearing their opponent will not bet again, decides to lead out on a later street.
The instinct is understandable — you have a big hand and you want to get money in.
But leading out from a position that has been passive signals unexpected strength, and smart opponents will often respond by folding the exact hands you were hoping to extract value from.
Causing your opponent to fold with a strong hand is just as bad as having them check behind.
In fact, it is probably worse, because at least a check-behind does not cost you any money.
There are exceptions — situations called donk bets, once derided as the plays of novices but now recognized as occasionally correct.
Donk betting is appropriate when a new community card dramatically shifts the balance of power in favor of the previously passive player.
A classic example: you call a pre-flop raise from the big blind, check-call the flop, and then the turn pairs a low card on the board.
Because a big blind caller's range is full of smaller cards, you are more likely than the pre-flop raiser to have turned trips.
In addition, the pre-flop raiser is less likely to have flopped middle pair — that is a medium-strength hand that offers little incentive to bet the flop.
So when the board pairs a smaller card on the turn, the conventional wisdom of checking to the raiser may be exactly backwards in certain spots.
You should consider leading out with a polarized range, including your turned trips for value and some of your draws as bluffs.
Yes, your opponent might realize you have trips, but that becomes apparent the moment the card falls.
The best deception at that point is not to slow down but to balance your strong hands with credible bluffs.
Another important real-world theme is what to do when the aggressor from the previous street stops betting.
A check from the player who had been betting is a signal that their range has shifted.
They likely hold either a weak hand that they decided not to bluff or a medium-strength hand trying to reach showdown cheaply.
This is your cue to start driving the action with a polarized range of your own.
You may still check the majority of your range — the concept does not mean you should bet with just anything — but you should be betting some strong hands and some bluffs.
On the topic of bluffing directly: many players only bluff when they feel confident that a fold is coming.
This is unnecessarily restrictive.
The threat of a bluff is what gives your value bets credibility, and the threat of strong hands is what gives your bluffs credibility.
You should expect to bluff in proportion to how many value bets you could reasonably have in a given situation.
The specific question to ask is not whether this is a good bluffing spot in isolation but which of your weak hands are the best candidates for a bluff in this spot.
The best bluffing hands tend to be those with some chance of improving — draws, gut-shots, flush draws — because they have two ways to win: you either make the hand or your opponent folds.
On the related topic of slowplaying: it is a common mistake to try to trap opponents into bluffing when you have a strong hand.
The Clairvoyance Game teaches us that deception does not come from checking strong hands — it comes from betting strong hands alongside credible bluffs.
The threat of bluffs is what gives your opponent incentive to call your value bets.
If you check your strong hands, you remove the pressure your opponent feels, and you often miss a bet they would have called.
You cannot get paid on a hand you do not bet.
The big blind defense situation illustrates another important angle on the condensed-range experience.
In games with antes and small raises, you will often find yourself in the big blind getting very good odds to call with weak hands.
Conventional wisdom has historically been to fold in these spots because the hand quality is poor.
But game theory supports calling when the price is right, even knowing you will be playing a condensed range on future streets.
In the Clairvoyance Game, the player with the condensed range guarantees herself sixty-seven cents even starting from a disadvantaged position.
If it only costs you fifty cents to enter a game where you are guaranteed to keep sixty-seven cents, you should play — even accounting for positional disadvantage and weak cards.
The key insight is that there is a floor to how badly the condensed range can perform, and that floor may be above the cost of entry when the price to play is low enough.
The instinct to bet medium-strength hands for protection deserves one more discussion.
Sometimes a player bets a medium hand because they know it is vulnerable and they want to lock up the pot right now.
The Clairvoyance Game shows why this almost always backfires.
When you bet a medium-strength hand, you allow your opponent to play perfectly: they call with everything that beats you and fold everything that does not.
You lose the bet to their strong hands and they fold before giving you anything with their weak hands.
If instead you check and your opponent bets, you have a chance to win that bet from their weak hands when you call.
Either way you lose to their strong hands, but only by checking and calling do you create an opportunity to profit from their bluffs.
Finally, on the topic of bet sizing: do not be afraid of big bets when you have a genuinely polarized range.
Large bets on the turn and river — pot-sized or even larger — extract more value from strong hands and more fold equity from bluffs than smaller bets do.

It is common to see uniform half-pot bets across all streets, but a better pattern is often to use small bets early, when ranges are wide and equity is distributed, and much larger bets later, when ranges have polarized naturally through the action.

sections.title: Test Yourself
lines: Chapter two, section ten. Test yourself.
This section applies the concepts from the chapter to concrete no-limit hold'em scenarios, all played at a nine-handed table with two and five dollar blinds and five-hundred-dollar effective stacks.
Working through these examples step by step reveals how polarized and condensed range theory translates directly into in-game decisions.
First scenario: the first player to act pre-flop raises to twenty dollars and you call from the big blind with the seven of hearts and seven of spades.
The flop comes queen of diamonds, seven of clubs, three of hearts.
You have flopped bottom set — a very strong hand.
Do you bet or check?
The correct answer is check.
Even though you have an extremely strong hand, your overall range as a big blind caller is condensed.
By calling a pre-flop raise from an early position raiser, your range is full of medium-strength hands and does not contain the premium holdings the raiser could reasonably have.
Playing according to your range means playing consistently with all the hands you could hold in this situation, not just the specific hand you happen to have today.
Checking with your set of sevens is the correct play because it is consistent with how you would play all the medium-strength hands in your condensed range.
Betting would be inconsistent and revealing — it would tell your opponent that you have something special, since nothing in the condensed range really benefits from leading out.
Checking allows the pre-flop raiser to construct a polarized betting range, and many of the hands he bets with will be weaker than your set.
You can then decide whether to call or raise after he bets.
If he checks the flop behind, you can revisit the decision on the turn.
Second scenario: now you are the pre-flop raiser, raising in first position with the seven of hearts and seven of spades, and the big blind calls.
The same flop comes: queen of diamonds, seven of clubs, three of hearts.
Your opponent checks.
Do you bet or check?
The correct answer is bet.
As the pre-flop aggressor, you are expected to have the more polarized range.
Your opponent's check is consistent with the condensed range she is playing from the big blind.

Betting your set is the most deceptive thing you can do here, because it is entirely consistent with how you would play many other hands from your polarized range: a continuation bet with two overcards, with an overpair like nines or tens, with air as a bluff.
Because all of those hands look the same when you bet, your strong hand is effectively camouflaged.
Your opponent still has incentive to call even knowing that you could easily have the best hand, because she must also worry about not getting pushed off her equity when you are bluffing.
Third scenario — the conceptual question: you held the same hand on the same board with the same stacks in both examples, yet in the first case you checked and in the second you bet.
What is the difference?
The difference is range, not hand strength.
As the big blind caller, you represent a condensed range, and checking is how you play the entire condensed range consistently.
As the pre-flop raiser, you represent a polarized range, and betting is how you play the entire polarized range consistently.
Strong hands do not have a fixed correct action — the right action for your strong hand is whatever is consistent with the range you are representing in that particular situation.
Fourth scenario: you call from the big blind with the eight of hearts and eight of spades against the same pre-flop raise to twenty dollars.
The flop comes queen of diamonds, seven of clubs, three of hearts.
You check, your opponent bets twenty-five dollars.
Do you fold, call, or raise?
The correct answer is call.
You have already made a good start by checking.
Now you have a medium-strength hand: a pair of eights that beats bluffs but likely loses to many of your opponent's value hands.

Raising is inappropriate here for the same reasons Opal should not raise in the Clairvoyance Game — your hand is not strong enough to raise for value since better hands will not fold, and it is far too strong to fold.
It is a classic bluff-catcher, and bluff-catchers call.
Raising with a pair of eights would be like betting the King in the Clairvoyance Game: your opponent's strong hands call and their weak hands fold, and you accomplish nothing good.
A common concern when calling in this spot is that you might face additional bets on the turn and river that you cannot call.
This concern is valid but should not push you to fold here.
First, your opponent does not know you have eights — for all he knows you could have seven-seven, king-queen, or some other hand that would happily play for a large pot.
That uncertainty constrains his ability to barrel you off your hand.
Second, facing additional pressure is the worst case, not the guaranteed case.
Sometimes he will slow down, and sometimes you will get to showdown and win.

Fifth scenario: same setup — you called from the big blind with eight-eight and check-called the flop, then check-called again when the turn brought the two of clubs and your opponent bet fifty dollars.
The river is the eight of diamonds, giving you a set.
Do you bet or check?
Check, even though you just made a very strong hand.
Your entire line through this hand has told the story of a condensed range: check-call, check-call.
Your opponent has the polarized range and should continue driving the action on the river.
By checking again, you let him do exactly that.
He might bet a hand that loses to your set, giving you a call and a win.
He might bet a bluff, again giving you a profitable call.
If you lead out, you are suddenly acting outside the narrative of your range, which signals strength and causes him to fold the very hands you want to extract value from.

Sixth scenario: you called from the big blind with the eight of diamonds and seven of diamonds, checked-called on a flop of jack of hearts, seven of clubs, three of hearts, and now the turn is the seven of spades.
You have turned trips.
Do you bet or check?
Checking is acceptable, but this is one of those rare cases where betting is worth serious consideration.
Because you are the big blind caller and the board has paired a low card, you are now more likely to have trips than your opponent.
His strong hands — overpairs — will often fear that you hold the seven and may check behind if you give them the chance.
If that happens, you might only win one more bet across the rest of the hand instead of two.
By betting, you present your opponent with a different risk: the risk of folding the best hand to a bluff.
You can balance your trip sevens with bluffs like draws that you check-called the flop with, making your bet range credible.
Seventh scenario: same situation but now you hold the ten of clubs and nine of clubs, giving you an open-ended straight draw on the turn when the seven of spades falls.
Should you bet or check?
Again, betting deserves consideration.
You hold a draw with roughly twenty-three percent equity — a meaningful chance to improve — but you are currently behind anything that would call a bet.
Betting as a bluff here is reasonable, both because you are more likely to have turned trips than your opponent and because your draw gives you outs if called.
Winning the pot immediately is worth more to you than a roughly one-in-four chance of improving to the straight on the river.
Eighth scenario: after a pre-flop raise that you called from the big blind with queen of spades and jack of spades, the flop comes queen of diamonds, seven of clubs, three of hearts.
You check, and your opponent checks behind.
The turn is the two of clubs.
Do you bet or check?
Bet.
When the pre-flop aggressor declines to bet the flop, that is a signal that his range has weakened.
He likely holds either a weak hand he declined to bluff or a medium-strength hand that prefers not to grow the pot.
This is your cue to take the lead with a polarized range.
Queen-jack is a strong hand on this board — not quite the nuts, but strong enough to bet for value.
Your opponent's range, having checked back, should contain many hands that will call you with worse: small pairs, ace-high hands, various draws.
Ninth scenario: same pre-flop and flop setup but now you hold ace of hearts and six of hearts, and you are on the turn after your opponent checked back the flop.
Do you bet or check?
Check.
While your opponent's flop check does give you an opportunity to bluff, you should not bluff with every weak hand in your range.
The correct approach is to bet a polarized range that includes your strong hands and a fraction of your weaker hands as bluffs.
Ace-six with a low flush draw has at most three outs to improve against anything that would call, making it a poor bluffing candidate.
Even if your opponent folds, he is probably folding a hand you already beat — not exactly a triumph.
Better bluffing candidates here are hands with more substantial draws, like five-four of diamonds or eight-six of clubs, which have much better chances of improving to winning hands if called.
You get to bluff some of the time after your opponent checks back, but not too much, and not with your worst bluffing candidates.

sections.title: Conclusion
lines: Chapter two, section eleven. Conclusion.
The Clairvoyance Game is a simple structure — three cards, one round of betting, two players — but the insights it generates are profound and far-reaching.
Let us consolidate what we have learned and understand exactly why these lessons matter.
The central dynamic of the game is a perfectly polarized range, Ivan's Aces and Queens, pitted against a perfectly condensed range, Opal's Kings.
Opal holds the middle card and has nothing to gain from betting.
If she bets, Ivan simply calls with hands that beat her and folds with hands she already beats.
So Opal always checks and, when Ivan bets, calls at a frequency carefully calibrated to make bluffing unprofitable for him.
That calling frequency is two-thirds in this particular game, derived from the condition that Ivan must be indifferent between betting and checking when he holds a Queen.
Ivan, by contrast, has a clear advantage from holding the polarized range.
He bets every Ace because there is no reason not to — it is the best hand and every dollar bet with it is a profitable investment.
He bluffs one-third of his Queens because at that frequency, Opal cannot profitably always call or always fold.
He is indifferent to which she does, and his guaranteed profit of thirty-three cents per game flows from the structural advantage of holding strong hands and weak hands rather than medium ones.
The key lesson about aggression is fundamental: aggression is valuable, but only because it is the vehicle for leveraging a polarized range.
Having a polarized range is the actual advantage.
If Opal were to bet aggressively with her King, she would not be leveraging any advantage — she has no structural advantage to leverage.
She would simply be making it easier for Ivan to play correctly against her.
Aggression without a polarized range is not strength — it is just noise that costs you money.
The second key lesson is about knowing your role on each street.
Before deciding how to play any hand, you should identify which player in the current situation is more likely to hold very strong hands.
That player — the one with the nuts advantage — should be driving the action.
The other player should be checking and finding an appropriate calling frequency.
Importantly, your role can change from street to street.
You might hold the condensed range on the flop, as a big blind caller, but acquire the nuts advantage on the turn if the board shifts in your favor.
Reassess every street.
The third lesson is that uncertainty about where you stand is not catastrophic.
Many players deeply dislike being in spots where they face a bet from a range that could be strong or weak.
That discomfort is understandable — there is a genuine disadvantage to holding the condensed range.
But the Clairvoyance Game quantifies exactly how bad it can be, and the answer is reassuring.
By calling at the right frequency, you can guarantee yourself a floor on your losses.
There is a limit to how much the polarized range can extract, and a properly calibrated calling strategy enforces that limit.
The fourth lesson is about building a fold range.
When you face a bet, some part of your range should fold and some part should call.
A useful mental check before folding is to ask yourself what hands you could plausibly hold in this situation that you would call with.
If you can name some, you can fold your current hand with confidence.
Before calling, ask the reverse: what hands could you hold here that you would fold?
These reciprocal checks ensure that you are never folding your entire range or calling your entire range, both of which are exploitable.
The fifth lesson is that never bluffing is exploitable.
Some players hold off on bluffing until they are certain a fold is coming, or until they have established some kind of read on the opponent.
But if you never bluff in a situation where you could plausibly have strong hands, an opponent who simply folds can beat you.
The check for this is the mirror image of the fold check: before checking a weak hand, ask yourself what other weak hands you could hold that you would bluff with.
If you cannot name any, reconsider whether you should bluff with the hand in front of you.
The sixth lesson is to bet bigger when you have a polarized range.
The mathematics are unambiguous: every increase in bet size increases the expected value of the polarized range.
Players often anchor to conventional sizes out of habit or risk aversion, but on turns and rivers where ranges are genuinely polarized, pot-sized bets and overbets are frequently correct.
Before closing, it is important to acknowledge the limitations of this framework.
The Clairvoyance Game models an extreme case: a perfectly polarized range against a perfectly condensed range with no overlap whatsoever.
Real poker is messier.
Before the river, even the weakest hands retain some equity and rarely have zero percent chance of winning, while even the strongest hands rarely have one hundred percent equity.
The perfectly polarized model is therefore most directly applicable on the river.
Real ranges also tend to contain some mixture of hand strengths on both sides, not a clean separation between polarized and condensed.
And a player whose range is mostly condensed may still contain a few slow-played monsters, which can complicate the analysis of overbets.
Future chapters will address these complications and show how to adapt the principles from this chapter to more realistic situations.

But the foundation we have built here — understanding why polarized ranges benefit from large bets, why condensed ranges should check and call, what drives the optimal bluffing frequency, and what drives the optimal calling frequency — is the bedrock on which all of the more advanced analysis rests.
Master this, and the rest of poker strategy becomes far more navigable.

sections.title: Overview & Objectives
lines: Chapter three, section one. Overview and objectives.

In the previous chapter, we explored what happens when the two players have fundamentally different kinds of ranges — one polarized, one condensed — and we found that the polarized player has a built-in structural advantage that the condensed player simply cannot overcome.
That naturally raises a question that every ambitious poker player should be asking: if having a polarized range is so valuable, how do I get one?

The answer is both elegant and a little uncomfortable, because getting a polarized range is something you do to yourself through deliberate decisions — and the very act of creating a polarized range also creates a condensed range in your own hands.
Here is why: suppose you raised before the flop and the big blind called, and now she has checked to you on the flop.
If you decide to bet only your strong hands and your weakest bluff candidates — the two ends of the spectrum — then what are you doing with your middle-strength hands?
You are checking them, which means your checking range becomes concentrated with those in-between holdings.
Your range for arriving at the flop is not simply divided into great hands and terrible hands; the flop itself scrambles things significantly.
A hand like pocket kings is outstanding before the flop, but the moment an ace lands on the board, it shrinks to medium-strength territory almost instantly.
Ace-king, one of the most powerful starting hands, becomes nothing more than a mediocre holding when it completely misses the flop.
The natural consequence is that in order to bet a polarized range — strong hands and bluffs — you must deliberately place your medium-strength hands into a checking range.
And because you typically have more weak hands than you need for bluffing, some of those go into the checking range as well.
This is not a tragic accident; it is actually a rational choice driven by incentives.

Betting your strong hands is often more profitable than checking them, even knowing that doing so will make your checking range more condensed and therefore more vulnerable to aggressive action from your opponent.
Similarly, checking your medium-strength hands is often the highest-EV play even though it exposes those hands to pressure from polarized bets.
In chapter three, we are going to zoom in on precisely this dynamic by looking at games where both players start with exactly the same range.
This is called the Reciprocal Ranges framework, and it is enormously instructive because any advantage one player earns cannot come from range composition — it must come from something else entirely.
First, we will examine a Half-Street version of the game, where only one player, Ivan, gets to bet while Opal is forced to check.
This mirrors the structural logic of the Clairvoyance Game but with the important twist that either player might hold any card.
Then we will open things up into a Full-Street game where both players have the option to bet, which lets us study the challenges of playing from out of position with reciprocal ranges.
By the time you finish this chapter, you should be able to do three things.
First, you should be able to take any range and mentally divide it into a polarized portion and a condensed portion.
Second, you should understand how to use both aggressive and passive actions — betting and checking — to create difficult decisions for your opponent.

Third, and perhaps most importantly, you should be able to articulate in precise terms why position has strategic value — not just the vague intuition that acting last is nice, but a rigorous understanding of what it actually enables you to do.

sections.title: Scenario: Reciprocal Ranges, Half-Street Game
lines: Chapter three, section two. Scenario: Reciprocal Ranges, Half-Street Game.
Let us set up the game carefully so that every number we encounter later makes sense.
Both players ante one dollar each, creating a starting pot of two dollars.
They are each dealt one card from a three-card deck containing exactly one ace, one king, and one queen.
Here is the critical difference from the Clairvoyance Game: either player could receive any of those three cards.
If Opal is dealt the ace, she knows Ivan's range is king or queen, but she does not know which one he holds.
If Ivan is holding a queen, he knows Opal has either an ace or a king, but again, not which.
The highest card wins at showdown, ace beats king beats queen, and there are no draws or community cards.
In the half-street version, Opal must check, and Ivan may either bet one dollar or check behind.
If Ivan bets, Opal can call or fold, but she cannot raise.
The first question the book poses is deceptively simple: what is Opal's equity in this game?
The answer is fifty percent, because the ranges are identical — each player has an equal probability of receiving any of the three cards, so the raw chance of having the better hand is a coin flip.
But equity and expected value are not the same thing, and that distinction matters enormously here.
The second question asks which player has higher EV, and the answer is Ivan, even though his equity is identical to Opal's.
The reason is asymmetric betting access.
Ivan will never have to face a bet when he holds a king, which means his king never has to make a difficult decision about calling against a polarized range.
But Ivan can value bet his ace and, if he chooses, bluff with his queen — both of those are profitable opportunities that Opal simply does not have.

Now here is where the analysis gets genuinely interesting: the third question asks whether Ivan's bluffing frequency with a queen should be higher, lower, or identical to what it was in the Clairvoyance Game.
The answer, perhaps surprisingly, is the same — and working through why teaches you something deep about bluffing.
In the Clairvoyance Game, Ivan knew Opal had a king, so he knew bluffing with a queen would put her in a difficult spot.
In the Reciprocal Ranges Game, Opal might have an ace and snap-call trivially, which seems like it should discourage bluffing.
But the book asks a sharper question: not whether Opal could have the nuts, but how often she will have the nuts.
When Ivan holds a queen, Opal's range is split evenly between aces and kings — exactly one of each.
Now remember the calling frequency math: with a one-dollar bet into a two-dollar pot, the pot is growing to three dollars, so Opal needs to call enough to make bluffing break-even.
The formula for the minimum calling frequency to neutralize a bluff is one minus the fraction bet over pot — specifically, one minus one divided by three, which gives two-thirds.
Opal needs to call two-thirds of her range to make Ivan indifferent to bluffing.
If Opal's strategy were to call only with aces and fold all kings, she would call half the time — only three hands out of six if we think in sixths — which is less than two-thirds.
That means bluffing would be profitable for Ivan if Opal took that approach, because she is not calling often enough to make his bluffs break-even.

There is a guaranteed profitable strategy buried in here: either Opal folds all her kings, in which case Ivan's bluffs are immediately profitable, or she calls with some kings, in which case Ivan's ace gets paid off more often.

The purpose of bluffing is specifically to make the bluff-catchers — the medium-strength hands, meaning the kings in Opal's range — face an unprofitable decision, and that logic does not change just because some of Opal's range consists of aces.
When Ivan bets with a queen and gets called, it genuinely does not matter to him whether the call came from an ace or a king — he loses the same amount either way.
So the bluffing frequency stays the same because the thing being targeted — the decision difficulty for the king — is governed by the same bet size and pot size as before.
The fourth question asks about Opal's calling frequency with a king, and here the answer does change: she calls less often with a king than she did in the Clairvoyance Game.
Let us walk through the arithmetic.
Opal still needs to call two-thirds of her range — four sixths if we keep the fractions unsimplified — to make Ivan indifferent to bluffing.
But now her range, when Ivan has a queen, includes both aces and kings in equal proportion.
The aces are trivial calls; Opal will always call with an ace, and aces represent half her range, which is three sixths.
That means aces alone get her most of the way to her required calling frequency of four sixths.

To reach four sixths total, she needs only one additional sixth of her range to call, and since kings are half her range — three sixths — she needs to call with one third of her kings to supply that final sixth.
So Opal calls with one hundred percent of her aces and one third of her kings, compared to the Clairvoyance Game where she had to call with two thirds of her kings.
The presence of strong hands in Opal's range — the aces — absorbs much of the calling burden, letting the kings off the hook somewhat.
The fifth question is a trap that even experienced players fall into: if the bet size increases from one dollar to two dollars, which player benefits?
The answer is Opal, and the book admits this was deeply counter-intuitive even to the author at first.
To see why, start with the one-dollar game and compute Ivan's overall expected value.
When Ivan holds a queen, his equilibrium EV is zero dollars — either he checks and loses to Opal's better card, or he bluffs and Opal calls at a frequency that makes his bluff exactly break-even.

When Ivan holds a king, his EV is one dollar: he never bets, and at showdown he wins the two-dollar pot half the time when Opal has a queen and loses it the other half when Opal has an ace, averaging one dollar back against his one-dollar ante.
When Ivan holds an ace, the calculation requires a bit more work.
With an ace in hand, Opal's range consists of a king and a queen.
The queen will never call a bet.
The king, as we just established, calls one third of the time.

So when Ivan bets his ace, he wins the two-dollar pot five sixths of the time — when Opal has a queen and always folds, which is half the time, plus when she has a king and folds two thirds of that, which is one third of the time, totaling five sixths — and he wins a three-dollar pot one sixth of the time when she calls with a king.

That works out to two dollars times five sixths plus three dollars times one sixth, which equals ten sixths plus three sixths, which is thirteen sixths, or approximately two dollars and seventeen cents.

Averaging Ivan's three hand EVs together — zero for the queen, one dollar for the king, and two dollars seventeen cents for the ace — gives a total of three dollars and seventeen cents divided by three, which is about one dollar and six cents.
Since Ivan put in one dollar as his ante, his profit is roughly six cents, or about a six percent return on investment.
Now let us see what happens at a two-dollar bet size.
The calling frequency formula changes: one minus two divided by four equals one half.
Opal needs to call only half her range to neutralize a bluff.
But here is the problem for Ivan: when he holds a queen, Opal's range is exactly half aces and half kings, meaning aces alone — which are trivial calls — already constitute half her range.
Opal can neutralize Ivan's bluffing entirely just by calling with her aces and folding every king.
She never has to put kings in a difficult spot at all.

The reason to bluff is to generate tough decisions for the medium-strength hands, but a larger bet means those medium-strength hands never have to feel any heat because the strong hands absorb all the required calling.
Since Ivan cannot profitably bluff with a two-dollar bet, he has no incentive to risk two dollars unless he has the nuts.
And Opal, knowing this, can simply fold everything except an ace against any bet.
