More often, the change is emotional and temporary — a player who has been caught bluffing several times in a row pulls back instinctively, even if he has not analyzed why.
Both kinds of changes affect the value of your current exploitative strategy and how long it will remain valid.
The book's practical philosophy here is a bird in the hand is worth two in the bush.
When you identify a profitable exploit, take it now.
You cannot know how long the opportunity will exist, and future opportunities to find yourself in the same spot — right hand, right opponent, right street — may be scarce.
Even at a nine-handed table where you are bluffing a specific player relentlessly on the river, that specific situation might arise only once or twice an hour.
Over a typical session, it could take a very long time before he even accumulates enough data to recognize a pattern, and by then the table might have broken or one of you might have left.
There is also a deeper psychological reason to take the bird now.
Most poker mistakes are rooted in emotion and habit rather than strategic misunderstanding.
A player who calls too often does so because he loves action, or hates getting bluffed, or is just curious about what you have.
Those psychological tendencies do not vanish the moment he realizes he has been losing money.
He might tighten up briefly, but he will almost certainly drift back toward his natural style when the pain fades — which it does, faster than you might expect.
This also cuts in the other direction in an interesting way.
Some exploits are essentially invisible.
If you are checking your Queens to an over-loose opponent who then checks back and wins, what does he conclude?
At equilibrium, checking a Queen is actually the more common play — you only bet it one-third of the time.
So even if your exploitative strategy were to never bluff with it at all, he would need to see that hand go to showdown quite a few times before he began to suspect something.
Other exploits are more visible.

If you bluff with a King and get called down, a thoughtful opponent will recognize that as an unusual play — it deviates significantly enough from any standard strategy that it is the kind of thing he might remember and adjust to.
Importantly, even if your opponent does change his strategy in response to your exploit, that is not necessarily bad for you.
A player who starts out too tight and folds too often might, after watching you bluff repeatedly, overcorrect and start calling far more than he should.
At that point, the exploit that was profitable yesterday — bluffing — becomes costly today, but a new exploit — value betting thin — opens up.
The players who profit most from table dynamics are the ones who stay alert to these shifts.
Pay attention not just to cards and betting patterns but to emotion: the player who sighs and says he is sick of getting bluffed is advertising an upcoming strategy change.
The player who mutters about making bad calls is likely to tighten up.

Every hand, at every decision point, you should be asking yourself: given everything I know about this player — his tendencies, his history at this table, and his current emotional state — how do I expect him to play right now?
The answer to that question tells you how to exploit him this time, even if the answer will be different next time.

sections.title: Test Yourself
lines: Chapter five, section five. Test yourself.
For all of the following problems, assume you are playing no-limit hold 'em at a nine-handed table with two-dollar and five-dollar blinds and five-hundred-dollar effective stacks.
Problem one.
You are first to act before the flop and raise to fifteen dollars with the Ace of diamonds and Jack of diamonds.
The big blind calls.
Your read on this player is that he is a typical splashy recreational player: he gets involved in too many pots before the flop but becomes tight and passive when large bets are required.
The flop comes Ace of hearts, Nine of spades, Six of spades, and the big blind checks.
Do you bet or check?
The answer is to bet.
Here is the reasoning.

At equilibrium, you would likely be roughly indifferent in this spot — betting your top pair with a good kicker is tempting because you want value, but checking is also reasonable because an Ace-high board hits many opponent ranges and betting exposes you to a check-raise from a balanced, polarized range that could put you in a genuinely difficult position.
For a hand as strong as Ace-Jack but not strong enough to comfortably withstand a well-constructed check-raise, the equilibrium treatment might be a mix.
But that indifference disappears as soon as you factor in your read.

A splashy recreational player calls too many hands before the flop, which means his range connecting with this Ace-high board is loaded with dominated Aces — Ace-rag combinations he would not have called with at equilibrium — and draws he picked up with weak suited or connected hands.
Both of those categories are exactly what you want to extract value from with Ace-Jack.
Critically, your read also says this player is tight and passive when it comes to putting in big bets.
That means the thing that makes betting scary at equilibrium — the check-raise — is essentially off the table.
One of the main reasons you might check a strong but not unbeatable hand on a dangerous board is the risk of getting raised off your equity by a polarized range.
Against a player who never check-raises, that fear evaporates.
Betting is strictly the best play.
Problem two.
You bet twenty dollars with your Ace-Jack and the big blind calls.
The turn is the Eight of spades, completing a possible flush on a board of Ace, Nine, Six, Eight, all with three spades.
The big blind checks.
Do you bet or check?
This is a legitimately close decision even with your reads in hand.
You still hold top pair with a good kicker, and there are hands in his range that you either want to charge for their draws or fold out of their equity.
But the Eight of spades is a threatening card.
Many hands that call your flop bet could now have a flush, and several others have picked up additional straight draws.
Against any player who checks, there is a real possibility that you are now behind and drawing very thin.

The more confident you are that your read holds on the turn — specifically that this player will not raise unless he has a hand that crushes you — the more comfortable you can be with betting and folding if raised.
Checking is also perfectly fine here.

The key takeaway for the four-step process is that reads do not always produce a definitive answer; sometimes they help you understand why a decision is genuinely close and that checking is defensible rather than exploitable.
Problem three.
You check the turn, and the river comes the Three of diamonds, making the final board Ace of hearts, Nine of spades, Six of spades, Eight of spades, Three of diamonds.
The big blind bets sixty dollars, just under the size of the pot.
What is your play?
Fold.
The first step in working through this spot is to recognize what your hand actually is at this point.
Ace-Jack on a four-spade board where you do not hold a spade is a bluff-catcher.
Your read told you that this player does not make big value bets with worse Aces.
That means when he fires sixty dollars into a pot on the river, he either has a hand that beats you — a flush, a straight, two pair or better — or he is bluffing.
The question is not whether he could be bluffing; he certainly could be.
The question is whether he bluffs at a high enough frequency relative to his value hands to make calling profitable.

As we established in our game theory work, a bluff-catcher facing a bet from a polarized range is roughly indifferent at equilibrium — if your opponent bluffs at exactly the right frequency, calling and folding produce the same EV.
So the relevant question here is not just can he be bluffing but rather does he bluff out of proportion to the number of value hands he can hold.
This board has created a large number of possible value hands for the big blind — any spade gave him a flush, any seven gave him a straight, and he could have two pair multiple ways.
Getting to a point where his bluffing frequency is high enough relative to that value density to make your call profitable would require him to be bluffing a very large fraction of his range.
And your read specifically describes him as passive when big bets are involved.
A tight, passive player who is not inclined to bluff big on the river is almost certainly not bluffing here at a frequency that justifies your call.
While you might be roughly indifferent at equilibrium, this read pushes you firmly toward folding.
Problem four.
You are first to act before the flop and raise to fifteen dollars with the King of spades and Jack of spades.
The big blind calls.
Your read is that this player is very loose.
The flop comes Ace of hearts, Nine of spades, Six of spades.
The big blind checks.
Do you bet or check?
Bet.
At first glance, bluffing into a very loose opponent on an Ace-high board might seem like a poor choice — loose players call too much, so your bluffs will face more resistance.
But look carefully at what you are holding.
King-Jack of spades on Ace-Nine-Six with two spades is not really a pure bluff.
You have two overcards that could improve to strong hands, and you have a flush draw that will frequently get there by the river.
Against a loose player's calling range, you are ahead of a significant portion of the hands he might call with.
A hand like Queen of hearts, Eight of hearts has equity against you, but you would not be unhappy at all folding it out now.
Even though a loose opponent is not the ideal target for stone-cold bluffs, your hand here has enough raw value and equity that betting is correct.
If you are raised, you have a clear fold, and if you are called, you have real outs.
Problem five.
You are first to act before the flop and raise to fifteen dollars with the Jack of hearts and Ten of hearts.
The big blind calls.
Your read is that this player is very tight and straightforward both before and after the flop — he is afraid to slow-play and never bluffs, and he always assumes that his opponents hit the board.
The flop comes Ace of hearts, Nine of spades, Six of spades.
The big blind checks.
Do you bet or check?
Check, even though betting would likely be profitable.
Here is why checking is the most profitable way to exploit this particular opponent.
Tight players have strong pre-flop ranges — they fold many of the weak hands before the flop that a splashy player would call with.
That means when they do contest pots, their hands connect with flops more reliably.
A tight player on this Ace-high board has either hit an Ace, picked up a draw, or genuinely missed.
If he has hit an Ace or has a strong draw, your flop bet is not folding him out anyway, so the bet buys you nothing and costs you chips.
If he has completely missed, he was going to fold on any street.
The insight comes from the second half of your read: this player is afraid to bluff.
If you check to him and he checks back, that gives you very strong information that he does not have a hand he plans to fight with.
On the turn, you can then make the same bluff you would have made on the flop, but now you have confirmation that he plans to fold, which makes the bluff much more likely to succeed.
If you check to him and he bets, you can fold confidently — this player does not bluff, so his bet is a real hand, and you save the chips you would have lost by leading the flop into him.
There is a nuance about board texture worth noting here.

On boards where the turn card could significantly change how the opponent feels about his hand — giving him a new pair, completing a draw, or threatening his holding — it can be better to bluff immediately rather than wait.
On this board, though, the dynamics are clear: he either connected with the Ace or he did not, and a turn card is unlikely to change that picture dramatically.
Gathering the free information by checking is the highest-EV play.
Problem six.
You are first to act before the flop and raise to fifteen dollars with the Nine of diamonds and Nine of hearts.
The big blind calls.
Your read is that this player is very tight and straightforward — afraid to slow-play, never bluffs, and always assumes his opponents hit the board.
The flop comes Ace of hearts, Nine of spades, Six of spades.
The big blind checks.
Do you bet or check?
Bet.
This hand looks superficially similar to the previous one — same opponent read, same board — but the hand itself changes everything.
You have just flopped a set of Nines.
This is not a bluffing situation at all; it is a value situation, and that completely alters the calculus.
Against a tight, straightforward opponent who will not slow-play, your set of Nines is almost certainly the best hand right now.
If he has an Ace, he is going to give you action — and you want that action right now, before the board gets more threatening and he starts second-guessing his Ace.
A fourth spade, a straight-completing Ten or Seven, or any other scary card could cause even a tight player to pump the brakes with top pair.
Getting the money in on the flop when you are a big favorite is correct.
If he has a draw, you want to charge him for it.
If he has nothing, he is not bluffing at you on the turn anyway, so checking does not create additional profit — he simply will not put chips in without a reason to.
The lesson from problems five and six side by side is that your read about an opponent is only half the equation.
The other half is what you are holding.
The same read — tight, passive, non-bluffing player — produces completely different optimal strategies depending on whether you have a bluffing candidate or a strong value hand.
Systematically working through the four-step process for each hand type is what keeps you from misapplying a read that is otherwise accurate.

sections.title: Conclusion
lines: Chapter five, section six. Conclusion.
Let us pull together everything this chapter has covered and leave you with a clear picture of how to carry these ideas forward.

At its core, the message of chapter five is this: choosing to play exploitatively is a calculated gamble, and like any gamble in poker, it should be made deliberately, with clear reasoning, and with a realistic accounting of what happens if you are wrong.
Any time you deviate from equilibrium to exploit a specific tendency, you open a window in your own strategy.
If your read is correct, that window is invisible — your opponent cannot take advantage of it because he is not playing optimally.

But if your read is wrong — if he anticipated your adjustment, or if you simply misjudged — then that window becomes a vulnerability, and you will have a lower expected value than you would have with the equilibrium strategy you abandoned.
The four-step process is your tool for keeping that gamble as disciplined and well-reasoned as possible.
Envision the equilibrium first, so you know what you are deviating from and why.
Make a read that is specific enough to actually drive a decision, not just a vague impression.
Identify all the exploits available, not just the obvious one, because the non-obvious ones are often where the most money lives.
And calibrate how far to deviate based on both the size of the opponent's mistake and your confidence that he is actually making it.
The scenarios from the Reciprocal Ranges Game illustrated several key lessons that you will encounter repeatedly in real play.
First, small deviations from equilibrium can warrant dramatic changes to your strategy when you are holding a hand that is near indifference.
Ivan folding only slightly more than the equilibrium frequency of Kings was enough to make bluffing every Queen strictly correct, even though the EV difference was modest.

That knife's-edge property of mixed strategies — where indifference can flip to pure preference with almost no change in the opponent's behavior — is one of the most counterintuitive but important ideas in this book.
Second, not all hands are equally well-suited to any given exploit.
Hands that sit at the indifference boundary are the natural first candidates for exploitative adjustment.
Hands that have dominant pure strategies at equilibrium — like the King in the Reciprocal Ranges Game — require dramatically larger opponent mistakes before it becomes correct to deviate.
Knowing the difference, and being able to quickly identify which category your hand falls into, is a skill that improves every exploitative decision you make.
Third, the most obvious exploit is rarely the only exploit.
When an opponent folds too much, bluffing more is the first thing anyone thinks of.
But checking strong hands to induce action, adjusting your calling strategy on earlier streets, and choosing which specific hands to bluff with are all part of the full exploitative response.
Step three of the process — identify the exploits — exists precisely to force you to look beyond the obvious.
Fourth, the question in problem four taught a lesson about the nature of equilibrium itself.
When Opal always bet her Aces, it appeared she was telegraphing her hand.
But as long as her bluffing and bluff-catching frequencies remained in balance, Ivan had no profitable response.
Predictability alone does not create exploitability — only an uncompensated deviation from equilibrium does.

This is a reminder that the goal of studying game theory is not to become robotic or balanced at all costs; it is to understand the structure of equilibrium well enough to know when a deviation is exploitable and when it is not.
The discussion of game flow and table dynamics added an important temporal dimension.
Exploitative strategies are not set-and-forget.
Your opponents change, sometimes deliberately and sometimes emotionally, and your reads and strategies need to change with them.
Taking an exploit when you see it is usually the right call — the opportunity may not recur soon, and players rarely change their underlying tendencies as permanently as they intend to.
But staying alert to shifts in behavior — verbal tells, body language, changes in betting patterns after a painful loss — lets you anticipate the next exploit before it announces itself.

The average poker player plays in a highly exploitable way, not because he has made a deliberate strategic choice, but because he has learned a style of play that feels comfortable or standard and has never interrogated it deeply.
He does not know that his strategy is exploitable because he does not have a clear picture of what the equilibrium looks like in the spots he faces.
That is the gap that studying game theory fills.
You are not studying game theory to play like a computer — to balance every range and mix every strategy with mathematical precision.

You are studying it to develop a clear enough mental model of optimal play that you can see where your opponents are straying from it, understand why those deviations cost them money, and craft targeted responses that capture that value.

Armed with the four-step process and the intuitions developed in this chapter, you should be able to approach any exploitative decision with more precision, more creativity, and more confidence than ever before.

The rest of this book will continue building on these foundations, giving you more situations to reason through and more tools to refine your exploitative instincts in the specific contexts where poker is actually played.

sections.title: Overview & Objectives
lines: Chapter six, section one. Overview and objectives.
Welcome back to our deep dive into game-theory-optimal poker strategy.

In the previous chapter we worked through the Reciprocal Ranges Game, and while that toy game taught us the fundamentals of polarized betting and bluff-catching, it had a significant limitation: the decisions were almost too obvious.
Think about it — folding an Ace or calling with a Queen are the kinds of catastrophic errors that even beginners would rarely make.

That means the game, while instructive, left something important on the table: it didn't really help us understand what happens when opponents make realistic, believable mistakes, and how we ought to profit from those mistakes.

There's also a widespread misconception in poker circles that game-theory-optimal play is somehow a defensive posture that caps your upside — that equilibrium strategies only ever break even and can't exploit errors.
This chapter is going to dismantle that misconception directly and decisively.
To do that, we're going to work through a meaningfully more complex toy game called the Ace-to-Five Game.

In this game, the deck is larger, the hand rankings cover a wider spectrum, and the boundary between a value bet and a bluff becomes genuinely unclear — which is exactly what makes it a better model of real poker.
With more cards in play, there will be more close decisions, more plausible errors, and more opportunities to profit from an opponent who doesn't find the right lines.

We'll start by solving for the equilibrium strategies across every decision point — what each player should bet, check, call, and fold — and then we'll pivot to the question of exploitation: how do you adjust when your opponent is straying from equilibrium?
By the time you finish this chapter, you should have developed four concrete skills.
First, you'll understand the strategic role that different parts of a range play, from the very strongest hands down to the pure air at the bottom.
Second, you'll know how to construct ranges that confront your opponents with genuinely difficult decisions, the kind where a reasonable player could easily go wrong.
Third, you'll be able to incorporate information about a specific opponent's tendencies into decisions about when to bluff, when to bluff-catch, and when to go thin with a value bet.

Fourth, you'll have a framework for identifying the right targets for your bets, your checks, and your calls — thinking not just about what you hold, but about which specific hands in your opponent's range your action is designed to affect.
These are not abstract concepts — they translate directly to decisions at the table, and we'll see exactly how in the real-world applications section.
Let's get into it.

sections.title: Scenario: The Ace-to-Five Game
lines: Chapter six, section two. Scenario: The Ace-to-Five Game.
Here is the setup.
Two players, Opal and Ivan, each receive one card from a ten-card deck containing exactly one card of each rank from Five up through Ace.
Both players post a one-dollar ante, creating a two-dollar pot before any betting begins.
The game allows only a single round of betting, and the only permitted bet size is one dollar.
Opal acts first: she can either bet one dollar or check.
If she checks, Ivan may then bet one dollar or check behind.
There is no raising — whichever player faces a bet can only call or fold.
If neither player folds, the hand goes to showdown and the highest card wins.
That's the whole game, but don't be fooled by its simplicity — the equilibrium strategies that emerge are quite intricate, and unpacking them reveals a tremendous amount about how real poker works.
Let's walk through the equilibrium starting with the most fundamental question Ivan faces: when Opal bets, which hands should he call?
At equilibrium, Ivan calls one hundred percent of the time with Ace, King, Queen, and Jack.
Below that, his strategy becomes mixed: he calls with a Ten eighty-three percent of the time, with a Nine fifty-nine percent, with an Eight forty-two percent, and with a Seven sixteen percent.
He always folds a Six and always folds a Five.
The first question to address is which of these calls are genuinely profitable, which are money-losing, and which are neutral.
The Ace, King, Queen, and Jack are all automatic calls — one hundred percent, no mixing — and that alone signals that these are positive expected value calls.
The interesting case is the Jack, because Opal can value bet a Jack herself, meaning the Jack does not beat the entire value portion of Opal's betting range.

However, when Ivan holds a Jack, he knows with certainty that Opal cannot also hold a Jack, so Opal's betting range is slightly imbalanced in ways that are favorable to Ivan — it is tilted a bit more toward bluffs relative to what Ivan's range-wide calling frequency would otherwise assume.
That blocking effect is enough to make calling with a Jack a positive expected value play.

The Six is the mirror image of the Jack: a Six can beat some of Opal's bluffs, but it also blocks one of Opal's potential bluffing hands, meaning that when Ivan holds a Six, Opal is actually less likely to be bluffing than her overall bluffing frequency would suggest.
That makes calling with a Six a money-losing proposition for Ivan, even though it looks like it could beat some of Opal's range.
The Tens, Nines, Eights, and Sevens are all equivalent to each other in strategic terms — these are the pure bluff-catchers, the hands that lose to every value bet but beat every bluff.
At equilibrium, Opal's betting range is polarized, meaning she bets strong hands for value and weak hands as bluffs, with nothing in the middle.
That structural fact is what allows Ivan's Seven to function just as well as his Ten as a bluff-catcher, because a Seven beats all the same bluffs that a Ten does.
Ivan can mix his calls any way he likes across the Seven-through-Ten group, as long as the total calling frequency across those four hands reaches the right level.
What is that level?
Ivan needs to call with two thirds of his possible holdings when facing a bet.
This is the same math we saw in earlier games: Opal is risking one dollar to win a two-dollar pot, so Ivan must call at a frequency that denies her a positive expected value bluff.
Ivan starts with ten cards in the deck, but since Opal holds one of them, Ivan effectively has nine possible holdings.
Two thirds of nine is six, so Ivan must call with the equivalent of six out of nine hands.
Four of those — Ace, King, Queen, and Jack — are unambiguous calls, giving him four out of nine right away.
He needs the equivalent of two more calls from the Seven-through-Ten group.

Notice something elegant here: in the table given for Ivan's equilibrium strategy, if you add up all four of the mixed calling frequencies for Ten, Nine, Eight, and Seven — eighty-three percent plus fifty-nine percent plus forty-two percent plus sixteen percent — those sum to two hundred percent, which is exactly the same as calling two of those hands one hundred percent of the time and folding the other two entirely.
That confirms the math: Ivan is calling with exactly two equivalent hands worth of bluff-catching from that group.
Now let's look at Opal's betting strategy, because this is where things get really interesting.
At equilibrium Opal's betting range is indeed polarized: she bets the Ace, King, Queen, and Jack for value, and she bets the Six and Five as bluffs.
She never bets a Ten, Nine, Eight, or Seven.
Her exact betting frequencies are: Ace at eighty-one percent, King at seventy-three percent, Queen at sixty-three percent, and Jack at fifty-seven percent.
For bluffs: Six at thirty-six percent, Five at fifty-six percent.
The fact that she is mixing — not betting any of these at one hundred percent — means she is indifferent between betting and checking with each of them.
So why is a Jack in the value betting portion of this range?
After all, a Jack will lose to an Ace, King, or Queen if called, and it only beats the Tens, Nines, Eights, and Sevens that might call it.
The key insight is this: when Opal holds a Jack, and Ivan holds something better, she is already going to lose a bet one way or another.
She cannot simply check and avoid that loss, because Ivan will bet his stronger hand into her, and Opal should call.
Either way, a bet goes into the pot and Opal loses it.

Given that reality, the right question is not whether to put in a bet — that's effectively already decided — but rather how to make that unavoidable bet go in under the most favorable conditions possible.
When Opal bets her Jack, she may get called by a Ten, Nine, Eight, or Seven, and she beats all of those.
When she checks her Jack and faces a bet, she gets value from the Tens, Sixes, and Fives in Ivan's range.
At equilibrium, both of these options produce the same expected value, which is why Opal mixes between them.
This is one of the clearest illustrations in the book of why checking to control the pot — what players sometimes call pot control — is not actually a free option for the player out of position.
Now let's move to Ivan's betting strategy when Opal checks.
At equilibrium, Ivan bets one hundred percent of the time with Ace, King, Queen, and Jack.
He bets fifty percent of the time with a Ten, and he also bets fifty percent of the time with a Six.
He always bets a Five — one hundred percent — and never bets a Nine, Eight, or Seven.
One of the most striking features here is that Ivan can value bet a Ten, while Opal never bets a Ten.
Why does Ivan get this privilege?
It comes down to the informational advantage of acting second.
When Opal checks, she reveals something about her range.

She doesn't reveal everything, because she does check some strong hands as traps, but she has strong incentive to bet a polarized range, meaning her checking range is genuinely weaker on average than Ivan's overall range, which has not yet revealed anything.

Because Opal's checking range contains more medium and weak hands than Ivan's full starting range, Ivan is less likely to run into a better hand when he bets a Ten, which shifts the expected value of that bet enough to make it competitive with checking.
Opal keeps Ivan indifferent to value betting a Ten precisely by checking a certain number of strong hands — her Jacks, Queens, Kings, and Aces at their mixed frequencies.
If she never checked a strong hand, Ivan would strictly prefer to bet a Ten every time.
Her checking frequency with strong hands is calibrated to just barely make Ivan indifferent.
Now for the bluffing asymmetry, which is one of the most subtle and important results in this chapter.
Ivan always bluffs with a Five, but only bluffs with a Six fifty percent of the time.
This is unusual: at equilibrium, Ivan's Five is not a break-even bluff — it is actually a positive expected value bluff.
How can a bluff be strictly profitable at equilibrium?

The answer lies in the fact that Ivan has two possible bluffing candidates — Five and Six — and checking with a Six is slightly better than checking with a Five, because a Six can occasionally win at showdown against a Five, while a Five can never win.
Opal cannot simultaneously make Ivan indifferent to bluffing with both his Five and his Six, because those two hands have different values when checked.

Opal could call at a higher frequency that would make the Five a break-even bluff, but doing so would require either checking more strong hands — costing her value — or calling with more weak hands — making Ivan's value bets more profitable.
The cost of that adjustment is too high, so Opal instead calibrates her check-calling frequency to make the Six indifferent to bluffing, which leaves the Five as a perpetually profitable bluff.
This is one of the position advantages Ivan enjoys: his worst hands generate small but guaranteed profits, something Opal cannot achieve from out of position.
Now let's look at Opal's check-calling strategy when Ivan bets after she checks.
Opal always calls with a Ten or better — including her trapped Aces, Kings, Queens, and Jacks at their residual checking frequencies.
She also calls with a Nine forty-nine percent of the time, an Eight forty-two percent, and a Seven forty percent.
She never calls with a Six or Five.

The Ten is worth focusing on: Ivan value bets his own Tens, which means that when Opal holds a Ten, she blocks a portion of Ivan's value range, making his overall betting range slightly more bluff-heavy relative to what it would look like without that blocking effect.
That makes calling with a Ten a positive expected value decision for Opal, not a break-even one, which is why she calls with it one hundred percent rather than mixing.
Opal's objective when constructing this check-calling range is to make Ivan indifferent to bluffing with a Six.
Notice she is not trying to make him indifferent to bluffing with a Five — that bluff is already profitable and there is nothing she can cost-effectively do about it.
Her target is the Six: call at a frequency that makes a Six a zero expected value bluff.

She also does not need to count the hands she folds that are worse than a Six — like Fives — as part of Ivan's free pass, because Ivan doesn't gain anything from betting a Six and having Opal fold a Five.
Those extra folds are irrelevant to the indifference calculation.
Let's summarize the equilibrium to crystallize all of this.
Opal bets a polarized range — Jacks through Aces for value, Fives and Sixes as bluffs — at mixed frequencies that leave her indifferent between betting and checking each of those hands.
The overall ratio of value bets to bluffs in her range is three to one, which is exactly right for a half-pot bet that offers the caller two-to-one odds.
Ivan, when facing that bet, calls with two thirds of his range — all his Jacks and better automatically, plus just enough of the Seven-through-Ten group to reach two thirds overall.

When Opal checks, Ivan bets a wider polarized range than Opal could achieve: Jacks through Aces always, Tens half the time, Fives always, and Sixes half the time, again in a three-to-one ratio of value to bluffs.
Opal, facing Ivan's bet, calls with her Tens and better automatically and calls with enough of her Sevens, Eights, and Nines to make Ivan's Six a break-even bluff.
Position is worth exactly twelve cents in this game: Opal's expected value is ninety-four cents, while Ivan's is one dollar and six cents.
The amounts at stake are small in this toy game, but the six-cent positional edge is real and persistent regardless of how well Opal plays — it is built into the structure of the game itself.

What makes the Ace-to-Five Game so valuable as a teaching tool is that the equilibrium is complex enough that real, non-trivial mistakes are easy to imagine, and as we'll see in the next section, those mistakes are expensive.

sections.title: Scenario: Exploitation in the Ace-to-Five Game
lines: Chapter six, section three. Scenario: Exploitation in the Ace-to-Five Game.
Now that we understand the equilibrium, we can turn to the more practically useful question: how do we exploit deviations from it?

The Ace-to-Five Game is the perfect vehicle for this because the equilibrium involves so many mixed strategies — hands where the player is genuinely indifferent — and those mixed strategies are exactly where real opponents are most likely to make mistakes.

We'll work through five different exploitative scenarios, applying what the book calls the Four-Step Exploitative Process: envision the equilibrium, make a read, identify the exploits, and determine the degree of deviation.
Let's start with the first scenario.
Suppose Ivan will only call when he holds a Jack or better — he folds everything else, including the Tens, Nines, Eights, and Sevens that he is supposed to call with some of the time at equilibrium.
The first step is to recognize what Ivan is doing wrong: he is folding too much.
At equilibrium, Ivan calls with six out of nine hands on average.
By restricting himself to Jacks and better — four hands — he is calling with only four out of nine, which is well below the two-thirds threshold that makes Opal indifferent to bluffing.
The exploitative response is clear in direction: Opal should bluff more and value bet less thinly.
Specifically, Opal should now bet all of her Fives and Sixes, and she should also bluff with hands she would never bluff with at equilibrium: Sevens, Eights, and Nines.
These hands have some showdown value, but when Ivan is folding far too much, the expected value of getting a fold with a Nine outweighs the expected value of checking it and winning at showdown.
Because Opal is bluffing more, her checking range becomes significantly stronger — it will contain more medium-strength hands than usual.
That means that when Opal checks and Ivan bets into her, Opal should fold some hands she would have called with at equilibrium.
Against Ivan's stronger-than-usual calling range, value betting a Jack no longer makes sense — Jacks become pure checks.

Aces, Kings, and Queens are still mixed between betting and checking, because even though the expected value of betting them has declined, the expected value of checking them has declined as well, and Opal remains indifferent.

After checking and facing a bet, Opal always calls with a Queen or better, but Jacks and Tens — which are pure calls at equilibrium — are now only called part of the time, because those hands are now much closer to the bottom of her checking range.
The bottom line here is instructive: Ivan's mistake, while it only involves hands that were indifferent at equilibrium, is collectively quite significant.
Those four hand categories — Tens, Nines, Eights, and Sevens — together constitute one third of Ivan's equilibrium calling range, and when he abandons them entirely, he is massively over-folding.
Opal's expected value climbs from ninety-four cents at equilibrium to approximately one dollar and ten cents when she exploits correctly.
That sixteen-cent improvement converts what was a slightly losing proposition for Opal into a winning one.
The chapter makes a pointed observation here: the players who complain most bitterly about tight, passive opponents are often themselves too tight and passive to exploit them.
Exploiting a player who folds too much requires bluffing — a lot of bluffing — and many players are emotionally or stylistically resistant to that kind of aggression.
If you want to actually profit from someone's mistakes, you have to be willing to make the adaptations those mistakes demand, even when those adaptations feel uncomfortable.
Now let's tighten the scenario further: suppose Ivan will only call with a Queen or better.
This is a bigger mistake than calling only with a Jack or better, and Opal's exploitative response follows the same logic but shifts the thresholds.
When Ivan required a Jack or better to call, the weakest hand Opal should still value bet was a Queen, and the strongest hand she should bluff was a Nine.
When Ivan requires a Queen or better to call, the weakest value bet shifts to a King, and the strongest bluff Opal should use shifts to a Ten.
After checking, Opal always calls with a Queen but is now only indifferent with a Jack — she never calls with a Ten, which she sometimes did against the player from the first scenario.
The general principle here is elegant: the magnitude of your opponent's deviation from equilibrium determines how far you should deviate yourself.
When folding mistakes are small, restrict bluffing to your ideal candidates — the Fives and Sixes.
As the mistakes grow larger, extend your bluffing range to progressively weaker value hands, starting at the very bottom and working up only as needed.

You never jump to bluffing with a diverse mix of hands at low frequencies — you bluff your weakest hands at high frequencies first, and only add stronger hands when you have exhausted the weaker ones.
Now let's flip the scenario and consider what happens when Ivan is the one making the mistake, specifically in the direction of insufficient bluffing.
Suppose Ivan will only bluff with a Five — his very worst hand — and never bluffs with a Six.
At equilibrium, Ivan bluffs with all his Fives and half his Sixes, so this is a relatively small deviation, but it still has consequences.
Ivan is not bluffing enough.
Opal's exploitative response has several components.
Most obviously, she should do less bluff-catching: if Ivan is not bluffing enough, the incentive to call with pure bluff-catchers goes down.
But here is the less obvious part: because Opal is checking more — folding to Ivan's bets rather than calling — checking is now a less attractive option for her strong hands.
So Opal should actually value bet more of her strong hands rather than checking them.
And because she is value betting more, she needs more bluffs to keep the ratio balanced, so her bluffing frequency goes up slightly as well.

The result against this type of opponent: Opal value bets one hundred percent of her Jacks and better, checks everything else with the intention of folding if Ivan bets, and bluffs her Fives and Sixes at somewhat higher frequencies than equilibrium, though not yet at one hundred percent for either.

This is a small deviation from equilibrium — Ivan's expected value only drops from one dollar and six cents to one dollar and two cents — but the structural changes to Opal's strategy are significant precisely because so many of her equilibrium decisions were right at the indifference threshold.
Of course, by choosing not to check-call at all, Opal is opening herself up to exploitation if Ivan ever decides to bluff with a Six.
That is the fundamental trade-off in exploitative play: you are betting on your read being correct, and if it is wrong, your opponent profits.
Now push the scenario further: Ivan will never bluff at all, not even with a Five.
This is a much larger mistake, and the optimal response is surprising.
You might expect Opal to simply keep doing more of what she was doing in the previous scenario — more value betting, less bluff-catching.
But the solver reveals something counterintuitive: Opal should stop betting her Jacks, and she should stop bluffing with Sixes.
Instead, she bets only Aces, Kings, Queens, and Fives — a smaller but still perfectly balanced betting range with a three-to-one ratio of value to bluffs.
Why does the Jack drop out when Ivan never bluffs?
And why does the Six drop out simultaneously?
Here is the logic: when Ivan never bluffs — never checks a Five — Opal's Six and Jack both gain showdown value that they previously lacked.
A Six can now beat a checked Five at showdown, which means checking a Six is no longer a purely wasted opportunity.
A Jack, similarly, no longer risks losing to a bluffed Five when Opal checks.
At equilibrium, part of what made betting a Jack attractive was that checking it meant sometimes losing to a Five that Ivan turned into a bluff.
Remove that risk, and checking a Jack becomes more appealing relative to betting.

Because the Six and the Jack are the most marginal hands in Opal's equilibrium betting range — the Jack is her weakest value bet and the Six is her break-even bluff — both of them tip over to checking when Ivan removes the bluffing threat.
The result is a tighter, cleaner betting range: Aces, Kings, Queens for value and Fives as bluffs, all at one hundred percent.

This mistakes costs Ivan significantly more than not bluffing with Sixes: his expected value drops all the way from one dollar and six cents to ninety cents, making him a losing player in a game he should have a built-in edge in.
The final scenario involves a mistake by Opal rather than Ivan.
Suppose Opal bets a straightforward range of Aces, Kings, Queens, and Fives — all at one hundred percent.
Notice that this is a balanced betting range — three value hands for every one bluff — so it might seem like a minor deviation.
However, Opal is now betting all of those hands rather than mixing, which means her checking range contains no Aces, Kings, or Queens.
That is a condensed checking range — she is never trapping with a powerhouse hand.
Ivan's response is to attack that condensed range with a more polarized betting strategy.

Specifically, Ivan should now bet his Tens one hundred percent of the time rather than fifty percent — because he knows Opal has no Queens or better hiding in her checking range, so a Ten faces a much safer landscape.
To maintain the three-to-one ratio with five value hands instead of four and a half, Ivan needs approximately one and two thirds bluffing hands rather than one and a half.
He fills the gap by increasing his bluffing frequency with a Six to two thirds — he still does not need to bluff with Sevens.
On the calling side, when facing Opal's bet, Ivan no longer automatically calls with a Jack, since Opal is no longer betting a Jack for value.
Jacks become bluff-catchers rather than blockers-with-a-call.
Sixes likewise no longer block any of Opal's bluffs — she has no Sixes in her betting range — so Sixes can become calls if needed to reach two thirds.
The broader lesson from this scenario is this: a balanced betting range is necessary but not sufficient.
Opal's betting range is properly constructed — the right ratio of value to bluffs — but her overall strategy is still exploitable because her checking range is wrong.
A well-constructed game plan requires the right betting range and the right checking range, with each one calibrated to keep the opponent indifferent in the appropriate places.
Get one right and leave the other wrong, and a good opponent will still find a profitable adjustment.

sections.title: Real World Applications
lines: Chapter six, section four. Real world applications.
Everything we have worked through in the Ace-to-Five Game has direct implications for how to play real poker, and this section draws out those connections explicitly.
There are two major themes: first, learning to target specific hands in your opponent's range, and second, learning to construct ranges that force your opponent into genuinely difficult decisions.
Let's start with targeting.
One of the most common errors that poker players make — especially out of position with medium-strength hands — is reasoning something like this: 'I'm not sure I'm ahead, so I'd better check.'
The problem with this thought process is not just that it is incomplete — it is that it is driven by fear rather than by strategic calculation.
It focuses on avoiding a loss rather than on maximizing expected value.

And critically, it doesn't resolve what to do after checking — if your opponent bets, you're back to making another fear-based decision: are you more afraid of losing to a better hand, or more afraid of being bluffed?
Consider what we learned about Opal holding a Jack in the Ace-to-Five Game.
At equilibrium, she is indifferent between betting and checking — both options produce the same expected value.
But in neither case is she acting out of fear or uncertainty.
When she bets, she has a specific target: the Tens, Nines, Eights, and Sevens in Ivan's range — the hands that will face a close call and might make the wrong decision.
When she checks, she has a different specific target: the Tens, Sixes, and Fives that Ivan might bet into her.
The reason to check a Jack is not that Ivan might have a better hand — after all, at equilibrium Opal even checks Aces sometimes.
The reason to check is to create a situation where Ivan has incentive to bet with hands that Opal can beat.
That is a completely different frame than fear-based reasoning, and it leads to completely different decisions.
Now notice something crucial: whether Opal bets an Ace or bets a Jack, she is targeting the same group of hands — the Tens, Nines, Eights, and Sevens in Ivan's range.
Those are the threshold hands, the ones where Ivan's decision is genuinely close.
The Aces, Kings, and Queens in Ivan's range are trivial — he calls automatically and there is nothing Opal can do to affect that outcome.
The Fives and Sixes in Ivan's range are also trivial — he folds automatically no matter what Opal holds.
The only hands Opal can actually influence with her bet are the ones right at the decision boundary.
This is a foundational principle: focus your strategic thinking on threshold hands, because those are the hands where your action actually changes the outcome.
Everything else is noise.
When playing exploitatively, this targeting framework becomes even more powerful.
Once you have identified which hands in your opponent's range are the relevant targets, you can think about what kind of mistake he is most likely to make with those specific hands.
If your opponent tends to pay off with bluff-catchers even when bet into, then betting becomes more profitable and you should value bet more aggressively.
If your opponent bluffs too frequently after you check, then checking and calling becomes the better line.

If your opponent is passive both ways — unwilling to bluff and unwilling to call with bluff-catchers — then checking and folding might actually be correct, because you'll often get to showdown for free and win with your medium-strength hand.
Now let's talk about the second major theme: constructing ranges that put your opponent to difficult decisions.
A single hand cannot do this on its own.
The difficulty of your opponent's decision depends not on the specific card you hold, but on the entire range of hands you might hold in that spot.
When Opal bets a Jack, Ivan has a difficult time with a Nine because of the possibility that Opal might be bluffing with a Five or Six.
When Opal checks, Ivan has a difficult time deciding whether to bet a Ten because Opal might be checking a Nine that pays him off, but she also might be trapping with a Jack or better.
Neither of these decisions would be difficult if Opal were a simpler, more predictable player who always bet her strong hands and never mixed.
The difficulty is produced by the range, not by any individual hand.
This has an important implication for how to plan your strategy before the action begins.
The book lays out a clear sequence of reasoning you should apply when constructing your betting range from any given position.
Start by determining how many of your strong hands you need to check, because you need a strong enough checking range to keep your opponent from profitably making thin value bets after you check.
The number of strong hands you check is calibrated to make your opponent indifferent to value betting his threshold hands — the Tens in the Ace-to-Five Game, the marginal top pairs in real hold 'em.
Once you have set aside the strong hands that belong in your checking range, the rest become available for value betting.
That gives you your value betting range.
Then look at the pot odds your bet will offer — how many bluffs you need relative to your value bets to keep opponents indifferent to calling with their bluff-catchers.
For a half-pot bet, the ratio is three value hands for every one bluff.

Fill your bluffing range starting from the very bottom of your hand spectrum — the absolute weakest hands you can hold — and work upward only if you have exhausted those hands and still need more bluffs.

This disciplined approach to range construction serves a dual purpose: it ensures you are not over-bluffing or under-bluffing, and it gives you the context to make the right decision with any individual hand you happen to hold.
Many players are reluctant to bet strong hands because they worry it will signal strength.

But if you are also betting weak hands as bluffs at the right frequency, your bet communicates nothing about the specific hand you hold — it only tells your opponent that you have a range that includes both strong hands and weak hands, which he already knew.
The strategic invisibility of individual hands within a properly constructed range is one of the most powerful concepts in this entire approach to poker.
There is a practical concession to reality here as well.
In a live game, you will rarely have the time or mental bandwidth to enumerate every combination of value bets and bluffs precisely.
What you can do is make a quick estimate: how weak is your current hand relative to the weakest hands you could hold in this situation?
Is it one of the very worst hands in your range, or will you sometimes have even weaker holdings?
If it is the worst or among the worst, it is a bluffing candidate.
Also ask yourself: roughly how wide is your value betting range in this spot?
A wider value betting range can support more bluffs, so if you are in a spot where you can bet many strong hands for value, you have more room to bluff with your weaker hands.
A narrow value betting range means you should restrict bluffing to only the absolute best bluffing candidates.
Thinking through this quickly before you act is far better than not thinking about it at all.
It will not produce perfectly balanced ranges, but it will prevent the large, easily exploitable mistakes — bluffing wildly or never bluffing — that cost players real money.
There is one more practical point worth dwelling on.
When you are playing exploitatively, this planning process actually gets easier, not harder, because you can simplify by taking certain considerations off the table.
If you know your opponent calls too much with bluff-catchers, you no longer need to bluff — the bluffing part of the calculation disappears.
If you know your opponent will not make thin value bets after you check, you do not need to trap with strong hands, so you can bet your entire strong range and simplify your value betting decision.

Exploitative play is not about abandoning the logic of game theory — it is about applying that logic with the added input of real information about a specific opponent, which allows you to simplify and tighten your decision making in ways that produce higher expected value.

sections.title: Test Yourself
lines: Chapter six, section five. Test yourself.
Now we get to apply everything from this chapter to real hold 'em situations.
All three problems assume a nine-handed no-limit hold 'em game with two-dollar and five-dollar blinds and five-hundred dollar effective stacks.
Let's work through each one carefully.
Problem one.
You open to fifteen dollars with King of hearts Ten of hearts on the button, and the big blind calls.
The flop comes Ten of diamonds, Six of spades, Two of clubs.
The big blind checks, you bet fifteen dollars, and she calls.
The turn is the Queen of clubs.
She checks, you bet thirty dollars, and she calls.
The river is the Two of diamonds.
She checks.
What do you do?
The answer is bet.
This is a value bet, and a fairly clear one when you think through it carefully.
The most common mistake here is to check back because second pair with second kicker feels too far from the nuts to bet.
That is exactly the kind of fear-based thinking that this chapter teaches us to move beyond.
Think about what your opponent's range looks like.
She has checked to you on every street and never raised.
Hands bigger than a Ten — pocket pairs like Jacks, Jacks with a high kicker, or a Queen — would typically re-raise before the flop or raise on the flop or turn when they are ahead.
Some of those hands exist in her range, but they are a minority.
What is much more common is that she holds hands like Queen-Jack, Jack-Nine, a small pair, a draw that missed, or flopped middle pair.
Now think about the key lesson from the Ace-to-Five Game: the player in position can value bet a Ten, the threshold hand, because position provides information.
Your position as the button opener — combined with three checks from your opponent and two calls — gives you strong reason to believe your Ten is likely best.
The bet size matters too.
A bet of one hundred dollars into a pot of approximately one hundred and twenty dollars puts your opponent in a difficult spot.
At those pot odds, she needs to call with more than half of her possible holdings to prevent your bluffs from being outright profitable.
That means her bluff-catching threshold is high, and many of the hands she'll call with will be worse than yours.
Think also about your bluffing range in this situation.
As a button opener, you have a wide pre-flop range that includes many hands with no showdown value on this river.
On the turn alone, you could have been betting with Ace-King, King-Jack, Jack-Nine, Nine-Eight, Eight-Seven, Five-Four, Four-Three, or a club draw — all of which miss completely by the river.
That large pool of potential bluffs gives your value bets legitimacy and forces your opponent to call with a wide enough range to protect herself.

King-Ten does not need to be the top of your value betting range to be a valid value bet — it just needs to be worth betting given the range of hands you could plausibly hold, and on a Ten-Six-Two board that double-pairs the Two on the river, it clearly is.
Problem two.
Same hand: you open fifteen dollars with King of hearts Ten of hearts, but this time from early position rather than the button.
The action goes the same way — big blind calls, Ten-Six-Two flop, you bet fifteen and she calls, Queen turn, you bet thirty and she calls, Two on the river, she checks.
What do you do?
The answer is check.
The contrast with problem one is instructive.
As an early position opener, your pre-flop range is significantly narrower — you raise with mostly high pairs, high Broadway cards, and a few suited connectors.
You have far fewer complete air hands in your range compared to a button open.
Fewer bluffing candidates means you cannot support as many value bets, so the value betting threshold shifts upward.
King-Ten is no longer comfortably within the value betting zone.

There is also a second factor: your opponent had less incentive to three-bet or check-raise an early-position raiser, because those hands benefit more from the implied odds of getting to see cheap cards against a strong opponent.
That means her range, when she just calls and checks repeatedly, is more likely to contain strong slowplayed hands than it would be against a button opener.
The combination of a narrower bluffing range on your end and a stronger continuing range on her end makes checking the prudent choice.
This is a beautiful illustration of how position and pre-flop range interact to determine how thinly you can value bet on the river.
The same two cards in the same board texture lead to completely different correct decisions based purely on position and implied range construction.
Problem three.
You open fifteen dollars with Queen of hearts Jack of hearts from early position, and the big blind calls.
The flop comes King of hearts, Nine of diamonds, Three of clubs.
The big blind checks, you bet fifteen dollars, and she calls.
The turn is the Ace of spades.
She checks.
What do you do?
The answer is bet big — really big, the book suggests perhaps two times the pot.
This is a situation that calls for an overbet with a polarized range.
Here is the reasoning.
As the pre-flop raiser from early position, you are the one who can credibly represent a heavily polarized range on this King-Nine-Three board that was then improved further by an Ace on the turn.
You are far more likely than your opponent to hold Aces, Kings, or Ace-King — the absolute top of the range on this board.
Those hands are the value portion of your polarized overbet range.
However, they are also a small fraction of your overall range.

With most of your hands — even reasonable hands like Ace-Queen or Ace-Jack — you would not want to build a giant pot because those hands do not want to be called by a lot of worse hands on a King-high board.
The hands that will make this overbet for value are relatively few, which means that to maintain a proper value-to-bluff ratio, you also have relatively few bluffing opportunities.
But Queen-Jack is one of the best bluffing candidates you could hold in this exact situation.
First, it is one of the weakest hands in your range — most of your range is pocket pairs, Ace-x, and King-x, all of which have too much showdown value to make good bluffs.
Second, Queen-Jack has a gutshot straight draw to a Ten, which is not much, but on this specific board it is the only draw available, which makes it particularly valuable as a bluff candidate.
The book emphasizes that the exact bet size matters less than the act of betting itself.
If you fail to think through what your range looks like in this spot, you will likely just check Queen-Jack by default because it feels weak and the board is scary.
But that default gives up a genuinely profitable bluffing opportunity.

There are no obvious bluffs on this board — no missed flush draws, no obvious missed straight draws — and if you do not force yourself to think explicitly about which hands in your range should be bluffing here, you will miss the right play.

The framework from this chapter — identifying your value range first, then filling in bluffs from your weakest holdings upward — makes Queen-Jack stand out as the natural bluffing candidate that it is.
These three problems together illustrate the chapter's key themes.
Position determines how thinly you can value bet and how wide your bluffing range can be.
Your range — not just your hand — is what gives your bets and checks their strategic meaning.
And identifying the threshold hands in your opponent's range — the ones where your action actually changes what she does — is the fundamental skill that drives all of these decisions.

sections.title: Conclusion
lines: Chapter six, section six. Conclusion.
Let's take a step back and pull the threads of this chapter together.
The Ace-to-Five Game is more complex than the toy games that came before it, and that complexity is precisely the point.

When there are more cards in the deck, more possible hands, and more close decisions, the equilibrium strategies become harder to implement correctly — and that difficulty creates real opportunities for error and exploitation.

The big picture of the equilibrium looks familiar: the out-of-position player bets a polarized range, the in-position player bets a polarized range, and each player calibrates their calling frequency to keep their opponent indifferent to bluffing.
But the details — exactly which hands end up in each portion of the range, at what frequencies, and why — require genuine strategic reasoning to work out correctly.
And it is in those details that the real poker lessons live.
A few key insights deserve to be re-emphasized as we close out this chapter.
The first is the value of position.

Position allows Ivan to value bet a Ten, to generate positive expected value bluffs with his Fives, and to avoid the trap that Opal finds herself in — the need to split her strongest hands between betting and checking ranges to keep Ivan's thin value bets unprofitable.
Ivan never has to worry about checking and then facing a polarized bet into a condensed range, because his check ends the betting.
That structural advantage is worth twelve cents in a game with two-dollar antes, which is a meaningful edge.
The second insight is how sensitive the out-of-position player's strategy is to the opponent's tendencies.
When Ivan folds too much, Opal should bluff aggressively and value bet less.
When Ivan bluffs too little, Opal should value bet more and bluff-catch less.
When Ivan stops bluffing entirely, Opal drops her most marginal value bet and her most marginal bluff simultaneously, producing a cleaner but smaller betting range.
The in-position player's strategy, by contrast, is considerably more straightforward: bet your strong hands, bluff with your weakest hands, and check the ones in the middle.
That clarity is itself another benefit of acting last.
The third insight is the importance of thinking in terms of targets.
Every bet, check, and call should be informed by a specific idea about which hands in your opponent's range you are trying to affect.
The hands that play themselves — the very strong hands that always call, the very weak hands that always fold — should not occupy much of your mental energy.
Your focus belongs on the threshold hands, the ones where your opponent's decision is genuinely close and where your choice of action can move the needle.
The fourth insight connects the theory to the practice: range construction is not just an abstract exercise.

By thinking through what your entire range of possible holdings looks like in any given situation — not just the one hand you currently hold — you give yourself the context to make the right decision with that specific hand.
The bluffing range you could have is what gives your value bets credibility.
The value betting range you could have is what gives your bluffs credibility.
These two things are inseparable, and the player who understands them together will always have an edge over the player who thinks about each hand in isolation.
There is a caveat worth naming clearly: the lessons in this chapter apply most cleanly to river decisions.
On earlier streets, the considerations multiply — there are future cards to come, future streets of betting, and the threat of a check-raise, which we have not yet discussed.
The next chapter is going to introduce raising into the Ace-to-Five Game, and we will see how that one additional option substantially complicates every decision we worked through here.

The threat of a check-raise, in particular, will change how the out-of-position player thinks about betting strong hands, because now checking does not simply lead to a free showdown — it can be the setup for a trap.
For now, though, let the core lessons from this chapter settle in.
Understand your range in every spot.
Know your targets.
Build your polarized betting ranges from the outside in — strong hands for value, weakest hands for bluffs — and calibrate each to keep your opponent unable to profit by deviating.
When you pick up a read that your opponent is straying from equilibrium, let the magnitude of that deviation determine how far you stray in response.
And always remember: any mistake your opponent makes is an opportunity for profit, but only if you are willing and able to make the right adjustment.

That is the practical promise of game-theory-optimal thinking — not that you play a rigid, automated style, but that you develop a clear enough understanding of equilibrium to recognize when and how to leave it.
We'll carry these ideas into chapter seven.

sections.title: Overview & Objectives
lines: Chapter seven, section one. Overview and objectives.
Up to this point in our study of toy games, we have deliberately left one option off the table — the raise.
Every game we have analyzed so far allowed players to bet or check, and when facing a bet, to call or fold, but nobody could come back over the top with a raise.
That restriction kept the math manageable while we built intuition about betting frequencies, bluff-to-value ratios, and how polarized ranges interact with condensed ranges.
Now it's time to remove that restriction and ask: what actually changes when a player is allowed to raise?
The answer turns out to be more nuanced than you might expect, and it reveals some deep principles about how range construction works under pressure.
The central insight of this chapter is that raising is a form of pressure — specifically, it is pressure applied against the bettor's range rather than against the checker's range.

When you are deciding whether to bet, you are choosing to expose your hand to potential counter-pressure in the form of a raise, and that possibility changes which hands benefit most from betting and which hands would rather avoid the whole confrontation.

The chapter works through this idea systematically by taking two games we already understand — the Reciprocal Ranges Game and the Ace-to-Five Game — and rerunning them with raising added as an option.
We will see that in one of those games, adding raising barely changes anything, while in the other it reshapes the entire equilibrium strategy in ways that are surprising and instructive.

After working through the equilibrium analysis, the chapter shifts to exploitation — four specific scenarios in which one player deviates from equilibrium, and we must figure out exactly how the other player should respond.

These exploitation scenarios cover a wide range of real-world mistakes: never bluff-raising, value betting too thinly, value betting too thickly, and refusing to call raises without the absolute nuts.

Finally, the chapter grounds everything in practical poker with a set of test problems featuring an actual hand played from first position with ace-eight of spades, walking through three streets of decision-making on a real board.

By the time you finish this chapter, you should have a clear sense of how raising changes the calculus of betting, why polarized ranges are so difficult to raise against, how medium-strength hands suffer most when facing pressure from either bets or raises, and how to build exploitative raising strategies when your opponent makes a specific mistake.

Those are the three stated learning objectives: appreciate the strategic role of raising, explain how the possibility of a raise affects betting strategy, and adapt both betting and raising strategies to exploit specific opponent errors.
Let's begin with the simplest possible environment in which to study raising — a game where the equilibrium is already very clean and the deck has only three cards.

sections.title: Scenario: Raising in the Reciprocal Ranges Game
lines: Chapter seven, section two. Scenario: Raising in the Reciprocal Ranges Game.
Let's revisit the Reciprocal Ranges Game with fresh eyes and then add the option to raise.
The setup is simple: each player antes one dollar and receives one card from a deck containing only an Ace, a King, and a Queen.
Both players know this, so if you have the Ace, you know your opponent has either the King or the Queen.
In the original version of this game, each player could either bet one dollar or check, and if a bet was made, the opponent could call or fold.
Now we are adding a third option for the player who faces a bet: instead of calling or folding, that player can raise to three dollars total.
If a raise is made, the original bettor can only call or fold — there is no re-raising in this version of the game.
Before diving into the equilibrium analysis, the book asks you to predict what will happen.
The first question is whether Opal's checking frequency will increase, decrease, or stay the same once raising is allowed.
The answer is that it stays the same — and this is the first surprising result of the chapter.
Adding the option to raise does not meaningfully change the equilibrium in the Reciprocal Ranges Game.
To understand why, you need to remember what the equilibrium looked like in the original game.
Each player bet a polarized range — strong hands to extract value and weak hands as bluffs — and each player called with a frequency designed to make the bettor indifferent to bluffing.
Middle-strength hands, specifically the King, faced difficult decisions between calling and folding.
Now the second question: if Opal does bet, what should Ivan's raising range look like?
Ivan should only raise when he holds the Ace.
And symmetrically, if Opal checks and then faces a bet from Ivan, Opal should only raise when she holds the Ace.
Furthermore, neither player ever calls a raise after having been the one who bet — they simply fold.
At first glance this looks dangerously exploitable: if your opponent always folds to a raise, shouldn't you just bluff-raise with everything?
The answer is no, and understanding why requires thinking carefully about what hands can actually be in the bettor's range.
Remember that the equilibrium betting strategy in this game is polarized — bets come from strong hands and bluffs, not from middle-strength hands.
The King, which is the middle-strength hand in this deck, never bets at equilibrium — and we confirmed that equilibrium doesn't change when raising is added.
So when Ivan holds a Queen and Opal bets, what does Ivan know?
He knows that since he has the Queen, Opal must hold either the Ace or the King.
Since Opal bets a polarized range, and since in this game a King doesn't show up in that range as a value bet, Opal almost certainly has the Ace.
Bluff-raising against someone who almost certainly holds the Ace is a disaster — the Ace calls every raise happily.
What if Ivan holds the Ace and Opal bets?
Then Opal must have the Queen, which means she is bluffing.
Ivan could raise, but Opal holds a hand that will strictly fold to any raise — she was bluffing with the weakest card in the deck.
So raising with the Ace does no extra damage; Ivan wins the pot either way once Opal folds, but he doesn't win any more money by raising.
The raise is harmless but pointless against a pure bluff.
This leads to the most important lesson from the Reciprocal Ranges Game with raising: the more polarized your opponent's betting range, the less useful raising becomes.
A polarized range contains hands at the extremes — very strong hands that call any raise happily, and very weak hands that fold to any pressure — but it contains no middle-strength hands.
Raising works by putting pressure on middle-strength hands, hands that are strong enough to be uncomfortable folding but not strong enough to confidently call.
When there are no such hands in the bettor's range, raising either runs straight into a call from the nuts or knocks out a hand that was going to fold anyway.
Neither outcome makes raising profitable as a bluff, and neither outcome generates extra value from a value raise beyond what a simple bet and call would achieve.

This is a profound insight that carries directly into real poker: when you are up against a very tight, polarized better who essentially only bets the nuts or total air, your raising range is restricted almost exclusively to your strongest holdings.
The middle of your range — hands that might raise for thin value or semi-bluff against a wider bettor — simply don't have good targets when the bettor has nothing in the middle either.
The Reciprocal Ranges Game gives us this lesson in its purest, cleanest form precisely because the game only has three cards.
With only three ranks in the deck, the structure forces polarization — there is almost no room for a genuine middle-strength betting hand.

In a richer game like the Ace-to-Five Game, where the deck has ten distinct ranks and equilibrium betting ranges are considerably wider and more nuanced, raising starts doing a lot more interesting work.
That's exactly where we turn next.

sections.title: Scenario: Raising in the Ace-to-Five Game
lines: Chapter seven, section three. Scenario: Raising in the Ace-to-Five Game.
The Ace-to-Five Game is a richer environment: each player antes one dollar and receives one card from a ten-card deck running from the Ace down to the Five.
Higher cards beat lower cards, so the Ace is the best hand and the Five is the worst.
In the original version without raising, each player could bet one dollar or check, and when facing a bet, could call or fold.

Now we are adding the same option we added in the Reciprocal Ranges Game: after a one-dollar bet, the opponent may raise to three dollars total, and the original bettor may only call or fold that raise — no re-raising.
Unlike the Reciprocal Ranges Game, this game has genuine middle-strength hands in equilibrium betting ranges, which means raising is going to matter a great deal here.
Let's work through the six questions the book poses, one by one, with full explanation of the numbers and the reasoning behind each answer.
The first question is whether Opal's betting frequency increases, decreases, or stays the same when raising is allowed.
The answer is that it decreases — significantly.
Without the option to raise, Opal bet nearly thirty-seven percent of her range at equilibrium.
With raising allowed, she bets only about twenty-seven percent of her range.
That is a reduction of roughly ten percentage points, and to understand why, you need to look at what happens to each hand in her range individually.
The book provides a detailed table, and the changes are striking when you walk through them hand by hand.
With an Ace, Opal went from betting eighty-one percent of the time without raising to betting only twenty percent of the time with raising.

That sounds like the Ace gets checked more often, and it does — but this is actually good news for the Ace, because when raising is allowed, the Ace is strong enough to check and then raise if Ivan bets.

The expected value of betting an Ace actually goes up slightly, from two dollars and fifty-six cents to two dollars and sixty-six cents, because when she does bet it and gets raised, she can call the raise profitably.

The Ace is indifferent between betting and checking precisely because both lines are now profitable — betting opens up the possibility of winning a larger pot, and checking opens up the possibility of executing a check-raise.
With a King, Opal goes from betting seventy-three percent of the time to betting one hundred percent of the time.
This is a complete reversal in the direction of change, and the reasoning is elegant.
The King is strong enough to call a raise — it does well against Ivan's calling range — but it is not strong enough to check-raise for value.
Because the King cannot profitably check-raise, it does not benefit from being in the checking range the way the Ace does.
Betting the King with full commitment is therefore strictly preferred over checking it.

With a Queen, betting frequency rises from sixty-three percent to eighty-six percent, and the expected value of betting a Queen drops from one dollar and sixty-seven cents to one dollar and fifty cents.
The Queen is the hand in the middle of Opal's value range — it is the weakest genuine value bet — and it becomes indifferent between calling and folding when raised.
The Queen suffers when raised, but not catastrophically; it's still profitable enough to bet because most of the time it won't face a raise, and when it does get called, that is still a good outcome.
With a Jack, the situation is completely different from the game without raising.
Without raising, Opal bet the Jack fifty-seven percent of the time, and the expected value of betting was one dollar and twenty-two cents.
With raising allowed, she bets the Jack zero percent of the time.
Why the complete abandonment of the Jack as a value bet?

The Jack's fundamental problem is that it wants to reach showdown more than anything else — it is a bluff-catcher, a hand that wins reasonably often against weak hands but loses to anything in Ivan's strong range.

Without raising, Opal could value bet the Jack because even though she frequently ran into stronger hands, the alternative of checking and calling those same hands would not have been much better — she was going to pay off Ivan's strong hands either way.

With raising allowed, betting a Jack creates a new nightmare scenario: she puts in one dollar, Ivan raises to three dollars, and now she has to either call another two dollars with a marginal hand or fold and lose the bet she already put in without even getting to showdown.
Checking the Jack is strictly better because it always gets to showdown at no additional cost.
The Ten and all lower hands in Opal's value range were already checking without raising being allowed, so they stay at zero percent betting frequency.
For her bluffs — the Six and the Five — Opal goes from betting thirty-six percent with a Six and fifty-six percent with a Five to betting thirty-four percent of the time with both.
The bluffing frequencies decrease slightly because less value betting overall means fewer bluffs are needed to balance the range.
The second question is about Ivan's betting frequency when facing a check.
Here too the answer is a decrease, though less dramatic than Opal's.
