Ivan goes from betting sixty percent of his range in the original game to about forty-five percent with raising allowed.
Looking at the individual hands in his range: Ivan still bets the Ace, King, and Queen one hundred percent of the time — these hands are strong enough to call a raise or even raise themselves.

With a Jack, Ivan drops from betting one hundred percent to betting thirty-seven percent of the time, since the Jack faces the same middle-hand dilemma we described for Opal — it doesn't love getting raised.
The Ten was bet fifty percent of the time in the original game but drops to zero percent with raising, for the same reasons the Jack dropped to zero in Opal's range.
For bluffs, Ivan still bets all his Fives at one hundred percent, because the Five has zero chance of winning at showdown so bluffing is always better than checking for the Five.

He bets eleven percent of his Sixes, and is essentially indifferent there, because the Six has some small chance of winning at showdown through checking, making it close between bluffing and pot-controlling.
The third question is about Ivan's strategy when Opal bets: what is the worst hand he raises for value, and what does he use as bluffs?
This is where the chapter produces its most surprising result.
Ivan raises the Ace for value always — that is not surprising.

He mixes raising and calling with the King: at equilibrium he raises the King ten percent of the time and calls ninety percent of the time, with equal expected value of two dollars and fifty-five cents either way.
Now, what does he use as bluff raises?

You might expect him to use his weakest hands — the Five and Six — because those are the ones that lose at showdown and therefore have the least to lose by raising and the most to gain from stealing the pot.
But that is wrong.
Ivan bluffs by raising with some combination of the Jack, Ten, Nine, Eight, and Seven — all of which are hands that can also call or fold — but he never raises with Fives or Sixes.
This reversal of the usual weakest-hands-are-best-bluffs logic requires careful explanation.
In every equilibrium strategy we have seen before, the weakest hands in a player's range were the preferred bluffs, because they had no showdown value to give up.
But here there is a crucial asymmetry: the Fives and Sixes in Opal's range are the hands that fold to a raise.
When Ivan holds a Five, that reduces the probability that Opal holds a Five — and a Five is exactly the kind of hand that would fold to Ivan's raise.
In other words, having a Five in your hand when you raise makes your raise less likely to work, because you have removed one of the cards from the deck that Opal might be bluffing with.
The same logic applies to the Six, though less extremely.

Meanwhile, hands like the Jack, Ten, Nine, Eight, and Seven are genuinely indifferent at equilibrium between calling, folding, and raising, because Opal's calling frequency is calibrated to make all three options yield zero expected value when bluffing.

So raising with a Jack doesn't cost Ivan more than folding the Jack would cost him — both actions yield zero — but it gives Ivan an extra way to win the pot that he can incorporate into his range without any penalty.
It is also worth noting that Ivan's folding frequency remains exactly the same as in the game without raising: he folds forty percent of the time when facing a bet.
This is not a coincidence.
The reason Opal's bluffs are indifferent between betting and not betting is determined entirely by Ivan's folding frequency, not by whether he responds to her bet by calling or raising.
So Ivan can shuffle his responses between calling and raising without affecting Opal's indifference on bluffs — as long as his total folding frequency stays at forty percent.
The fourth question is about how often Opal must call a raise to make Ivan indifferent between bluff-raising and not raising at all.
The answer is one half of the time — fifty percent.
This is different from the two-thirds calling frequency we saw in earlier chapters, and it is worth understanding why.
The general formula for making a bluff indifferent is: one minus the result of the bet divided by the sum of the bet and the pot.
In the original game, Ivan bet one dollar into a two-dollar pot, so the formula gave one minus one-third, which equals two-thirds.
In this scenario, Opal has already put one dollar into a pot that had two dollars before her bet, so after her bet there are three dollars in the pot.

Ivan is raising to three dollars total, but he already put in one dollar calling the bet doesn't apply here since we are computing the raise itself — the raise costs him three dollars to go into a three-dollar pot.
So the formula gives one minus three-sixths, which equals one-half.
Opal must call fifty percent of the time after being raised to deny Ivan a profitable bluff-raising opportunity.
The fifth question asks what Opal's check-raising range should look like.
The book provides a detailed table of her strategy after checking and facing a bet.
The first thing to notice is that not all hands sum to one hundred percent, because some hands sometimes end up in Opal's betting range instead of her checking range.
For instance, Opal checks an Ace eighty percent of the time and bets it twenty percent of the time, and when she checks an Ace and faces a bet, she always raises.
So eighty percent of the Aces she is dealt end up in her check-raising range, while the other twenty percent go into her betting range.

After checking and facing a bet: Opal raises with eighty percent of her range's Aces and zero percent of her Kings — the King is never checked at equilibrium, so it never appears in her check-raising range.
She calls with fourteen percent of her Queens, one hundred percent of her Jacks, and roughly forty-four percent of each hand from the Ten down to the Seven.
She folds roughly forty-nine percent of the Tens through Sevens, and one hundred percent of the Sixes and Fives.
She raises with about six point six seven percent of the Tens, Nines, Eights, and Sevens as bluffs.
Looking at the overall totals after checking: she calls forty percent of her checking range, folds forty-five percent, and raises fifteen percent.

The ratio of value raises to bluff raises in her check-raising range is three to one — she raises three Aces for value for every one bluff raise — and this is exactly the correct ratio given that she must call half the time when raised.
The book notes that the decimal bluff-raising frequencies for the Seven through Ten are left precise specifically so you can verify this three-to-one ratio yourself.
The sixth and final question is whether the option to raise increases or decreases the overall value of the game for Ivan.
The answer is that it technically increases his expected value, but only by an almost negligible amount.
Without raising, Ivan's expected value over the course of the game is one dollar and six point one cents.
With raising, it rises to one dollar and six point three cents.
That is a gain of two-tenths of one penny per hand — barely measurable.
However, the book makes an important observation here: these numbers assume both players play perfectly.
In practice, adding the raising option makes the game more complex, which means more opportunities for mistakes.

All other things being equal, the more skilled player benefits most from added complexity, because they are better positioned both to keep their own ranges balanced and to identify imbalances in the opponent's play.

Ivan, as the in-position player, also has a slight structural advantage: he does not need to worry about balancing a checking range because he always acts last, which means his equilibrium betting strategy is somewhat simpler and therefore less error-prone.

So while raising adds only a hair of theoretical value at equilibrium, the real value of understanding how to raise correctly comes from the exploitation opportunities that open up when opponents make mistakes with or around raising — which is exactly what the next section covers.

sections.title: Scenario: Exploitation with Raising
lines: Chapter seven, section four. Scenario: Exploitation with Raising.

The rules are the same as in the previous section: each player antes one dollar, each player may bet one dollar or check in an unopened pot, after a one-dollar bet the opponent may raise to three dollars, and facing a raise the only options are call or fold.
This section asks us to work through four different mistakes that a player might make with raising, and to figure out the best exploitative response in each case.
The framework the book uses is the Four-Step Exploitative Process: envision the equilibrium, make a read, identify the exploits, and determine the degree of deviation.
Let's work through all four problems carefully.
Problem one: Suppose Ivan will never raise as a bluff.
Other than that, we do not know anything specific about how he plays.
How should Opal adapt?

Envision the equilibrium: at equilibrium, Ivan raises a polarized range when Opal bets — all his Aces for value, some of his Kings mixed in, and a number of bluff raises using hands in the Jack-through-Seven region.
This polarized raising range is what makes Opal indifferent between betting and check-calling a Queen, and between betting and check-raising an Ace.
It also makes it unprofitable for Opal to bet a Jack, because the threat of a raise from Ivan's range makes the Jack uncomfortable in the betting seat.
Make a read: Ivan is essentially abandoning his option to raise as a bluff entirely.
His raises, when they come at all, are only for value.
This means that when Ivan raises, Opal faces no difficult decision — she can simply fold anything that is not the nuts, because Ivan's raising range is pure value.
Identify the exploits: This change has two opposite effects on Opal's strategy.
First, it increases the value of betting hands that would ordinarily be in trouble against a raise.
If Opal bets a Jack and Ivan raises, she can fold with no grief — she never faces the nightmare scenario of a bluff-raise she cannot beat.
This makes the Jack a much more comfortable bet.
Opal can now bet Jacks and Queens without worrying about paying off bluff-raises, so she can expand her value range considerably.
Second, and counterintuitively, it decreases the value of betting with the Ace.
At equilibrium, one of the reasons to bet the Ace is that Ivan might raise and she can call the raise profitably, winning a large pot.
If Ivan never bluff-raises, then the only raise Opal faces when she bets an Ace is from another Ace — but there is only one Ace in the deck, so Ivan cannot have an Ace if Opal has it.
Wait — in the Ace-to-Five Game there are ten different cards, not three, so this is not quite as simple.

The core point is that Ivan's raises without bluffs are less threatening and less rewarding for Opal when she has the Ace, because she already expects to win without needing the extra value from a raise-and-call sequence.

The option to check-raise becomes more attractive, though: since Ivan will never raise as a bluff, Opal can check the Ace hoping to induce a bet from Ivan, then raise him, and know that if Ivan re-raises it will be legitimate and she should fold — but he has already said he never raises as a bluff, so she just calls.
Determine the degree of deviation: This is a large deviation from equilibrium, even though the individual hands Ivan is adjusting — his bluff hands — were all indifferent at equilibrium.
In aggregate, eliminating bluff-raises completely removes raising as a meaningful strategic tool for him.

Opal's best response involves checking her Aces to trap with a check-raise, betting her Kings one hundred percent of the time as before, and now also betting Qs and Js one hundred percent of the time — a major expansion of her value range.
She balances those additional value bets with the right proportion of bluffs using Fives and Sixes.
If Ivan does raise, Opal folds unless she has an Ace.
She never worries about bluff-raises because they don't exist in Ivan's strategy.
The broader real-world lesson here is important: many poker players you will encounter — especially on the turn and the river — are passive and essentially never raise as bluffs.
Against these players, you should not be concerned about pot control or about protecting yourself from raise-bluffs.
You should bet whenever you expect to be ahead, and fold if raised, confident that your opponent's raise is legitimate.
Problem two: Suppose Opal will only value bet with a King or better.
That means she never bets a Queen for value.
How should Ivan adapt?

Envision the equilibrium: at equilibrium, Opal bets Qs at a frequency specifically designed to make Ivan indifferent between calling, folding, and raising with hands in the Jack-to-Seven range, and also indifferent between calling and raising with a King.
Make a read: Opal is not betting enough thin value.
She is leaving Qs in her checking range even though at equilibrium they belong partly in her betting range.
Identify the exploits: A lack of thin value bets is a specific new kind of mistake.
It is not the same as having a range that is too strong or too weak overall.
The specific effect is that the hands in the middle of Opal's betting range — the Qs — are gone, which changes what Ivan should do with his Kings and with his bluffing candidates.
At equilibrium, Ivan was indifferent between calling and raising with a King, because sometimes Opal held a Q that would be in trouble when raised.
Now that Opal has no Qs in her betting range, Ivan's King has less incentive to raise — a Q is no longer there to fold and make the raise profitable through bluff-raising value.
So Ivan's Kings become pure calls.
Meanwhile, Ivan's Qs — which at equilibrium were standard calls — are now no stronger relative to Opal's range than his Sevens are.
He will sometimes raise them as bluffs and sometimes fold them.
But overall, Ivan's raising frequency is lower: he now raises only Aces for value instead of Aces plus some Kings, and he has a correspondingly smaller pool of bluffs to balance that value.
Determine the degree of deviation: Opal's mistake is small — she is playing a pure strategy with a hand that was supposed to be mixed.
Correspondingly, Ivan's adjustment is modest.
He even benefits slightly without adjusting at all, simply because fewer of his medium hands face difficult decisions from Opal's narrower betting range.
The book uses this problem to make an important broader point about thin value betting.
Many players believe they should never value bet a hand that would face a difficult decision if raised.
This belief is mistaken.
The whole point of including thin value bets in your range is precisely to create hands in the middle of your betting range that create difficult decisions for the opponent's raising hands.
If your betting range contains only hands that are either strong enough to call any raise happily or weak enough to fold trivially, then you have given your opponent no incentive to raise as a bluff.
That is exactly what happened in the Reciprocal Ranges Game, and it is what happens to Opal's range when she removes the Qs.
Yes, getting raised after betting a Q is painful — what was a positive expected value hand becomes a zero expected value hand.
But this pain is outweighed by the profit from all the times the Q gets called, and more importantly, by the strategic benefit of making your opponent face a dilemma with the raising option.

Checking Qs does not even insulate Opal from difficult decisions — it just shifts the difficulty onto her Kings, which become the new thinnest value bet and which face the same raise-versus-call dilemma.

If she wanted to truly avoid difficult decisions, she would have to narrow her betting range all the way down to just Aces and bluffs — a hyper-polarized range that misses out on enormous value against Ivan's medium hands that would otherwise fold to value bets or call them off.
Problem three: Suppose Ivan has observed Opal betting some Jacks, and he believes she will keep doing so.
How should he adapt?
Envision the equilibrium: Opal's equilibrium strategy is to check Jacks always and to bet Qs at a mixed frequency.
The mix of Qs in her betting range makes Ivan indifferent between raising and calling with a King.
Make a read: Opal is value betting too thinly — she has added Jacks to her betting range, which is a deviation from equilibrium since a Jack should strictly prefer checking.
We do not know whether she is also adding extra bluffs to compensate.

Identify the exploit: When an opponent value bets too thinly, the right response is not simply to raise more for value or to raise more as a bluff in isolation — it is to raise a wider polarized range: more value raises and proportionally more bluff raises together.
Ivan should still raise a balanced, polarized range, but that range should be wider than at equilibrium.
Specifically, Ivan is now indifferent to raising a King at equilibrium, but against Opal's expanded betting range he strictly prefers raising with Kings.
A K is a better value raise because it benefits from Opal having Jacks in her range — Jacks are exactly the kind of thin-value hands that will call and lose to a King.
Determine the degree of deviation: Opal's mistake is more significant than just playing a pure strategy with an indifferent hand.
She is betting a hand that strictly prefers checking, which means she is giving up expected value even to an equilibrium strategy from Ivan.
Ivan should capitalize by widening his raising range.
Importantly, Jacks themselves become less attractive as bluff raises for Ivan, and more attractive as bluff-catchers.
Why?

Because a Jack in Ivan's hand blocks Opal's Jack — it reduces the chance that Opal is holding a Jack — and a Jack in Opal's range is exactly the kind of hand Ivan wants her to have when he raises, because she might call the raise with it and lose.
So calling with a Jack rather than raising it shifts the equity in Ivan's favor.

The book makes a beautiful observation here about the nature of a polarized raising range: you do not need to know in advance how Opal will respond to a raise in order to exploit her error of betting too widely.
Once she bets too wide a range, those overly weak hands have to go somewhere when Ivan raises.
If she calls with them, Ivan's value raises profit.
If she folds them, Ivan's bluff raises profit.
The error is already made when she bets; the raise just collects the tax.
If Ivan also happens to know that she will fold or call too much in response to the raise, that gives him an additional opportunity on top of the base exploit.
But the base exploit — widening the polarized raising range — works regardless of Opal's specific response.
Problem four: Suppose Ivan will only call a raise with an Ace.
He folds everything else when raised, including Kings and Queens.
How should Opal adapt?
This is the most complex of the four problems because Ivan's behavior is ambiguous in an important way.
Envision the equilibrium: after Opal checks and Ivan bets, his equilibrium strategy involves betting a polarized range of strong hands and bluffs.
When Opal raises, Ivan calls with Aces, Queens, Kings, and Jacks collectively — those medium holdings account for half of his calling range.
Make a read: This is tricky, because we know Ivan is making one of two mistakes, but not which one.

Mistake one: Ivan is betting his equilibrium range but then folding too much to raises — if he is betting Queens and Kings at equilibrium frequency but folding them to raises, he is giving up when he should call.

Mistake two: Ivan is aware of this weakness and compensates by betting a narrow range — mostly Aces and bluffs — so that when he does bet and face a raise, calling only with Aces is approximately correct.
The problem is that these two mistakes are not exploitable in the same way.
Identify the exploit: If Ivan is making Mistake One — betting wide but folding too much — Opal wants to check-raise more often as a bluff.
However, she does not want to check strong hands waiting for a check-raise, because Ivan is betting a wide range and her strong hands would profit from those calls.
Wait — actually she does not want to check strong hands against this mistake because they would be happy to bet and face a wide betting range from Ivan.
Actually the book clarifies: checking strong hands is correct if Ivan is making Mistake Two — betting narrowly — because then strong hands would rarely induce bets and would miss value.
In both cases, the book concludes that betting strong hands — Aces, Kings, Qs — is the better play against either of Ivan's possible strategies.
Betting strong hands is never punished by either of Ivan's possible strategies, so that becomes the anchor of Opal's exploitative approach.

Determine the degree of deviation: Opal cannot go crazy with check-raise bluffing, because if Ivan is pursuing Mistake Two — betting only when he has near-nuts — then her bluffs will run into the nuts and lose money.
She needs to calibrate her check-raise bluffing to a level that exploits Mistake One without being devastated by Mistake Two.
The maximally exploitative solution turns out to be: Opal bets all her Aces, Kings, Qs, and Fives, and checks everything else.
This is a balanced, polarized betting range and a condensed checking range.

Normally a condensed checking range is easily exploited by aggressive betting, but here Ivan is constrained: if he bets aggressively to exploit her checking range, he will be betting a lot of thin hands that will fold to Opal's check-raise.
After checking and facing a bet, Opal raises with about seventeen percent of her checking range, using any mix of the Jack-through-Seven hands.
Notice that this raising range is not polarized in the usual sense — it contains no value hands, only bluffs.
She doesn't need value hands in her check-raising range because Ivan will never call a raise without an Ace, and she is the one who has the Ace in her betting range, not her check-raising range.
She avoids using Fives and Sixes as bluff raises because, as we have established, they block Ivan's folding range.
The elegant conclusion is that Opal's maximally exploitative strategy places Ivan in a dilemma between two options, both of which are worse than his equilibrium strategy.
Option one: Ivan bets a wide range, which means many thin hands that will fold when Opal check-raises, even though those check-raises are pure bluffs.

Option two: Ivan tightens up his betting range, which means Opal's bluffs lose money but she gets to check and show down many marginal hands for free, winning value she would have lost to Ivan's bluffs under a different strategy.
Opal calibrates her check-raise bluff frequency precisely to the level that makes Ivan indifferent between these two bad options.
If she check-raises too often, Ivan strictly prefers Option Two — bet rarely and pick off bluffs with Aces.
If she check-raises too little, Ivan strictly prefers Option One — bet wide and exploit her condensed checking range.
The right amount of check-raise bluffing leaves him stuck between two suboptimal strategies, which is the essence of a well-constructed exploit.
The takeaway is that even partial information about an opponent's mistake can allow you to craft a strategy that benefits regardless of how they compensate for it.

sections.title: Test Yourself
lines: Chapter seven, section five. Test yourself.
Now let's apply everything we have learned about raising to some actual poker scenarios.
The chapter walks through a hand with Ace-Eight of spades opened from first position, with the big blind calling.
We follow this hand through three streets of decisions and then look at a separate hand with Ace-King of diamonds.
Problem one: You open to fifteen dollars with Ace-Eight of spades in first position, the big blind calls, and the flop comes Ten of hearts, Ten of spades, Two of diamonds.
The big blind checks to you.
What do you do?
Against a tough opponent, the correct play is to check.
Ace-Eight of spades on this board is a medium-strength hand.
You have a pair of Tens on the board with an Ace kicker, you have a backdoor flush draw, and your Ace is an overcard to everything except the Ten.
But you should not expect to be ahead when an opponent calls a bet on this board — players who call here tend to have Tens for trips, pocket pairs for full houses, or at minimum decent made hands.
The critical strategic consideration is the threat of a check-raise.

As the opener in first position, your range on this flop is generally strong — you showed initiative before the flop and your range contains many overpairs, top pairs, and high-card hands that flopped well.
But your big blind opponent, having called a raise out of position, is more likely than you to hold a Ten.
When someone holds trips on a paired board, the best play for them is often to check and induce a bet from the preflop raiser.
If you bet here, you risk running into a check-raise from a polarized range: your opponent either has trips — a very strong hand — or a bluff.
A medium-strength hand like Ace-Eight faces the worst possible situation when raised by a polarized range.
If you call the raise with trips out there, you are almost certainly behind.
If you fold, you have lost your bet and surrendered the pot to what might be a bluff.
Neither outcome is good, and you get into that uncomfortable spot by betting in the first place.
As the in-position player, you have the valuable option of controlling the pot by checking behind.
Checking behind accomplishes two things: it keeps the pot small on a board where your hand is medium-strength, and it prevents you from facing a check-raise that puts you in an agonizing spot.
The book even notes there is a case to be made for checking stronger hands like pocket pairs here — overpairs on a paired board are excellent candidates for pot control as well.
Against weaker players who simply will not take advantage of the opportunity to check-raise bluff you, the calculus changes.
Against a passive opponent who only raises when they have it, you can bet the flop more freely because the scary check-raise is much less likely to come.
The lesson is that the decision to bet is not just about whether your hand is strong enough to want a call — it is also about whether you can handle the worst-case response.
Problem two: You checked behind on the flop, and now the turn brings the Ace of hearts, making the board Ten of hearts, Ten of spades, Two of diamonds, Ace of hearts.
The big blind checks again.
What do you do?
Your hand has improved significantly — you now have two pair, Aces and Tens.
There are also draws on the board: a heart flush draw has appeared with the Ace of hearts, and any hand with a backdoor straight might now have a cleaner path.
This is a close decision, and the book calls it exactly that.
Your hand is stronger, but the strategic considerations from the previous street do not fully disappear.
As the first-position raiser, your range on this board is weighted toward pocket pairs and hands that connected with the Ace — Ace-King, Ace-Queen, and similar holdings.
This means your range generally improved a lot with the Ace on the turn.
But here is the problem: when your range is that strong, your opponent does not have much incentive to call a bet with weak hands.
If your opponent reads you as likely to have an Ace after this board texture, they will fold hands that are weaker than a good Ace, and your value bet against those hands shrinks.
Furthermore, you are still at risk of a check-raise.
Your opponent may have slowplayed a Ten or flopped Two pair and is now ready to spring a check-raise when you bet the turn.
A well-timed check-raise on the turn in a bigger pot would be even more uncomfortable than a check-raise on the flop.
The book's guidance is that against a typical passive and straightforward opponent, you should probably bet here.
Squeezing value from good-but-not-great hands against players who are unlikely to check-raise is one of the primary ways you exploit passive players.
But against a tough, thinking opponent who might check-raise a polarized range, you might check again and wait for a better spot.
The nuance is in your read on the specific player.
Problem three: You checked the turn as well.
The river brings the Six of hearts, completing the board as Ten of hearts, Ten of spades, Two of diamonds, Ace of hearts, Six of hearts.
The big blind checks a third time.
Now what do you do?
Now it is time to bet.
Your opponent has checked three times in a row, showing nothing but weakness throughout the hand.
Yes, there is a new threat on the board — three hearts have arrived and a flush is possible.
But your opponent has never shown any strength, and the range of hands that would lead to three checks on this board is generally one that does not contain many strong hands.
The risk of a raise constrains you somewhat — you cannot go too large, because if you get raised on the river you will be indifferent between calling and folding, which is never a comfortable spot.
But the threat of a check-raise on the river, while real, needs to be weighed against the probability that your opponent simply has a medium or weak hand and will call.
As the in-position player, you have significant information from your opponent's repeated passivity.
Strong hands — trips, flushes, full houses — would have had strong incentive to lead out or check-raise on an earlier street.
The fact that your opponent checked three times shifts the probability distribution of their holding toward medium-weak hands that have been taking the free showdown.
You can value bet thinly here because of that information.
If the big blind does check-raise, you acknowledge that it is a bad outcome — but it is the worst-case scenario, not the expected outcome.
The expected outcome is that your opponent calls with a weaker hand and you win an extra bet.
That expected gain outweighs the occasional cost of running into a check-raise.

The book frames this as a specific application of the general principle: you must sometimes bet medium-strength hands and accept that you will occasionally face a raise, because the alternative of always checking medium hands gives up too much value against the many opponents who would have called.
Problem four: You open to fifteen dollars with Ace-King of diamonds in middle position, and the button calls.
Your read on the button is that he is loose and passive both before the flop and after.
The flop comes Ace of hearts, Nine of hearts, Six of diamonds.
You bet twenty dollars and he calls.
The turn is the Nine of spades — now the board is Ace of hearts, Nine of hearts, Six of diamonds, Nine of spades — and you both check.
The river is the Six of hearts, making the final board Ace of hearts, Nine of hearts, Six of diamonds, Nine of spades, Six of hearts.
Your opponent checks.
What do you do?
The answer is to bet — and you can probably get away with a fairly large bet, close to the pot.
Your hand, Ace-King, has been counterfeited somewhat by the paired board.
The board now contains two Nines and two Sixes, and any opponent holding a pocket pair above a Six now has two pair with the board pairing.
An opponent with a Nine or a Six in their hand has a full house or near one.
You have Aces and Nines with a King kicker — a solid hand but not an unbeatable one.

Many players in your position would instinctively check because they are out of position, their hand is medium-strong but not unbeatable, and they are afraid of paying off a better hand or getting raised by a bluff.
These concerns are understandable, but checking is a mistake here for a specific structural reason.
As the out-of-position player, you cannot guarantee a free showdown by checking.
If you check, your opponent is likely to bet when he has a hand that beats you — full houses and better two-pair hands will not pass up the opportunity to bet for value.
You would then be in the uncomfortable position of calling with your medium-strength hand, paying off hands you were hoping to avoid.
So whether you bet or check, you are likely to put money into the pot against the hands that beat you.
Given that you are going to pay off stronger hands one way or another, the question becomes: is it better to bet yourself or to check and give your opponent the choice of bet size?
When you bet, you extract value from your opponent's medium-strength hands — Aces with worse kickers than King, for instance — hands that will call your bet but might not have bet themselves.
When you check, you give your opponent the option to bet when they want a larger pot, which is exactly when they have a better hand than you.
The read on this specific opponent matters enormously here.
He is loose and passive before and after the flop — he is unlikely to raise as a bluff, and he is unlikely to bet as a bluff if you check.

Against this player, you bet because you believe he will call with worse hands, and you plan to fold if raised, with confidence that a raise from this player means a legitimate strong hand rather than a bluff.

This is the distilled application of the exploitation principles from earlier in the chapter: against a passive player who will not raise as a bluff, you can value bet medium-strength hands more aggressively, fold when raised, and feel good about the outcome.

sections.title: Conclusion
lines: Chapter seven, section six. Conclusion.
Chapter seven has taken us through one of the most important and nuanced additions to our growing framework of game theory optimal poker: the raise.
Adding this option to our toy games generated insights that would not have been visible if we had only studied betting and calling.
Let's consolidate the major themes before moving on.

The first and most fundamental lesson is that raising is a form of pressure applied against the bettor's range, and the effectiveness of that pressure depends entirely on what is in the bettor's range.
We saw this most clearly in the Reciprocal Ranges Game, where adding the option to raise changed essentially nothing about the equilibrium.
The reason was that the equilibrium betting range in that game was already highly polarized — strong hands and bluffs, with nothing in the middle.
A raise works by threatening the middle: hands that are strong enough to want to stay in the hand but not strong enough to comfortably call a raise.
Without a middle in the bettor's range, raising becomes either an expensive way to lose more chips to the nuts or a pointless gesture against a bluff that was going to fold anyway.

The Ace-to-Five Game showed the other side of the coin: when the bettor's range contains genuine middle-strength hands like the Jack and the Queen, raising becomes a meaningful strategic tool that reshapes the entire equilibrium.
The second major lesson is that the threat of a raise — even without a raise actually happening — changes optimal betting strategy.
When raising is allowed, value bettors must become more selective.
Opal's betting frequency dropped from thirty-seven percent to twenty-seven percent when raising was introduced, and the Jack disappeared from her value betting range entirely.
The Jack wants to get to showdown; the risk of being raised and having to make a difficult decision, or worse, fold and lose the bet, makes betting the Jack strictly worse than checking it.
The general principle is that hands in the middle of your value range suffer the most under the threat of a raise.
Strong hands — Aces and Kings in the Ace-to-Five Game — either call raises profitably or check-raise for value.
Very weak hands fold trivially when raised and the raise just takes their bet.
But the middle — the Jacks and Tens and similar hands — face a genuinely painful decision when raised, and the threat of that decision makes them prefer the passive line of checking.
This is another expression of a principle we have encountered in earlier chapters: medium-strength hands suffer the most when facing pressure from polarized ranges.
The third lesson concerns the composition of bluff-raising ranges, and it contains perhaps the most surprising reversal from conventional wisdom in this entire chapter.
Normally, the weakest hands in a range are the best bluffs because they have no showdown value to sacrifice.
But when it comes to bluff-raising, the weakest hands can actually be the worst choice.
The reason is that your weakest hands are also your opponent's most likely folding hands — and if you hold one, your opponent is less likely to hold one, making your raise less likely to work.
In the Ace-to-Five Game, Ivan never bluff-raises with Fives or Sixes when Opal bets, because those are exactly the hands he wants Opal to be holding when he raises — they are the ones that fold.
Instead, he bluff-raises with hands in the Jack-through-Seven range, which are genuinely indifferent between calling, folding, and raising, and which do not block Opal's folding hands.

This is a concept that shows up in real poker as blocking — choosing your bluffs partly based on which cards in your hand make it more or less likely that your opponent holds a hand that will fold to your bluff.
The fourth lesson comes from the exploitation scenarios: different mistakes require different responses, and you need to diagnose the specific mistake before crafting the optimal counterstrategy.
An opponent who never bluff-raises tells you that you can bet thinner and fold to any raise.
An opponent who does not bet enough thin value tells you that you should narrow your own raising range slightly.
An opponent who value bets too thinly tells you to widen your polarized raising range — more value raises and proportionally more bluffs.

An opponent who will not call a raise without the nuts presents the most complex situation: you must determine whether the exploitable strategy is folding too much or betting too narrowly, and calibrate your response to exploit both possibilities simultaneously.
The chapter's key lessons, spelled out explicitly, are worth reviewing one by one.
First: the more polarized your opponent's betting range, the less aggressively you should raise, because a polarized range contains few hands that face difficult decisions when raised.
Second: raising is the specific tool for punishing thin value bets.
Many players respond to aggression by checking and calling, but that is only the right response when the aggression comes from excessive bluffing.
If an opponent bets a wide but balanced range that includes many thin value hands, checking and calling plays right into their strategy.
The correct counter is to raise — both for value and as a bluff — putting those thin value hands in the very uncomfortable spot they were hoping to avoid.
Third: you make more money dealing with difficult decisions than running from them.
Optimal play sometimes requires betting hands that are not the nuts, knowing that a raise will create a genuinely hard decision.
The instinct to avoid those situations is understandable, but surrendering the value of all the times the bet gets called or the opponent folds is too high a price to pay.
The occasional painful raise is the cost of doing business when you bet a balanced range.
Fourth: when you need strong hands in your checking range, your strongest holdings make the best traps.
An Ace can afford to wait in the checking range because it can check-raise for value if Ivan bets.
A King cannot check-raise for value against Ivan's range, so the King is better deployed as an immediate value bet rather than a trap that will never get paid off.
The chapter also highlights two important caveats to keep in mind as you move from toy games toward real poker.
The first caveat is that re-raising constrains raising in the same way that raising constrains betting.
Our toy games did not allow re-raises, but in real poker, if you raise and your opponent can re-raise, you face the same dilemma that Opal faced when deciding whether to bet a Jack.
Your raising range must be calibrated to handle the threat of a re-raise.
The second caveat is that on earlier streets — the flop and the turn — board coverage matters.

Because hands can change value as more cards come out, you often want to raise a somewhat less polarized range on earlier streets, incorporating draws and semi-strong made hands alongside pure bluffs and pure value.
This ensures that your range stays reasonably balanced regardless of how the board develops, rather than becoming excessively strong or weak when specific cards arrive.

Taken together, the lessons of chapter seven give you a much richer vocabulary for thinking about raising as a strategic tool: when it is effective, why it changes betting frequencies, how to construct balanced raising ranges, and how to exploit specific mistakes opponents make with or against raises.

These principles will become increasingly important as we build toward more complex multi-street games where raising, re-raising, and range construction across multiple decisions are all in play simultaneously.

sections.title: Overview & Objectives
lines: Chapter eight, section one. Overview and objectives.

We have spent the earlier parts of this book building up our understanding through simplified toy games, and now we are ready to take everything we have learned and apply it to real, full poker situations.

The Ace-to-Five game and its various extensions gave us a clean laboratory for understanding equilibrium strategy, polarized versus condensed ranges, when to bluff, how often to call, when raising makes sense, and how the actions players take shape the ranges they hold as a hand progresses.
All of that analytical machinery transfers directly to hold 'em, with one important addition that the toy games could only hint at: card removal, and the strategic concept of blockers.
In a hold 'em hand, the two cards you are dealt are not just your hand — they are also a window into what your opponents cannot hold.

If you have the ace of spades, nobody else at the table has the ace of spades, and that simple fact can be enormously consequential when an opponent's range is weighted toward hands that contain the ace of spades.

The toy games touched on this idea in passing, but in hold 'em the effect is more nuanced because you have two hole cards, because suited and connected combinations matter enormously, and because it is rarely an all-or-nothing question — it is usually a matter of degree.
This chapter has two main jobs to do.

First, it will give us a careful, grounded introduction to how blockers function strategically, explaining when they influence bluffing decisions, when they influence value betting decisions, and what role they actually play relative to other factors.

Second, it will walk us through a richly detailed real-world poker scenario — a nine-handed no-limit hold 'em hand in which we will apply every tool in our kit: equity reasoning, range construction, polarized betting, bluff ratios, calling frequencies, check-raising, and blockers.
By the time we finish this chapter, we should be able to do four things well.

We should be able to account for blockers when we are deciding which hands to bluff, which hands to value bet, and which hands to call with — not treating blockers as a vague afterthought, but as a precise, deliberate factor in hand selection.
We should be able to construct appropriate betting, calling, and raising ranges for bets of many different sizes, recognizing that a small bet demands a very different response than a large one.

We should understand the powerful strategic principle that checking a strong hand can actually make that hand more valuable — that by checking, a player conceals strength, widens the gap between their range and their opponent's perceived range, and unlocks polarized betting opportunities on later streets that would not otherwise exist.

And we should be able to look at a specific hand, think about whether it is a better candidate for betting outright or for check-raising, and make that choice with clear logical reasoning rather than instinct.

This is the chapter where all the pieces come together, and it is worth taking the time to follow every step of the analysis carefully, because the reasoning here will generalize to an enormous range of real situations you encounter at the table.

sections.title: Blockers Tell You What Your Opponents Don't Have
lines: Chapter eight, section two. Blockers tell you what your opponents don't have.
A blocker is simply a card in your hand that reduces the probability that a specific strong hand exists in your opponent's range.
Because your opponents cannot see your hole cards, you and you alone possess this piece of information, and that asymmetry can be exploited.
Let us start with a concrete example to anchor the concept.
Imagine the board on the river reads queen of spades, jack of diamonds, seven of spades, six of diamonds, four of spades — a board with three spades, meaning the spade flush draw has come in.
Now suppose your hole cards are the ace of spades and the king of hearts.
Every player at the table can see that a flush is possible, and any observant opponent will be asking themselves whether you might have one.
But here is what only you know: because you hold the ace of spades, it is literally impossible for anyone else to hold the nut flush.
The nuts would require the ace of spades, and you have it.

Lower flushes are still completely possible — someone could have the king of spades paired with another spade, or the queen of spades with another spade, or any number of other combinations — but the strongest possible flush is categorically unavailable to your opponents.
Why does this matter strategically?
Because a meaningful portion of the hands that would call a river bet or a river raise on this board are nut or near-nut flushes, and those hands don't exist in your opponents' ranges right now.
The set of hands that would call you down has been quietly thinned out, and only you know it.
That makes bluffing substantially more attractive — you are facing a reduced risk of getting called by the exact monsters that would defeat you most convincingly.

Equipped with this information, a well-timed river bluff is likely to show a positive expected value, not because you have read your opponent as weak, but because game theory and math support it given the card removal effect.
Now, it is important to place blockers in the correct perspective within the overall strategic framework.
The book is explicit on this point: blockers are not the be-all and end-all of no-limit hold 'em strategy.
In most situations, they function as tiebreakers.
What this means is that the primary work of deciding whether to bluff, call, or raise is done by equity reasoning, pot odds, range analysis, and the principles we have studied throughout this book.
Blockers come in when you have already determined that you want to bluff with some hands or value bet with some hands, and you now need to choose which specific hands fill those roles.
When the choices are otherwise close, blockers decide.
Let us think about bluffing first.

In the toy game scenarios we analyzed earlier, bluffing at equilibrium was almost always a zero expected value proposition — you were indifferent to bluffing with the relevant hands, meaning it did not matter whether you bluffed or not.
There were two exceptions to that pattern.
The first was with the very weakest hands, which had no showdown value and therefore always preferred to bluff rather than check and lose automatically.
The second exception is exactly what we are discussing now: hands with important blockers.

When your hand contains a card that dramatically reduces the probability of villain holding the most dangerous calling hands, bluffing with that hand is better than zero EV — it is positively profitable, even at equilibrium.
That is a meaningful statement, because it means that unlike most bluffing decisions, the decision to bluff with a blocker hand is not a coin flip — it is a genuine edge.
Blockers work similarly for value betting decisions.

Again, in the toy games, players were generally indifferent with their thinnest value bets — the analogy was Ivan betting a ten in the Ace-to-Five game, where he was right on the margin between value and not-value.
If your hand happens to block some of the stronger hands your opponent might hold, then betting for thin value with that hand becomes safer.
You are less likely to be raised off your hand or called by a dominating holding.
The blocker converts a marginal, roughly zero-EV thin value bet into a mildly positive one.
One more thing deserves emphasis before we move on to the scenario.
The informational advantage from holding a blocker works because your opponent does not know you have it.
If your opponent could see your cards, they would adjust their strategy accordingly, and the blocker effect would be neutralized.
The power of the blocker is entirely a function of private information — it is a secret edge, and it only exists as long as your hand remains hidden.

That is why it is particularly dangerous to be predictable about which hands you bluff with, because if an observant opponent notices that your bluffs always come in a particular spot with hands of a certain type, they can infer what blockers you tend to hold, and the advantage evaporates.

Keep this concept in the back of your mind as we walk through the full scenario in the next section, because blockers will reappear repeatedly as the most important tiebreaker in a whole series of close decisions.

sections.title: Scenario: After the Turn Checks Through
lines: Chapter eight, section three. Scenario: After the Turn Checks Through.
Let us now work through the central scenario of this chapter, step by step, examining the full equilibrium strategy for both players.
The setup is a nine-handed two-dollar, five-dollar no-limit hold 'em game with five hundred dollars effective stacks and no rake.
The first player to act before the flop — Under the Gun, or UTG — raises to fifteen dollars.
Only the big blind calls, so we go to the flop heads up.
The flop comes ace of spades, nine of hearts, six of spades.
Big Blind checks, UTG bets half the pot, and Big Blind calls.
The turn card is the four of diamonds, and both players check.
The river is the jack of diamonds.
This is the starting point for our analysis: we have reached the river after the pot was built on the flop and then both players checked the turn.
Big Blind can check, or bet thirty-three percent of the pot, seventy-five percent of the pot, or two hundred percent of the pot — a massive overbet.
If Big Blind checks, UTG can bet seventy-five percent or two hundred percent of the pot.
Either player is also permitted to raise to fifty percent of the pot when facing a bet.
Now let us work through all eight key questions about the equilibrium strategy in this situation.
Question one: which player has more equity when they reach the river in this way, and why?
The answer may be surprising at first glance — Big Blind has significantly more equity, about sixty-one percent, compared to UTG's thirty-nine percent.
This is a solver result, and while the exact numbers depend on the assumptions built into the simulation, the directional conclusion is robust: Big Blind is the favorite on the river.
Why would Big Blind, who called from out of position and then checked the turn without betting, be winning the equity battle on the river?
The answer lies in understanding how actions shape ranges.

On the flop, UTG had the range advantage — UTG raised preflop from early position, which means a strong, tight starting range full of high-card combinations, strong aces, and medium to large pocket pairs, all of which connect well with an ace-nine-six board.
So UTG bet the flop with a polarized range, meaning strong hands and bluffs, while Big Blind called with a condensed range of medium-strength holdings and some slowplayed monsters.
Big Blind was forced to fold many of their weakest hands on the flop, which cut away the low end of their range and moved their average equity upward.
On the turn, the same dynamic was expected to repeat: UTG would bet strong hands and some bluffs, while Big Blind would call with solid medium-strength hands.
But both players checked the turn.
When UTG checks the turn, that is highly informative.
UTG has been playing a polarized betting strategy, meaning their strong hands mostly go into the betting range.
A turn check suggests, strongly, that UTG does not have a very strong hand right now.
UTG's turn-checking range contains some showdown-value hands and many weak hands that simply declined to bluff again.
Essentially, UTG traded away most of their strong hands into the turn betting range from earlier streets — those hands are simply not available to them anymore on the river.
Big Blind, by contrast, has been calling with a condensed range and slowplaying some strong hands throughout.
Those slowplayed monsters — sets, top two pair, and strong top pairs — are still present in Big Blind's range on the river.
So by the time the jack of diamonds hits on the river, Big Blind is holding a range that contains real strong hands, while UTG is holding a range that is relatively weak.
That is not a mistake by UTG — it is a deliberate tradeoff, the cost of having executed a polarized betting strategy on earlier streets.
Question two: which player is better positioned to bet a polarized range on the river?
Both players will bet polarized ranges when they get the opportunity, but Big Blind's polarized betting range will be meaningfully wider.
The logic here flows directly from what we just established.
The player with the condensed range keeps checking to the player with the polarized range — that is the equilibrium dynamic on earlier streets.
But once the player with the polarized range stops betting — as UTG did when checking the turn — an opportunity opens up for the previously passive player to take the betting lead.
On the river, Big Blind is in exactly that position: UTG has checked the turn and weakened their range, and now Big Blind gets to bet a polarized range of their own.
For Big Blind to bet a polarized range, they need both strong hands and weak hands — a value region and a bluffing region.
The strong hands in Big Blind's polarized range come from three sources.

First, there are slowplayed premium hands: combinations like ace-nine, pocket nines, and pocket sixes that were strong enough to check-raise the flop but that Big Blind just called as a deliberate trap.

By slowplaying these on the flop and checking the turn, Big Blind has hidden their strength and now gets to bet them as apparent out-of-nowhere aggression on the river, which is very hard for UTG to play against.
Second, there are hands that were not strong enough to raise the flop but that improved on the turn or river, including hands like jack-nine suited and ace-four.
The jack-nine combination is particularly interesting here because the river jack gave it two pair, and the four of diamonds made ace-four two pair as well.

Not many hands improved in this way on this particular runout, but on other boards — say, if a spade had fallen completing the flush draw, or a ten had come in completing a straight — there would have been many more such improved hands available.
Third, there are hands that were not strong enough to play a huge pot but that are strong enough to bet for thin value on the river: ace-ten is the key example here.

Ace-ten is not a hand you want to check-raise the flop with, because check-raising commits you to a trajectory where you put four bets into the pot, and ace-ten with a bad kicker is not a hand you want to build a four-bet pot with against UTG's strong preflop range.
But calling the flop and then betting the river for two bets total — that is a perfectly fine plan for ace-ten.
On the bluffing side, Big Blind has some candidates, though they are fewer than you might expect, because Big Blind had to have something to call the flop with.
Big Blind's truly unpaired hands are limited: king-queen of spades, and hands like ten-eight and eight-seven that missed straight draws.
Those hands always bluff when Big Blind bets, because they have no showdown value.
But they do not fill enough of a bluffing range on their own, so Big Blind also uses their weakest pairs — six-five suited and seven-six suited — as bluffs for certain bet sizes.
Question three: what are the weakest hands Big Blind bets for value, and what bluffs balance those bets?
This is where bet sizing becomes crucial, because the answer changes dramatically depending on how large Big Blind bets.
At the smallest bet size — thirty-three percent of the pot, which is sometimes called a blocking bet — Big Blind can bet as thin as ace-ten for value.

The reason is that a small bet forces UTG to call with a very wide range in order to prevent Big Blind from bluffing profitably, and when UTG must call with many weak hands, Big Blind can profit by betting weaker value hands.
At seventy-five percent of the pot, the thinnest value bet is ace-king.
At twice the pot — the massive overbet — Big Blind cannot even bet jack-nine for value; the threshold rises to ace-jack or better.
The bluffs used to balance these value ranges are always drawn from the weakest hands in Big Blind's range: the missed straight draws, the spade combos, and the weakest pairs, as described above.
Question four: what are the worst hands Big Blind calls with after checking, when UTG bets?
Against a seventy-five percent pot bet, Big Blind never folds when they hold an ace or a jack — those hands always call.
Big Blind folds most combinations of pocket nines, though they are roughly indifferent with those hands.
Against a two hundred percent pot overbet, Big Blind's calling range narrows sharply, because now UTG is demanding that Big Blind risk a very large amount to stay in.
Jack-nine and pocket nines always call the overbet.
Ace-jack and pocket jacks are split between calling and raising — they are strong enough to play for stacks, and raising is sometimes the right move.
Hands that contain just a pair of aces become indifferent between calling and folding, regardless of what kicker they hold alongside the ace.

That last point is particularly revealing about UTG's strategy: the fact that Big Blind's kicker does not matter tells us that UTG should not include just a single pair in their overbet value range, because if UTG's overbet value range contained one-pair hands, then the kicker would matter a great deal — an ace with a king would beat an ace with a five.
Since the kicker does not matter, we can infer that UTG's two-hundred-percent value range must contain two pair or better, not mere one-pair hands.
Now here is one of the most instructive blocker effects in the entire scenario: Big Blind never folds when they have a jack in their hand, regardless of what UTG bets.
Think carefully about why.
The jack is a blocker to the hands UTG is most likely to be betting for value here: ace-jack and pocket jacks.
If Big Blind holds a jack, they have removed one of those combinations from UTG's possible holdings.
The risk of being up against a strong hand is meaningfully lower when Big Blind holds a jack.
So instead of folding, Big Blind always does something with a jack — sometimes they call, and sometimes they raise as a bluff, but they never simply give up.
Question five: what hands should Big Blind check-raise for value, and what bluffs balance those check-raises?
Big Blind check-raises ace-jack and pocket jacks for value almost exclusively, regardless of UTG's bet size.
Pocket nines are included as a check-raise at the smaller bet size but not the overbet.
This creates a fascinating strategic wrinkle: against the overbet, Big Blind raises the weaker hand — ace-jack — but calls with the stronger hand — pocket nines.
In our toy games, we never encountered a situation where a player raised with a weaker holding and called with a stronger one.
The explanation is all about blockers.
When Big Blind check-raises ace-jack, they have a jack in hand.
That jack blocks UTG from holding pocket jacks or ace-jack themselves, which are the main hands that would crush Big Blind's check-raise.
The risk of running into a monster is reduced.
When Big Blind has pocket nines, they hold no jack, so the risk of UTG having pocket jacks or ace-jack is higher, making a check-raise riskier than it might initially appear.

Against the overbet in particular, where Big Blind must commit a very large amount to raise, the danger of running into a jack-high hand is severe enough that Big Blind prefers to call with pocket nines rather than risk a check-raise.
The blocker is genuinely the deciding factor.
For bluffing check-raises, Big Blind always uses hands that contain a jack.
There are two complementary reasons for this.
First and most obviously, having a jack in hand blocks the strong jack-containing hands UTG might hold, reducing the probability of getting called by a monster.
Second, and more subtly, a hand with a jack has some showdown value — there is a real chance it wins at showdown if checked down.
That parallels the lesson from the Ace-to-Five game, where Opal check-raised tens, nines, eights, and sevens as bluffs but not sixes and fives.
The hands that are so weak they have no showdown value prefer to just bet outright rather than check — they need to generate fold equity immediately.

The hands that have a little bit of showdown value can afford to check first, and if UTG bets, that is new information suggesting that perhaps Big Blind's marginal jack is not good enough after all, which gives Big Blind the incentive to sometimes turn it into a bluff by raising.
Question six: what does UTG's raising range look like when facing Big Blind's bet?
UTG raises more liberally against smaller bets, because a small bet implies a wider range, and wider ranges contain more weak hands worth isolating.
Against a thirty-three percent pot bet, UTG is indifferent between calling and raising with ace-king.
Against a seventy-five percent pot bet, UTG needs ace-jack to raise for value.
Against the two hundred percent overbet, UTG also raises ace-jack — but here, raising ace-jack functions as a bluff, not value.
The logic is that Big Blind sometimes folds pocket sixes, pocket nines, and even ace-jack to this raise, making it profitable even though UTG's hand is not that strong.
UTG's bluff raises also shift based on bet size, and blockers are the deciding factor.

Against the small bet, it is most valuable to block ace-king and ace-queen, because those are the thin-value hands Big Blind is betting, so UTG's bluffing hands include king-queen, king-jack, and even pocket kings — pocket kings has no showdown value here because if it does not raise, it will fold, so it might as well try to fold out Big Blind's better holdings.

Against larger bets, UTG wants to block jacks, because jacks dominate the strong hands Big Blind would continue with, so UTG's bluffs shift to queen-jack, jack-ten, and even ace-jack when facing the overbet.
Question seven: what are the weakest hands UTG calls with when facing Big Blind's bet?
Against the thirty-three percent pot bet, UTG is indifferent to calling with pocket tens and pocket sevens.
Against seventy-five percent, UTG is indifferent with jack-ten, ace-five, and ace-ten.
Against the two hundred percent overbet, UTG is indifferent with ace-king, ace-queen, queen-jack, and jack-ten.
Notice the direct relationship here: as the bet grows larger, UTG must call with stronger hands and can let go of weaker ones.
And as UTG calls with stronger hands, Big Blind can bet for value with stronger hands only — which is exactly the pattern we observed in Question three.
The two constraints are linked: the calling threshold and the value-betting threshold move in lockstep, because they must — that is what equilibrium demands.
Question eight: what are the weakest hands UTG bets for value when Big Blind checks, and what bluffs balance those bets?
At seventy-five percent of the pot, UTG can bet for value down to ace-queen.
Notice that this is a weaker hand than Big Blind can bet for the same size from out of position.

This is exactly the positional advantage we saw in the Ace-to-Five game: the player in position can make thinner value bets than the player out of position, because being in position is safer — you get to see your opponent's action before committing, and if they show aggression, you can get away from a marginal hand.
At two hundred percent of the pot, UTG needs ace-jack or better for a value bet.

For bluffs, UTG is in an interesting spot: against Big Blind's checking range, UTG has a lot of showdown value with medium-strength hands, which means those medium hands are better served by checking and trying to win at showdown rather than betting.
UTG draws their bluffs from their very weakest hands: unpaired holdings like queen-ten, king-ten, and king-queen, as well as their lowest pocket pairs.
These are hands with little to no chance of winning a showdown, so turning them into bluffs is the natural play.

The entire eight-question analysis reveals a beautifully interlocked system where every decision by one player constrains and is constrained by the decisions of the other, and where blockers serve as the final, precise tiebreaker that determines which specific hand goes into which specific role.

sections.title: Real World Applications
lines: Chapter eight, section four. Real world applications.
The detailed scenario we just worked through is not just an academic exercise — it contains lessons that translate directly into how you should think about real decisions at the poker table.
Let us work through the most important of those lessons systematically.
The first real-world application concerns bet sizing.
There is a common misconception among poker players that betting different amounts with different types of hands is inherently exploitable.
The thinking goes: if you bet small with bluffs and large with strong hands, your opponent can just fold whenever you bet big and call whenever you bet small.
That is absolutely true if you use sizes predictably in a way that telegraphs your hand strength.
But the claim that different bet sizes are exploitable is not true in general — it is only true when those sizes are used without balancing.

A fully worked-out equilibrium solution for most no-limit hold 'em river situations actually involves multiple different bet sizes, each one carefully balanced between value hands and bluffs, such that an opponent cannot profitably exploit any of them in isolation.

In the scenario we just analyzed, Big Blind bet thirty-three percent, seventy-five percent, and two hundred percent of the pot, each with its own distinct range of value hands and a corresponding set of bluffs.
None of those sizing choices alone revealed the strength of the hand.
Before concluding that a particular play is exploitable, always ask yourself: how exactly could my opponent exploit this?
Because the answer is sometimes, 'They can't, if I adjust my ranges correctly.'
You may also find that you are comfortable being theoretically exploitable in some minor way, because you do not expect your real-life opponents to take advantage of it.

In the case of bet sizing, the general principle is that larger bets call for more polarized ranges: you need a stronger hand to bet big for value, but the bluffs that accompany those value bets can be more aggressive as well.

Smaller bets permit thinner value bets — because your opponent must call with more weak hands to prevent your bluffs from being profitable, and those calls from weak hands are exactly what you want when you have a marginal value hand.

The key risk with small bets is that if you use them exclusively with thin value hands and bluffs, and never with your strongest hands, then a smart opponent can recognize that your small-bet range has no monsters in it and start check-raising you frequently for thin value.
The equilibrium response to that threat is to occasionally include your very strongest hands in the small-bet range, specifically to make your opponent indifferent to check-raising.
If they check-raise too liberally, they will run into your disguised monsters; if they check-raise too rarely, your thin value bets run unchallenged.
The balancing act between those two outcomes is what keeps the equilibrium stable.
The second major real-world application concerns river bluffing.
Most players think about bluffing in exploitative terms: I believe my opponent has a weak hand that will fold to a bet, therefore I bluff.
That is a perfectly valid reason to bluff, and when you genuinely have that read, it is a great one.
It is also true that bluffs work better when they represent a coherent story — when they credibly tell a narrative of a strong hand, given the board texture and your preflop and postflop actions.
But neither of those conditions is actually necessary or sufficient for bluffing to be the right play.
A bluff does not need to tell a story in order to be profitable — it needs the math to work out.

And you do not need a specific read on an opponent in order to bluff — game theory tells you that bluffing is a mandatory component of equilibrium strategy in virtually every situation where you are also value betting.

If you are in the habit of not bluffing whenever you lack a specific read on your opponent — or when you are playing against someone new, or early in a tournament, or when you are ahead in the session — then you are playing exploitably.

An observant opponent who recognizes that you only bluff in specific circumstances will simply never fold their medium-strength hands when you bet, because they know your betting range is value-heavy.
The question to ask on the river is not 'Is this a good spot to bluff?' but rather 'Which hands should I be bluffing with in this spot?'
That shift in framing is enormously important.
The first question invites you to assess whether your current hand is a good bluffing candidate in isolation, and it often leads to checking because no individual hand feels obviously like a bluff.
The second question acknowledges that bluffing is already required and asks you to make an intelligent choice about which hand to do it with.

A practical approach is to think about your bluffing candidates as a pool — a set of hands that are unlikely to win at showdown and therefore need to generate fold equity to have positive expected value.
The size of your value betting range determines how many bluffs you need: the more value hands you have, the more bluff combinations you need to maintain the correct ratio.

By thinking in terms of filling a bluff pool rather than making individual bluffing decisions, you force yourself to be active and intentional, instead of bluffing randomly when you feel like it or never bluffing in spots where bluffs are hard to find.
When you are about to check and give up on a hand, take a moment to ask yourself: what other weak hands would I bluff in this spot?

If you genuinely cannot identify any hand that you would bluff with, that is actually a signal that you should reconsider bluffing with your current hand — you may be the obvious bluffing candidate and simply not realize it.
Now, the two specific factors that make a hand a particularly good bluffing candidate on the river are, first, a lack of showdown value, and second, blockers.
We have discussed both, but it is worth understanding how they interact.
In many situations, you will find that you have more weak hands than you need for bluffing — more hopeless holdings than the ratio demands.
In those cases, you cannot bluff all of them, and blockers serve as the tiebreaker: you choose the weak hands whose hole cards reduce the probability of your opponent having a strong calling hand.
As pot sizes grow and ranges become narrower, the blocker effect becomes more pronounced.
In the scenario we analyzed, when the pot was large and UTG could only call with a small number of very strong hands, those strong calling hands disproportionately contained jacks.

That meant Big Blind's bluffs almost always included a jack — blocking that jack dramatically reduced the probability of getting called, shifting the bluff from approximately break-even to genuinely positive expected value.

In smaller pots, where your opponent must call with a wider range that includes many different hand types, any single blocker has a smaller effect because the opponent's calling range is so diverse that no single card dominates it.
But in big pots, the calling range is tight and concentrated, and blocking even one card from that calling range can have an outsized impact.
There is also an interaction between blockers and showdown value that is worth appreciating.

Hands with absolutely no showdown value — hands that lose at showdown one hundred percent of the time — have a clear preference for betting rather than checking, because checking means automatically losing.
Those hands should be bluffing, not checking.

Hands with a small amount of showdown value — hands that might win at showdown occasionally — have a reason to consider checking, because there is a chance they pick up the pot without putting in extra chips.
But if an opponent bets into them, that bet is new information: it suggests the opponent has something, which makes the marginal hand's showdown value smaller than it appeared.
At that point, it can become correct to turn the marginal hand into a bluff by raising, because the hand has just learned that its showdown value has been diminished.

This is exactly the dynamic behind Big Blind's check-raise bluffs in the scenario, all of which contained a jack — the jack gave them some showdown value, and the opponent's bet revealed that even the jack might not be good enough, so raising became the correct play for some frequency.
The overarching lesson for real-world play is this: never approach the river passively without a clear strategic purpose.
Before you check and give up, identify your bluffing range explicitly, even if you are not going to bluff this particular time.
Before you bet, know which hands are your value hands and construct a bluffing range that balances them.
When choosing your bluffs, think deliberately about blockers rather than just grabbing the first weak hand you see.

And when you are deciding between bet sizes, understand that different sizes require different ranges, and make sure each range is properly balanced so that no single sizing reveals your hand strength to a thoughtful opponent.

These habits of mind — thinking in ranges, constructing bluffing pools deliberately, using blockers as tiebreakers, and balancing bet sizes — are what separate a rigorous, game-theory-informed poker strategy from the loose intuitions that most players operate on.
The scenario in this chapter is complex, but every element of it traces back to principles we established in the simpler toy games.

The math is more involved, the range construction is more detailed, and the blocker effects are more nuanced, but the underlying logic is the same: equilibrium strategy requires each player to make their opponent indifferent between their available options, and every decision — value bet, bluff, call, fold, raise — is in service of that overarching goal.
Chapter eight has brought all of those pieces together, and the analytical approach you have developed here will serve you in every real poker situation you encounter going forward.

sections.title: Summary of Exploits
lines: Wrap-up, part one. Summary of exploits.

One of the most powerful gifts that game theory gives you is an intuition for when your opponents are making mistakes — and equally important, a concrete framework for how to profit from those mistakes.
Understanding why an equilibrium strategy is constructed the way it is allows you to spot the seams in a flawed opponent's approach and pry them open.
Some exploitative adjustments are obvious on their face: if someone is bluffing every time they bet, you call them down.
But many exploits are more subtle, requiring adjustments earlier in the hand or higher up in the game tree — and those are the ones worth studying carefully.
An important framing idea before getting into specifics: think of an equilibrium strategy as a perfectly balanced scale.

Many decisions at equilibrium are genuinely close — the kind where both options earn roughly the same expected value — and any shift your opponent makes away from equilibrium tips that scale, sometimes dramatically, creating opportunities for you.

You can afford to make small exploitative adjustments liberally, even when you have only modest confidence in your read; converting a mixed strategy into a pure one in response to a minor opponent error is low-risk.
Larger adjustments — like calling with hands that would be pure folds at equilibrium, or folding hands that would be pure calls — require stronger evidence of a meaningful deviation.
With that framing in place, let's go through the major opponent types and the appropriate counters for each.
The first exploit concerns the opponent who calls too many weak hands.
This is by far the most common leak in poker — calling is more entertaining than folding and less mentally demanding than raising, so the average player does it far too often.
At equilibrium, one of the reasons you bluff is to make your opponent indifferent between calling and folding with their bluff-catchers.
If your opponent is going to call regardless, that indifference breaks down, and bluffing hands that were only marginally profitable at equilibrium are no longer worth firing.
That does not mean you stop bluffing entirely — some hands are so well-suited to bluffing that they remain profitable even against a calling station.
Strong draws on early streets fall into this category, because they have genuine equity and benefit from fold equity on top of it.

On the river, your very weakest holdings — hands with almost no chance of winning a showdown — may also justify a bet, because a loose opponent often arrives at the river with missed draws and other trash that your bet can push out.
The flip side is that you should be much more aggressive about thin value betting.

At equilibrium, some hands are right on the borderline between value betting and checking; against a loose caller, those hands almost always want to bet, because they get called by a wider range of worse hands.

Strong hands that equilibrium might mix between betting and check-raising should lean toward pure bets, since you want to keep a loose caller's chips in the pot rather than scaring them off with a raise.
Even hands below the normal value-bet threshold can sometimes turn a profit by betting into a player who calls too liberally.
One mistake to avoid: don't get lazy just because you can profit by waiting for monster hands against a loose player — that strategy leaves a lot of money on the table.
The second exploit addresses the opponent who folds too often — the so-called nit.
Against this player type, your strong hands are less valuable at showdown because you simply won't get paid off as often, so you need to shift resources toward bluffing to make up the difference.
Specifically, hands that are right on the value-bet/check borderline at equilibrium should become pure checks, because a nit is likely to fold worse hands that would have called at equilibrium.
