On the other side of the coin, hands that sit on the bluff/check borderline at equilibrium become profitable pure bluffs, because a player who folds too often is giving you a clear green light to steal.
You might think the answer against a nit is also to trap with your best hands, but there is a complication: opponents who dislike calling typically also dislike betting.
If your nit opponent rarely bets, trapping just results in a check-back, so you are usually better off betting your strong hands than hoping they will bet into you.
The real edge against tight opponents comes from identifying board textures where they cannot logically hold the top of their range, and then representing those strong holdings yourself.
A player with a narrow, predictable range is much easier to put on a hand, and when you determine they can't have the nuts, you can apply pressure with impunity.
The third exploit is for when your opponent does not bluff enough.

The most direct adjustment is to fold all of your pure bluff-catchers — hands that at equilibrium are indifferent between calling and folding — because those hands only break even when your opponent bluffs at the correct frequency, and they become losers when bluffs are rare.

In extreme cases, you extend this to folding hands that would normally be pure calls at equilibrium, moving down through your calling range until the remaining hands beat enough of your opponent's actual betting range to justify a call.
A practical trick to keep this grounded: when an under-bluffer bets, ask yourself whether they would bet this confidently with any hand weaker than yours.
If the honest answer is no, you should fold regardless of how strong your hand feels in absolute terms — there is no rule in poker that a strong hand must call.
On the offensive side, you can value bet very thinly against an under-bluffer, because the main risk of a thin value bet is getting raised as a bluff, and that risk is minimal here.
All hands that equilibrium mixes between bluffing and checking become profitable pure bluffs against this opponent, because every time they encounter a bet they tend to fold.
What you should avoid against an under-bluffer is the bluff-raise — if they are willing to bet, that signals real confidence in their hand, and a raise is unlikely to move them off it.
The fourth exploit deals with the opponent who bluffs too much.
At equilibrium, bluffing at the right frequency keeps your opponent indifferent about calling with their marginal holdings.
When your opponent over-bluffs, that indifference dissolves in your favor: hands you would have mixed between calling and folding at equilibrium now become straightforward calls.
In severe cases, you can even call with some hands that would have been pure folds at equilibrium.

Beyond the direct calling adjustment, an over-bluffing opponent is also vulnerable to raises, because a range that is heavy with bluffs cannot comfortably defend against a raise — the bluffs usually have to fold.
How you adjust your raises depends on how your opponent will respond.

If you expect them to fold to raises frequently — meaning their bluffs simply give up — then you should raise more liberally as a bluff, using hands that were indifferent between raising and folding at equilibrium, and in extreme cases even hands that would have been pure folds.
Those bluff-raises should be small, ideally close to the minimum, because the profit comes from making the bluff fold, not from extracting value when called.
Hands that equilibrium mixes between calling and bluff-raising should become pure calls in this situation, since the raise is already profitable enough without mixing.

If you expect the opponent to respond to raises by widening their calling range instead of folding, then any hand that equilibrium mixes between calling and raising for value should become a pure raise, capturing more value from that wider calling range.
If you genuinely cannot predict how this opponent will respond to a raise, the safest approach is to widen both your value-raising range and your bluff-raising range simultaneously.
Even if one of those adjustments turns out to be slightly wrong, the other will more than compensate.

The guiding principle here is that the right way to attack a range loaded with marginal hands is with a polarized range — strong hands and bluffs — and the more marginal hands your opponent holds, the wider that polarized range should be.
The fifth exploit concerns the opponent who traps excessively.
In an equilibrium strategy, very strong hands sometimes check on early streets to trap bluffs and induce thin value bets.
If your opponent does this far more often than equilibrium calls for, their checking range becomes dangerously weighted toward strong holdings.
In response, you should do less bluffing and less thin value betting when they check, because more of those checks are slow-plays.
The silver lining is that you save money with your marginal hands — you check back spots where you might otherwise have called a bet, preserving your stack.

When a trappy opponent actually does bet, their betting range is lighter — stripped of many of the strong hands that they have checked — so you can raise for thin value more freely, and you may find more profitable bluffing opportunities as well, depending on how well they defend that lighter betting range.
This situation mirrors several exploits from the opponent who bluffs too much, because the mechanics are similar.
On early streets specifically, be careful about betting draws when you are in position against a trappy player.
Draws are usually excellent bluff candidates, but they hate running into a check-raise, which a trappy opponent deploys much more frequently than expected.
When you are in position, you can simply check back and take a free card.

When you are out of position, checking for a free card is not available, and a trappy player may also be less inclined to check-raise an out-of-position bettor, so you can still generally bet your strong draws from that seat.
The sixth exploit targets the opponent who does not trap enough.
Some players simply cannot sit on a strong hand — they are too worried about losing value or getting outdrawn, so they bet every time they have something good.
Against these opponents, their check becomes a highly reliable signal: if they check, they almost certainly do not have a strong hand.
After that check, you can bet for thin value or as a large bluff with a lot of confidence, mimicking the role of the clairvoyant player in the polarized range toy games covered earlier in the book.

You may be able to exploit their calling and folding tendencies on top of this, but even if their tendencies are close to equilibrium in those dimensions, overbetting a balanced polarized range after their check will earn a healthy profit.
The counterpart is that when this opponent does bet, their range is heavily weighted toward strong hands.
You should almost never raise them — not for value and not as a bluff — because there is too high a probability they hold the nuts.
Hands that are indifferent between calling and folding at equilibrium may actually prefer to fold, because their betting range is thin on bluffs.
There is one important nuance: it is possible, at least in theory, for an opponent to compensate for not trapping by adding extra bluffs to their betting range, keeping things balanced that way.

But this is unlikely in practice, and if you are wrong about it — if they actually are bluffing at an appropriate rate — you lose very little by folding your marginal bluff-catchers, since those hands were only barely profitable calls in the first place.
Taken together, these six exploit clusters give you a concrete, principled toolkit for adjusting to nearly any opponent tendency you encounter at the table.

The thread running through all of them is the same: understand what equilibrium looks like, identify where your opponent is deviating from it, and shift your own strategy in the direction that punishes that specific deviation.

sections.title: Conclusion
lines: Wrap-up, part two. Conclusion.
If this entire journey through game theory and optimal poker strategy had to be reduced to a single takeaway, it would be this: learn to think in a different way.
Before you reach for an assumption about how your specific opponent is likely to play, pause and ask what an equilibrium strategy would look like in that spot.

That step alone changes everything, because once you have a baseline — a picture of what balanced, unexploitable play requires — you can measure your opponent's behavior against it and identify exactly where they are deviating.
That is far more powerful than reacting to surface impressions or gut feelings alone.
Even when your goal is explicitly to play exploitatively, which it often should be, the equilibrium framework gives you a map.
It tells you the size of your opponent's error, the direction in which to adjust, and roughly how far to push that adjustment given the strength of your read.
Without that reference point, exploitation is guesswork.
With it, exploitation becomes deliberate and calibrated.
The study of game theory in poker is not a destination — it is an ongoing journey, and an honest one requires accepting that there will always be more to learn.
Even the process of writing this book surfaced new insights, and the learning did not stop once the book was finished.
You can find that fact discouraging, or you can find it energizing.

When you look at a complex solver output full of precisely mixed strategies — bet this size forty percent of the time, check sixty percent of the time — the reaction that serves you best is not confusion or frustration, but curiosity.
Ask not what the numbers are, but why they are what they are.
What is the mix accomplishing strategically?
What two opponent responses is it making equally attractive, and which of those responses is your particular opponent more likely to choose?
The moment you answer those questions, you no longer need the mixed strategy — you can replace it with a pure exploitative strategy tuned precisely to this opponent's tendencies.
That is the human element that numbers alone cannot supply.
Sizing up an opponent, finding the crack in their approach, and designing a targeted attack around it — that has always been the beauty at the heart of poker.
Game theory and solvers do not eliminate that process; they sharpen it, giving you more precise language and better tools for the same competition that has always defined the game.
They are the newest weapon in an arms race that will keep evolving, and the players who engage seriously with the theory will be better equipped at every stage of that race.

This companion podcast is an original explainer of the ideas in Andrew Brokos's book Play Optimal Poker, and listeners who want the full treatment — including all the diagrams, tables, and worked examples — should read the book itself.
